I get on flights, and I see people stand up, take my luggage to my car, they have been amazing. I haven't encountered that sort of pursuit and I have been very touched and humbled by the way they have treated me.
My husband's friends call me regularly to see how I am doing- I mean a lot of widows complain that that they have issues with people proposing to them. But in my own case, I must say that I have been lucky to have wonderful support system based on respect and a sense of protection. If that is a function of the respect they had for my husband, I don't know.
When I travel abroad, I also meet Nigerian men who are respectful. I also believe that it also depends on the woman's attitude-sometimes we lay blame at the doorstep of the men-- but the fact is that if you are engaged in your work, if you are a woman who have a sense of purpose, regardless of the fact that you operate in a terrain that is dominated by men, once you can hold your own, it will be difficult to fall into that quagmire where you feel you are being propositioned or your gender is playing a derogatory role.
Once you are not making excuses for bad performance, or once you are not looking for a man to cover for you, for your inadequacies, once you are able to let you work speak for you, it's a lot easier to survive and live a life of dignity, and once you don't present yourself as a weak and defenseless woman- one to be pitied and really cuddled by a man just by a virtue of being of a weaker sex - then it's much easier to live a life that is not being truncated by those pressures.
Since you husband passed on, there have been stories about your in-laws concerning the family estate. Some even went to the extent of saying you were not legally married to Ojukwu. How have you been able to cope with these issues?
I came into a deeply divided family- a lot of my husband's relatives, I never saw physically before he died. Ojukwu Transport Company issues have been in court, they still have not resolved the issues concerning their father's wealth, and this was an issue that started in 1966. So they have been in and out of court since then. And I came into their family knowing that, literally every month, one brother had one case or the other. So those issues are not strange- they are still to be determined- the issues of Sir Louis Ojukwu's estate.
What we are seeing now is a form of transferred aggression- bitterness, resentment vitriol-towards a brother that they are exhibiting which, of course, in his lifetime, they were not able to succeed. What they had expected was a docile widow who would quietly retreat into the shadow and let them have their way. I am very comfortable if not for anything, for being the daughter of Chief C.C.Onoh, and my father took care of me; so the issue is not just about their family.
But my husband was also a very serious person, so he anticipated all what is happening now by making sure that most of my inheritance is bequeathed to me by the Deed of Gift. So, prior to his death, most of those gifts had already been registered under my name. So I am so grateful that he was able to legally take care of that because, quite honestly, I didn't expect this level of vitriol. He ensured also that any interest outside of the country was also taken care of- because he had a Will outside of the country as well, and I didn't have problem with the probate on such a Will.
But I will say that, at the end of the day, it's not a question of acquisition. All I am saying is that I am the only wife that he wedded, first and foremost, at the Kaduna Registry as witnessed by his friend, the late Clement Akpamgbo, and we had a small reception after the event at Apo Quarters, Abuja. After the registry marriage, we went through the Catholic marriage at Our Lady Queen of Nigeria Catholic Church, Abuja, and it was officiated by the then Archbishop Onaiyekan- then the Archbishop of Abuja Dioceses.
We finally went through the full traditional marriage in my father's compound that literally had all the traditional titled chiefs, paramount rulers of the community were in attendance, alongside the five governors of the South-east states at that time. The Abuja wedding, for instance, was almost a state wedding. So the evidence is there for all to see, and, when you are faced with that situation, and you have been properly married, and you have offsprings from that union, but purely because they want to cheat your offspring of their inheritance, in addition to other things, by virtue of their being the youngest, any mother would do all she can to protect them.
My husband was quite specific in wanting to protect their interests; that was why he ensured that I would be in the Board. When the Will was read, and they realized that my husband had protected my interests and those of his children, they came up with all kinds of subterfuge. For instance, they said they don't allow women to be on the Board, or that for one to be on the Board, you had to be an Ojukwu. Any right thinking person will see that they had no provision for the children, so I was left with no option than to pursue legal means in order to ensure that my children were not cheated out of what rightly belongs to them.
In doing so, I was aware that a lot of mud and ridiculous assertions would be thrown my way, and they have not disappointed. They have hurled all sorts of vitriol and outlandish claims about me- the most laughable has been what they claim that I was not legally married to my husband. I mean, this is a marriage that took place in 1994 and not 1884; so the evidence: the videos, media reports are there for everyone to see.
I mean these people who are making these outlandish assertions were also present, not just at the wedding, but at the wine carriage, wearing uniforms, so that was just laughable. People have called me to say they have descended to a ridiculous level. But the good thing about all these is that battles are not won or lost on the pages of newspaper, the courts will determine the outcome. But as I said earlier, my interest in all this is to ensure that my children are not cheated out of what rightly belongs to them- I have no personal interest in all of it, because no one can take from me what rightly belongs to me, as my husband took time to ensure that my personal interest was well taken care of.

Original_Search_Title: Women Offer Themselves to Me, but I Can't Remarry - Justice Akanbi
Headline: Nigeria: Women Offer Themselves to Me, but I Can't Remarry - Justice Akanbi
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Former president of the Court of Appeal and pioneer chairman of the Independent Corrupt Practices and other related offences Commission (ICPC), Justice Mustapha Akanbi, described as Mr. Anti-Corruption for his hard stance against graft, veered off his usual topics and opened up about losing his wife Hajiya Munfatu Aduke, whose one year remembrance was marked last week. The erudite jurist, who is in his 80s, shared fond memories and the sense of loss he feels over her demise. Excerpts:
It's one year now. How has life been without your wife?
Before answering your question, let me say what happened when I celebrated my 80th birthday. My wife was asked by journalists: 'If she had to come back to this world, who would see marry,' she said she would marry me. That meant that I have given her satisfaction in life and if you ask me the same question, if I die and I were to marry again, she would be the same woman that I would marry. We were married for 48 years before her death and somebody had never settled any dispute for us. She was a role model of a wife; she had been a worthy mother to our children and a decent wife. So when you lose such a character, a woman of great virtue, of decency, naturally you are bound to feel pain and loss.
It is interesting to recall when we were doing the Fidau, an elderly man in Ilorin, I don't want to mention his name called me aside. I thought he wanted to say something important, but he was advising me on the sort of wife I could get after the demise of my wife. Honestly, because of the respect I have for him, I couldn't insult him. I thought that was too much. But what is interesting is that on my 80th birthday, there is a lady who came to me and said she wanted me to marry her mother who has lost her husband long time ago. This was not private; there were some friends of mine there. After the death of my wife, one woman started bringing things to me, and I had to stop her. I even had a woman of high standing who came here and said: 'My lord, if you would marry me, I am available.'
The point which I want to make is this, I can't find another one like my late wife. She was an ideal Muslim wife. She was obedient. Where I go is where she goes. If I say 'No, don't go there,' she would not. When we got married, I made her understand that I wanted to build a scholarly and Islamic home. She was a dedicated Muslim and she would wake up in the late hours of the night to pray when sometimes I would be asleep. From Magrib to Isha'i, she would be reading the Quran. She had tremendous respect for me. When you lose that kind of woman, your life can never be the same again. I have faith in Allah and as far as I am concerned, everything He has done is for good.
Even when she was dying, she was still concerned about me. In Mecca, she would say 'I hope, you are not taking salt'. Even here, when I say I am not feeling fine, by the time I would realise it, either Dr. Khatibi or Dr. Olanrewaju would have been here, without me knowing how as she would have already phoned them. There is nobody to do that for me again, that is quite clear. The children cannot be substitutes for the wife. No relation can be. Everybody is doing his best to make me happy, wives of friends, like the wife of Justice Salami my friend, the Adetunjis who would come all the way from Ibadan to come and pound yam for me and many others. I am a very lucky man, people value me, people appreciate me but there is no substitute for my wife.
Because of my Spartan life, which she enjoyed too, it is not easy for people to see me weeping, crying and gnashing teeth. The only thing is to project the ideals and values which both of us cherished when she was alive and that's what I set myself to do.
What are your schedules like now?
Very hectic. I should have published my autobiography by now. On her sick bed in Saudi Arabia, my late wife would be asking 'when are we going to launch your autobiography?' So after her death, I wrote a special chapter on her. When the book is read by anybody, her worth would be clear. I spent more time also to continue what we have been doing together and with each child, I know the capacity and the capability of any of my children. I didn't spend time bemoaning her death. I pray for her every day, even this morning, for two solid hours. Her death has not stopped me from doing all the humanitarian things we have been doing.
When you look at Nigeria, people think that apart from money, nothing is important but the future belongs to people with knowledge. Forget about what is happening. Today, I have written a book about my life. Some groups have made collections and have written about me, they have made collection of essays from Obas, from governors, from politicians, from scholars, from professionals. I just saw a copy of the book but they won't release it to me and I was pleasantly surprised about a few things that I was able to read. Now, if they think that my wife's life is worthy of emulation, let them emulate her.
How supportive have your friends been?
My friends know me very well, like Alhaji Adetunji from Ibadan, who is a very tough character, not a softie. But I think apart from Almighty Allah and Prophet Mohammed, I am the person Alhaji Adetunji believes in. He knows that when he does something wrong, I will tell him. And my advice to people is that they should take a leaf from the way we are living.
One of your sons delivered his inaugural lecture as a professor as part of the programme to mark your late wife's one year remembrance. How did it feel?
Great. He specially requested that he would want to tie his inaugural address with this ceremony. The only thing I felt when he was delivering the lecture was I wished my wife was alive to be part of this but since I know I would go one day and I believe we will meet and I would go and tell her: 'This is what your son did.' But I am also grateful to the Almighty Allah, he could have taken the two of us away before that day but he chose to leave me probably because he wanted me to be in charge of things Hajia herself would love to see it going on well. I feel happy and I thank God.
Can you recall how your relationship started and blossomed into marriage?
I married like a blind man. Before I left for England, there were a lot of offers for me to marry because in those days they betrothed women to you but I was determined not to marry until I had a profession or qualified for one thing or the other. So I was the one who stayed long before marrying among my siblings. I married at the age of 32 after I qualified as a lawyer. I didn't go the Yoruba or Hausa way when they would just give you a wife and you would take it. So when I finished in Britain, I was in Lagos Law School, we used to do a three-month course. I had offers. Two people who knew my parents made them, but I turned them down. When the result came out and I had my certificate, I also decided that I would not marry without consulting my father. My father was in Accra. So I went to Accra after the call to bar. At that time, I had no people to follow me anywhere. So when I got to Accra, I now told my father, I have now been called to bar. I remember when I was coming from England, I passed through Accra with my friends, Justice Mohammed Uwais, Justice Shehu Mohammed, and so on and we were given a heroes' welcome. When I got to my father, he asked me about marriage and I told him I will discuss with him after the Call to Bar.
After the Call to Bar, I went to Accra and I met a girl. I discussed with her, then I went to Kumasi, where there were two ladies who had been mentioned to me, and they are all in my autobiography, and one of them ended up being my wife. After interviewing both of them, I left. In other words, I saw her only one day when I returned to Accra. When I got back, my father said: 'What have you decided?' I said 'When I got to Lagos, I will write you and tell you my decision.' So when I got to Lagos, the result of the bar exam had already come out and I passed. I now wrote to my dad that Munfatu Aduke Alao, daughter of Oba Alao, the chief of the Yoruba community in the whole of Kumasi and Ashanti region, is the one I wanted to marry.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Carpets Gavi for 'Incomplete, Unilateral' Audit Report On Vaccine Misuse
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Carpets Gavi for 'Incomplete, Unilateral' Audit Report On Vaccine Misuse
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Supervising Minister and Minister of State for Health, Dr. Khaliru Alhassan, has described the international audit report which indicted Nigeria for allegedly misusing millions of dollars meant for vaccination of millions of Nigerian children against killer diseases as "incomplete" and "unilateral".
Alhassan also alleged that Gavi, which commissioned the audit report, did not consider Nigeria's response before publishing the findings.
The audit report, as exclusive reported by The Guardian, had asked the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) to investigate the Ministry of Health and the National Primary Health Care Development Agency (NPHCDA) and officials found culpable.
More than two weeks after the story was broken by The Guardian and lifted by some media platforms and became an object of editorials, the Ministry has said Nigeria was not carried along, stressing that government was committed to ensuring transparency and effectiveness in managing donor support.
The statement signed by Director Press and Public Relations at the Ministry, Mrs Ayo Adesugba noted: "The media publications were based on an incomplete and unilateral report of the Cash Program Audit (CPA) commissioned by GAVI, which did not contain Nigeria's response, as is the custom in bilateral and multilateral relationships."
She noted: "The attention of the Supervising Minister and Minister of State for Health, Dr. Khaliru Alhassan, has been drawn to misleading and inaccurate reports published in some sections of the media on the purported "indictment" of Nigeria by the Global Alliance for Vaccines and Immunization (GAVI) for "alleged misuse of vaccine funds for program activities under the National Primary Health Care Development Agency NPHCDA."
"Additionally, the report implied that Global Alliance for Vaccines and Immunization, has withdrawn its cash program support to Nigeria. It must be stated clearly, that nothing could be further from the truth as GAVI and Nigeria, represented by the Federal Ministry of Health signed a letter of understanding on 17 October, 2014, aimed at further improving the working relationship between the two parties for support in systems strengthening, in order to enhance transparency and accountability.
"The Ministry is committed to ensuring that immunization activities are carried out effectively and transparently as buttressed by Nigeria's success and commendation by WHO, the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) and the International Board for the Monitoring of Polio (IMB), in curtailing the occurrence of the Wild Polio Virus in Nigeria."
Though Alhassan described the audit finding as "unilateral", a joint letter of understanding signed by immediate past Minister of Health, Professor Onyebuchi Chukwu, and Chief Executive Officer of Gavi, Dr Seth Berkley, agreed that truly there were systemic weaknesses in financial controls which may have resulted to the inappropriate use of funds.
The letter dated October 17, 2014 was entitled 'letter of Understanding on the Principles regarding the GAVI Cash Program Audit 2011-2013' and secured the commitment of Nigeria to refund any misuse identified in the final report. It was also agreed that an external auditor would be appointment to scrutize the books for use of funds released before 2011.
It was not immediately clear if the attention of the Supervising Minister had been brought to the letter signed by Chukwu in respect of the initial findings of the audit.
But the letter, which was signed based on preliminary findings before the release of the final audit, emphasized that the government of Nigeria and the management of Gavi were passionate about saving children's lives through immunisation and have been working in partnership to improve routine immunisation and introduce new vaccines since 2000.
A copy of letter obtained by The Guardian read: "The GoN (government of Nigeria) and Gavi have together carefully reviewed the findings of GAVl's cash program audit (CPA), which began in November 2013 and covered financial grants disbursed in the period 2011 to 2013. Select areas of support were subject to detailed examination.
"The CPA had the following objectives: to validate that expenditures made under the Gavi grants were in accordance with the mutually-agreed commitments, and were properly documented; to gain assurance that the systems) processes and controls at the executing entities; the Federal Ministry of Health, NPHCDA, and states were adequate to ensure that Gavi funds could be channelled securely, while identifying opportunities to strengthen these institutions."
The letter noted: "This statement was agreed upon before the completion of the audit. In the meantime, the Government of Nigeria and Gavi have agreed on the following: there have been systemic weaknesses in financial controls which may have led to the inappropriate use of funds. An external fun-scale audit will be undertaken to examine pre-2011 expenditures not covered in the scope of this CPA as when as to further validate certain items raised by the Ministry of Health in the current CPA. The GoN agrees on the principle to reimburse Gavi for any amounts identified as inappropriately used at the conclusion of the current CPA. The Ministry of Health is committed to transparency and accountability in the use of public resources, and adherence to the Country's Procurement laws and Financial Regulations, breach of which will attract appropriate sanctions.
"Gavi is committed to continue to support Nigeria in sustaining the achievements made in its vaccination programmes, and to support the efforts to eradicate polio on which much has already been achieved."
Meanwhile shock and outrage have greeted the international audit report.
Responding to the development, Professor of Virology and President of the Nigerian Academy of Science (NAS), Prof Oyewale Tomori in an email exchange with The Guardian expressed shock and outrage over the development. Narrating how the report had further dented Nigeria's image, he stressed why government should move quickly to bring the culprits to book.
Tomori, a former Regional Virologist and Laboratory Coordinator of the World Health Organization, observed that a preliminary version of the report released earlier in the year created discomfort within the donor community.
Responding to the audit, he told The Guardian: "This is a great story and glad that it was finally made public. I heard about this sometime ago. I was at a Gavi meeting earlier in the year and I was not too happy with the way people were looking at me, when I said I am from Nigeria. Only at tea break did someone tell me about the problems with Gavi funds in Nigeria.
"In better climates, the Minister under whose watch this shame has taken place will be dismissed and banned for holding any public office," He noted.
He stressed: "At least we have accepted responsibility. You remember the past times, such misuse, misappropriation, misdirection of funds happened...with EU funds and with Global Funds....we tried to 'bragaddo-brazzo' our way through. We even threatened to sue anybody who said we stole or misapplied the funds. This time, at least we took responsibility, accepting to pay back. It is not enough to pay back, Nigeria must find out who stole or misapplied the funds. When found, if found, they should be charged with the murder of all the children who died or were affected because they did not get the vaccine that Gavi provided funds for. But I am asking for the impossible! As we say nobody used the missing knife to cut yam!"
On the likely implications for Nigeria's quest for immunization for millions of children, he was worried that unless Nigeria takes the necessary action, the country could witness a decline in global funding for immunization and related activities.
His words: "We are likely to see a temporary reduction in financial support for procurement of vaccines. But they, donors, will soon forget and we too will also forget and continue where we left off, endangering the life of our children, ensuring more die from vaccine-preventable diseases."
He, stressed how, that the introduction of an intermediary to disburse funds from Gavi would add to the administrative cost of the programme, meaning less money for vaccines, stressing the need to quickly sanitise the system to restore the confidence of global support agencies.
"Confidence in Nigeria will nose dive, and who knows Gavi now wants to look back on how we spent money in previous years, and our government has accepted to refund any such funds that may be found to have been stolen. Integrity wise, we will move down a notch or two on the Transparency Index," he said.
He went on: "The readiness of the government to accept responsibility without at the same time give an undertaking to fish out the culprits also tells a lot about our respect for honour and probity."
He also charged the international donor agencies to evolve proactive measures to checkmate what he described as 'a national penchant for misusing funds.'
He spoke further: "They will still continue to supply more funds, for the sake of the children, but it is certain that the more children we produce, the more our greed will become for stealing more donor money.
"It is left for Nigeria to make examples of all those who might have been responsible for this international disgrace. But as sure as Christmas will come this 25th December, so sure I am that the culprits will receive national honours next year."
The Cash Programme Audit in Nigeria conducted by Geneva based Global Alliance for Vaccines and Immunization (Gavi) had taken a swipe at Nigeria for allegedly misusing funds meant to immunize millions of Nigerian children against killer diseases.
Since 2002, Gavi, which manages vaccine procurement centrally, had through the Federal Ministry of Health and the National Primary Health Care Development Agency (NPHCDA) given vaccine and cash-based support to Nigeria. A total amount of US$165m is said to have been disbursed for vaccines as at 31 March 2014, while US$143.5m represents cash support through a number of mechanisms.
The Ministry of Health and the National Primary Health Care Development Agency were fingered in the audit report to be involved in wide spread malpractices that has put the nation in bad light before international donor agencies.
The report released in Geneva and obtained by The Guardian, noted that the funds cover areas such as health systems strengthening (HSS), immunisation system support (ISS), operational costs for vaccine campaigns (Measles SIA, Meningitis,and Yellow Fever) and vaccine introduction grants (VIG).
Executive Director of NPHCDA, Dr. Ado Mohammed, had earlier told The Guardian that his agency and the ministry of health were preparing a comprehensive response, which would be made available soon.
The report indicates that the audit of cash support in Nigeria relate to funds disbursed in the period 2011-2013 Geneva, 28 October 2014.
And following from the initial preliminary report of the audit, cash-based support to Nigeria was suspended in April and funds previously disbursed but not yet used were immediately frozen. Information obtained from the report indicates that no further funds had been given to the Ministry of Health since the concerns were first identified.
"Remediation of the identified weaknesses will need to be undertaken before disbursements can start again," the final report noted.
According to Gavi, "To ensure that children will continue to benefit from vital immunisation activities despite these necessary measures, Gavi has asked UNICEF Nigeria to oversee the implementation of a limited number of activities that are deemed critical to routine immunisation and health system strengthening. Gavi , a vaccine alliance, brings together public and private sectors with the shared goal of creating equal access to vaccines for children, wherever they live.
According to Gavi: "The evidence is clear: vaccines are one of the most cost-effective investments in health and development in history. Gavi helps save children's lives and protect people's health by increasing access to immunisation in poor countries."
Following the report, Gavi issued an official statement requesting the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) to conduct a comprehensive investigation into the infractions.
The statement notes: "Gavi and its partners have a zero tolerance on the misuse of funds. When misuse is identified Gavi requires reimbursement. Inappropriate use of funds not only undermines confidence in development support but it means that the desired beneficiaries, who often already live in challenging circumstances, do not see much-needed improvements in the services.
"The report describes systemic weaknesses regarding the operation of controls and procedures in national systems used to manage Gavi cash-based support. As a result of specific findings the Government of Nigeria has agreed to repay funds deemed to have been misused, which are quantified as $2.2 million.
It went on: "Additionally, Gavi has requested that Nigeria's Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) carries out a thorough and detailed investigation. The EFCC has the jurisdiction to pursue individuals responsible for any criminal activity. Gavi's support for the provision of vaccines in Nigeria - a country where every year three million children do not receive a full course of even the most basic vaccines - remains unchanged."

Original_Search_Title: Algeria, Greece, China and Burkina Faso Partake in 6th FCIDC
Headline: Algeria, Greece, China and Burkina Faso Partake in 6th FCIDC
Body_Text:
Algiers — Troupes from Algeria, Greece, Burkina Faso and China played Monday night at the National Theatre Mahieddine Bachtarzi (TNA), part of the 6th International Cultural Festival of Contemporary Dance (FCIDC), held under the sign of reconciliation and friendship between peoples.
The Algerian troop "Duo nuance",which opened the evening with "Shadow and reality," a choreography of a quarter of an hour designed and made by Hennani Aissa and Najib Salhi in stretches of a dialogue between a sad reality facing its own failings, backed by a music with melancholic tone, composed by the duo.
For its part, Greek "Prosxima Dance Compagny " presented "A Solo for Harp year, a woman and a deer," choreographed by Maria Koliopoulou which searches to learn more about the body, carrying a living message.
"Yam-Ni-Ka" troupe from Burkina Faso entered into competition with "Macardey", a show dedicated to the abandoned children.
Chinese troupe "Dance College of Shanghai Theatre Academy" of China, the last to appear before the public, proposed a show where the precision of gesture compacted depth of message.

Original_Search_Title: Traditional Institution, Bedrock of Nigeria's Democracy - -Ozekhome
Headline: Nigeria: Traditional Institution, Bedrock of Nigeria's Democracy - -Ozekhome
Body_Text:
BENIN--HUMAN rights activist, Chief Mike Ozekhome, SAN, has said that the nation's traditional institution was the secret behind the level of development the country had attained so far.
He said that the nation's democracy would have crumbled but for the solid traditional institution which, according to him, had been able to curtail the excesses of overzealous politicians.
Speaking to newsmen in Benin shortly after completing the ceremonies and rituals that now make him a palace chief in Benin Kingdom, Ozekhome who attributed his success to the media and commended what he described as the fatherly role being played in the country by the Oba of Benin, said: "I Feel exhilarated and elated.
This is what Chinua Achebe will call growing like a yam tendril in the rainy season. I am grateful to the Oba of Benin Kingdom, Oba Erediauwa, for giving me this honour, a non-Bini person, deeming me worthy not just to be a son and scion of Benin Kingdom but to be a high chief and belong to the highest group in Benin Kingdom that represents the Oba anywhere in the world as an ambassador.
"I will continuously remain grateful to our traditional institution because without traditional rulers such as our Oba of Benin, we don't know where Nigeria would have been today

Original_Search_Title: Kate Henshaw - 'My Mum Didn't Want Me to Be a Politician'
Headline: Nigeria: Kate Henshaw - 'My Mum Didn't Want Me to Be a Politician'
Body_Text:
Kate Henshaw is considered to be one of the most talented actresses in Nigeria, which was why her family and friends were shocked to hear of her intentions to run for office in Rivers State, where she's from.
How did your family and friends respond when you said you wanted to become a politician?
My mum said 'no way'. I told her to pray for me and she said she wasn't going to, so I told her I'd pray for myself. Some cousins of mine in Calabar then called her and told her to encourage me, that what I want to do is good. She told them someone else should go into it, and they asked her why someone else should. They told her I should do it because it would be good for the state. She finally agreed.
Friends have also been supportive. They say I'm strong. They also say this is my kind of thing because I fight for people. But they advised me to be careful because politics is dirty. I told them the only way things will get better is if good people get inside. One can only change things if inside, not from the outside.
I'm gunning for the House of Reps representing Akpabio Bakassi federal constituency. During my campaign, one thing I know for sure is that I'm not going to promise heaven and earth. When you promise too much, you are may end up not doing anything and that becomes a problem. First and foremost, I'll start with the people. Mine is inclusive, I want to hear from the people directly, and how we're supposed to move forward and so on. I want to hear people say 'this is how we want it, this is how we should do it and this is how we are looking at it'. That way, we can move forward. I'm saying we need to gainfully engage youth to be able to support them. Like the case with Nollywood before government's recognition, the youth made themselves self-reliant. Also, looking at young women, a lot of them are skilled. I will do a lot for them. I want to support people.
Entertainers are gravitating towards politics in droves. Some say it is because it is seen as 'where the money is". What do you feel about this?
There's nothing wrong with entertainers going into politics. Doctors go into it, people without certificates go into it, so why shouldn't an entertainer do so? It is a right. As long as they are credible and will do the work they say they will, give them a chance.
How would you juggle entertainment and politics?
If I'm successful, of course entertainment would have to wait.
So when you get into politics, putting entertainment aside, would you say you have paid your dues?
Definitely. After over two decades, I would like to say so with all humility. And after all the time and energy I've put into my career, I want to believe that I have been professional, disciplined and dedicated. It is not easy to have worked for twenty one years and people still want to work with you. I like to think I have left a good legacy.
People have said some entertainers are magnets for bad PR. What's your take on that?
People who say that are those who don't know the entertainers personally or intimately. We have family and friends who know us very well outside of the roles we play in movies. They know who we are and what we stand for. We're human. Entertainers do a lot of good in the society. I can't judge or speak for everyone but some who are not afraid have stood up to speak when something is wrong.
Exiting the entertainment industry, won't you miss all those juicy endorsements?
This is about moving to another level, a bigger platform. This is more about serving and I think that is the greatest thing you can do for humanity. If you serve, there is no amount of money that can make up for the joy and happiness you feel when you know that you helped somebody and the person is happy. That is priceless.
Fast-forward to the future: If you win and it's your first day at work, how easily would you slip into that role?
I'm prepared. I have built myself to capacity. There will also be trainings and all that. It's not hard to do the right thing, really. One should know what people want and be able to speak out and get colleagues' support.
You are known for your candour. How will that factor into politics, an area known for all kinds of shadow games?
There are shadow games, true. But I believe that there are some good politicians out there. I'd say Hon. Abike Dabiri and Hon. Beni Lar are two excellent examples. There are other ladies I admire a lot in politics. I'll sit at the feet of such good company and learn a lot. I'm sure there are some good men out there too.
Politics is tough for women, but we're built strong and up to the task. It has not been easy at all. The thing that has worked for me is that they know my face, I'm well-known and they say I'm a good ambassador for the people. That's a good foundation for me, but then you still have to do the work of knowing the grassroots politicians.
Are you willing to sacrifice the kind amount of money that politics entail?
It has already cost me a lot, running into millions. It is not encouraging for any young person.
What is your favorite way to relax?
The spa, the spa, the spa! I can be there all day. I need it now more than ever, a quiet place where I can get a steam, massage and all the relaxing things available there.
What is your favorite dish?
Eba and Ekpang Nkukwo, which is a soup is made from cocoyam, water yam and pumpkin leaves with periwinkles, dried fish and palm oil. It is a special meal in Cross-River State for weddings and special occasions. My mum, God bless her heart, knows I love it so she makes it whenever I'm in town.
Do male politicians see you as a threat?
Perhaps they did earlier. They didn't expect me to come this far. But now they're beginning to identify with me as someone who is formidable and who will not back out anytime soon. Some of them who've interacted with me are like 'this small girl, where is she getting all this money from? Who is her godfather?'
But 'godfatherism' is the norm in Nigerian politics. Who is your godfather?
God the Father above.

Original_Search_Title: Don't Boycott Region, West African Tourist Chiefs Urge Travellers
Headline: West Africa: Don't Boycott Region, West African Tourist Chiefs Urge Travellers
Body_Text:
The outbreak of the Ebola virus in West Africa may not have impacted too negatively on many African economies as peddled. Judging from the reactions of government representatives and stakeholders at Platinum Suite 6, the recently held World Travel Market in London, where they reiterated how safe their countries were, despite the Ebola scourge providing facts and figures on how Ebola virus was curbed or prevented. West Africa's vibrant tourism industry might not be on a standstill as speculated after all. Omolola Itayemi writes.
Nigeria and Senegal have been declared Ebola-free and Ghana is yet to experience one index case, so vanguards of tourism in these countries are definitely qualified to discuss how big the problem of Ebola is in Africa for tourism. Moderated by Adama Bah, Director of the International Center for Responsible Tourism-West Africa (ICRT-WA), the panelists included Director General, Nigeria Tourism Development Corporation (NTDC), Dr Sally Mbanefo and some foreign tour operators. The discussion started with the misconception of Africa as a country and not a continent. This view which is very prevalent in other continents aside Africa sees the continent as one whole country where what affects the Southern part will invariably affect the West. This is never the case as cultures, lifestyles and locations separate each country from the next. "Africa is not a country, Africa is a continent," said Ola Wright, the chief executive of West Africa Tourism, warning that fear over Ebola in Guinea, Liberia and Sierra Leone was having a damaging impact on neighbouring countries. The deadly virus has brought an abrupt and indefinite halt to international tourism in those three affected countries, where almost 5,000 people have died in the outbreak. But tourism has been affected across the region and even in east and southern Africa, which are thousands of miles away and have not reported Ebola cases. "We've had a lot of complaints from different people, from hoteliers, tour operators, complaining that sales have really gone down and of loads of different cancellations. The fear is more damaging than the actual problem. It's just ignorance," Wright told AFP at the World Travel Market fair in London. Sierra Leone pulled out of the international travel show exhibition last week, while Guinea and Liberia do not normally attend, but Senegal and Nigeria both had stands.
Curtailing Ebola and Hosting Key Tourism Events During The Scare... In this instance, Nigeria and Senegal were the countries on the hot seat. Starting with Mbanefo who in her speech declared that the ability of Nigeria to curtail the deadly Ebora Virus Disease (EVD) was made possible by the commitment of the federal government and the love Nigerian citizens have for each other.
The development came even as she praised the role played by the late Senior Consultant Physician at the First Foundation Hospital, Obalende, Dr Stella Adadevoh who she said single-handedly saved Nigeria from what would have been a disaster, if she had allowed the first index case, Liberian/American Patrick Sawyer leave the hospital despite mounting pressure. She also asked the audience to observe a one-minute silence for the deceased who literally put her life on the line for citizens of her country. Asked to educate the world on how Nigeria was able to curtail the disease, Mbanefo identified several strategies employed by the federal government to nip in the bud the disease to include the successful tracing of the over 800 people discovered to have contact with the victims and their subsequent monitoring.
Also employed were the aggressive enlightenment campaigns in the social media, advertisement, patriotism on the parts of the media, generousity of private sectors, the love for one another by the Nigerians to join hands with the government to end the scourge. "In Nigeria, we have a committed government which cares for its citizens. The government did not waste a second in embarking on serious activities across the country to prevent the spread of the disease. We are grateful to Dr Adadevoh for sacrificing her live to safe Nigeria," she declared.
She however insisted that despite the temporary experience of the disease, tourism activities still continued, a situation she attributed to the proactive nature of the government. According to her, throughout the periodof the fight against the disease tourism activities were ongoing. These included Osun Osogbo, Sango and National New Yam (Iri Ji Ndi Igbo) festivals respectively; with over 50, 000 foreign tourists in attendance while 25, 000 tourists participated in the New Yam festival in the Eastern part of the country. Mbanefo speaking the mind of others, cautioned against the ongoing misconception, the foreign media had about Africa, saying it was not the true picture of things happening in West Africa that are being portrayed.
According to her, about 16 countries made up the West African region and therefore, the outbreak of the Ebola virus in just three out of the lot should not attract the level of stigmatisation the entire region is experiencing from the international community. Others who spoke at the conference while calling for cooperation among the African countries as a whole cautioned that any attempt to do otherwise might do the entire continent more harm in the face of the geographical misconception of the continent by the developed world.
Houma Mbaye Dia of Senegal speaking the mind of others cautioned against the ongoing misconception the foreign media had about Africa saying it was not the true picture of things happening in West Africa that were being portrayed. Senegal still retains its pride of place in the tourism industry. Not resting on their oars, Dia informed the audience about more infrastructures such as the new conference centre in Senegal that will aid tourism. Tourism activities did not suffer any lull during the scare period. Ofosu Adjare of Ghana in her speech reiterated the need for tourism in West Africa. "Fortunately, Ghana has not had a case of Ebola but we are not sitting pretty. We have put all the infrastructures in place to avoid such. Our borders are on high alert and we have our isolation wards ready in case we come up with such. "Tourism is big with us and we have all what tourists want, from our sandy beaches to historic sites to enchanting castles. We have it all, come to Ghana and your experience will never be the same", she said.
Unite4Africa... Tour operators working in West Africa were also at the London event to launch a new campaign to support people of the region in their response to Ebola. "Unite4WestAfrica intends to raise money for charity and also promote a positive story of West Africa to help it attract tourists in the future. Tourism in countries such as Sierra Leone was only really beginning to develop following the ravages of the country's civil war in the 1990s, and it has now been set back again. These countries were just establishing themselves," said David Oades of Overlanding West Africa. He has cancelled this season's overland trips because of border closures but is hopeful they will resume next year.
Safari Booking Canceled... The effects of Ebola are being felt even further afield. More than half of South African travel agents, tour operators, car hire and conferencing businesses say the Ebola outbreak has had a negative impact, according to the latest Tourism Business Index published in October. The lucrative safari industry is suffering from this misconception, little wonder, a survey based on feedback from more than 500 safari operators conducted by African booking company Safaribookings.com found that as of late September, more than half reported a 20 to 70% decline in bookings. "Tour operators reported that many tourists view Africa as a single country when it comes to risk assessment. They don't realise that east and southern Africa, where most safaris are conducted, are just as far from the outbreak area as Europe or South America," the website said at the time. "The general misconception that Africa is one huge country might just be one of the major setbacks for tourism in Africa. Most people don't realise how vast Africa is and that East and southern Africa are just as far from the outbreak area as Europe or South America. "Most guilty of this are electronic media houses in the Western world who usually believe and disseminate information that what happens in one region of Africa automatically affects the other as seen during the Ebola scourge. "But despite the fact that the main safari destinations in southern and eastern Africa are thousands of kilometers from the Ebola-stricken countries of the west, industry leaders say fears of the disease are keeping tourists away. "Numerous safari tour operators report to us that clients view Africa as a single country when it comes to risk assessment," Robert Cerea of TransAfrica and Ben Keene of Tribewanted, tells the audience.
Keene says the impact is most noticeable in East Africa, home to safari hotspots such as Kenya's Masai Mara and Tanzania's Serengeti -- places located nearly 5,000 kilometers from the West Africa Ebola outbreak area. However, Precautionary measures in western, eastern and southern African countries fighting to keep Ebola from entering their borders vary, with some countries banning travelers who have visited affected countries, while others have adopted strict border screening measures. From Nigeria to Senegal to Ghana, the response was the same from its representatives on the high table: "Ebola is no threat to Tourism in our countries."

Original_Search_Title: I Picked Banking From My Step Mum - Omorede Osifo
Headline: Nigeria: I Picked Banking From My Step Mum - Omorede Osifo
Body_Text:
Omorede Osifo is the immediate past Commissioner for Sports, Youth Development and Social Mobilisation in Edo State. In this exclusive interview with Special Correspondent, ISAAC OLAMIKAN, the tall and well built woman talks about her childhood; why she studied banking; her passion and much more. Excerpts:
Madam, how was your childhood days?
I had a wonderful childhood that prepared me for who I am today. I went to one of the best primary schools in Benin City then, Emotan Primary School. In my days it was a Catholic school run by the nuns - Reverend Fathers and Reverend Sisters. From there, I went to St. Maria Gorretti Girls Grammar School, another school that was run by a Reverend Sister from Ireland. From there, I went to the University of Benin (UNIBEN), where I did my first degree in Economics and Statistics. I went back there when I was working in the bank to do Masters in Banking and Finance. So, as you can see, I did all my schooling in Benin, outside of Harvard University where I went to do a course in governance, because, I felt that I am now in government, I need to do something in government. So, I did a course, Innovation in Governance at Harvard University in the United States of America.
Growing up was good although a sad aspect was when I lost my mother at a very young age of 11 years. I am the first girl in the family; I became the mother to all my younger ones at that very young age. That is the only sad aspect. But, before my mother died, she schooled in Europe, she did nursing in Europe - London and Ireland - she was the only black woman in her class then. She was at a time the head of the UBTH (University of Benin Teaching Hospital) Nursing School before her death - Principal Nursing Tutor. We had a nice time with her and my father when she was still alive. But, after she left, we had to get stronger, we had to work harder. I believe that part of her not been there made me a strong woman that I am today.
What informed your choice of banking as a career?
When my mum died, my father had a wife who was a banker. She used to talk about the profession a lot. So, I said: What is in this banking? I too would be a banker. It was one of those things. Somehow, I started studying bankers and I fell in love with them. I like figures. My dad, whom we all got that gene from, was a Mathematician when he was in the university. Everybody knew him as a Mathematician. Till date, some people still ask me, 'How is your father, the Mathematician?' Even if I am not good in any subject, I love Mathematics. My kids are like that too. They do Mathematics, Further Mathematics and the other Mathematics courses. So, it runs in the family. You need Mathematics, you need calculating to be in banking. It all fell into place - loving Mathematics and now something that is interesting, having to do with money and Mathematics, which was banking. That was why I became a banker. And of course, bankers are professional people. I used to like the way they look in their suits, both the men and the women, when they dress up to go to work. You know, very professional and serious minded kind of people in those days. Those were part of the reasons I did banking.
When did you travelled to America and how was life there?
I got to America in 1997 after I finished my masters in UNIBEN. Then, I had left banking and I just wanted to have a change of scene. I didn't stay too long there. I was there for about three years. Basically, I went to see what banking was like in the US. As God would have it, I got a job in a mortgage banking institution. I was doing mortgage loan, which was my aspect in banking. In banking, I was a loan officer. It was my job to analyse multinational companies - I was in a merchant bank. I did my NYSC in Allied Bank from where I moved to Crown Merchant Bank. At Crown Merchant Bank, we used to deal with multinational companies. We give them loans and it was my duty to analyse their proposal and approve their loans. In America, I worked in a mortgage banking institution where I was approving loans for individuals to buy houses or refinance their houses.
My stay in America was good. It was while I was in America that I decided I wanted to be a politician; I wanted to come back home and serve my people. That aspect of my life made me who I am today. Probably, if I didn't go to America, and didn't see the likes of Hillary Clinton and the rest of them, I'll probably not have had that desire to come back home and be a politician.
What did you pick from Hillary Clinton and the others that made you have that desire to be a politician?
One of the things that I picked from them is that when I came back home, the first thing I wanted to do is to empower women. Hillary was in the forefront of women empowerment. During the Beijing conference, she presented some papers and in one of those papers which I read online, a little girl was saying that in those days, her mother's place used to be in the kitchen; a woman is not supposed to be heard, etc. But now, things have changed. We need to talk. We can be in the kitchen; but, we need to talk to make changes that we want to happen. We can't make changes by sitting at home and not talking. I like that aspect of Hillary Clinton. Another aspect of her that I like was the way she stood behind her husband, Bill, during the Monica Lewinsky issue. That was another thing that endeared her to me. I said: 'This is a strong woman. I want to be like her in my country'. So, immediately I came home, I started pulling women together all over the country, to see how we can empower women. I had several meetings with them to tell them 'we cannot continue to sit at home. Like Hillary was doing, we have to come out. We're our husband's mate, we're there to support. If a man carries all the burdens it would be difficult for him. A woman comes out and makes a living and works hard too it would be easier for our children to get quality life, education and all that. A woman must be educated. Women should not just be at home without been educated.'
That was where we started. The news spread everywhere.
Thereafter, I joined politics. I contested the primaries for the House of Representatives which I won; but, I was denied victory. That was in 2002/3. From there, I have held Commissioner positions; I have also been a Special Adviser among several other executive positions.
Have you achieved your goal of women empowerment?
Well, to an extent, I'll say yes. But, I have not reached the level I want to reach. I have been fulfilled in the fact that I have talked to a lot of women and I have one way or the other impacted knowledge on a lot of them. A lot of them have now come out to participate in politics. Before I engaged them only a few women were in politics. As at that time, only Senator Daisy Danjuma and I were the women who were eyeing politics at the national level. I am happy that what we started in 2001/2 is now spreading like wild bush fire. Today, we have a lot of women in the National Assembly. It is not enough as we're yet to reach our target of 35 per cent. For me, I have had opportunities to work in government and it has been good, because, I have had opportunities to impact on the life of my people. The greater part is yet to come. When we get to where we want to, then, we'll be fully fulfilled.
It seems Hillary Clinton is one of your role models? Which other role models do you have?
There is also Queen Idia Esigie of Benin Kingdom. She was a strong Benin woman who stood for her son, the Oba of Benin, Oba Esigie. While trying to save him, she lost her life. Emotan is another. She was a trader who saved the Oba too. She heard people plotting and she quickly passed the information. Today, she is a heroine too in Benin.
There is also Queen Edeh. These are powerful women who, when they believed in something, they'll go all out to give it their all. I am a strong woman. When I mean to do something, I'll go all out for it. So, I love strong women.
You're the immediate past Commissioner for Sports, Youth Development and Social Mobilisation in Edo State. Till date, you're a reference point in the sports sector in the state. Why is it so?
I am passionate about everything I do. I remember that my boss then, the Comrade Governor, used to tell everybody that one thing he likes about me is that I am very passionate about sports; I am very passionate about what I am doing. I am passionate about everything I do. I put everything I have into any assignment I am given, because, I don't want to be a failure. I want to go and let people remember me that 'yes, this person was there.' I did the same thing when I was in Culture and Tourism (Ministry). Up till now, they still talk about what I did there. Sports is something that I love; something that I grew up with as my dad was the Director of Sports in Bendel State. We used to go to the stadium and play different sports - Volleyball, Basketball, Handball, Table Tennis, etc. When I came into the Ministry of Sports, I had the encouragement of Governor Adams Oshiomhole, who said that he wanted some changes in the sector. I was working hard not to disappoint him and the people of Oredo Local Government Area who nominated me as a commissioner. I was close to the sports men/women, coaches, etc. I ran an open door policy. I was not one of those commissioners that you need to book an appointment with one week or you come to my office and wait for five hours before you can see me. The sportsmen/women, coaches and the likes come in to see me and leave when I have attended to them. What has to be done has to be done. That policy helped me to know what their desires are. Once you know their desires then you can proffer solutions. When I came in as the commissioner, Bendel Insurance was on the verge of relegation.
They were been owed salaries for several months. On assumption of office, I said 'no, Bendel Insurance cannot go into relegation.' I brought in some new faces to the team and within a short time the team was no more in the relegation zone. The team was now in the middle of the table in the second division. My plan was to get the team to the premier league. We tried as much as possible to pay the players a large chunk of the salaries that they were owed by previous administrations. They were not owed match bonuses as they were paid promptly. Welfare matters a lot to sportsmen, women and their coaches. So, we ensured that we do not lag behind in this regard.
To win national competitions you must take part in a lot of competitions. I made sure that they went for national competitions wherever they were held. All our sporting associations were given the opportunity to travel to any part of the country where national competitions were held. I was present in most of these competitions as it boosts the athletes' morale. I have gone for a match where the Bendel Insurance was not doing well but immediately I came in the tide changed and they won.
What is your most cherished fashion item?
I don't think that I have any fashion item that I cherish. I don't think I worship any fashion item.
What determines what you wear when you go out?
The function I am going to determines what I wear. If I am going for a traditional event, I won't wear suits. I'll wear my traditional outfit. If I am going for some official things that needs the wearing of suit, that is what I'll wear. If I am going for a birthday party, I won't go and wear native if it is an indoor party. I'll probably wear my jeans and shirts, which I feel comfortable in. The thing is that as I decide what to wear, I will make sure that it looks good. I won't wear a covered shoe that is meant for a suit on a native outfit. Those things matter. I won't wear coral beads on a skirt suit. I can wear my coral beads when I am wearing my traditional attire. Those are the things to look out for - matching your colours, wearing the right shoes, the right bag with the right outfit, etc. That is what makes you a perfect person when you dress up.
What are you passionate about?
My passion at this point in my life is first of all to be the best in whatever I do; to serve my people, which I have done so many times at the state level; I also have a dream of serving them at the national level some day. I want to stand out from the crowd. Passion can vary from time to time. It can be passion in my job; passion in my service to my people in politics; it can also be passion in my house among my family, my kids. I want to see them be the best that they are.
What is your favourite meal?
I love a variety of food. I love local and continental food. It depends on my mood. When it comes to Nigerian food, I love starch and banga soup. I love yam and owo. I love continental food. I love to have vegetable salad, French fries, etc. I love Chinese food as well to be really spicy.
What is your favourite colour?
I love lemon green. It is one of my favourite colours. I love pink as well. It is a woman's colour.
Do you love pets?
No. I am not in love with pets. I don't have dogs, pussycats, crocodiles and lions. Some people have lions as pets.
You are a well travelled woman. Which city do you love most?
I have been in over 16 countries. Basically, my travels are to see the difference in lifestyles. I believe that it is when you see the different ways of living that you become a greater person. For example, developments in some countries are far better than in others. I had a programme on TV where I had to showcase tourist sites, because, when I left my appointment as Commissioner for Tourism and Culture, I decided to do something on culture and tourism. That took me to places like the Great Walls of China to compare with our Benin Moats to see what and what we can do to revive arts, etc. When I was the Commissioner for Arts and Culture, I was the first to pioneer a cultural exchange programme between Nigeria and Germany. This saw Nigerians acting alongside the Germans. We did a play, Shakespeare's Tempest in Bochum, Germany. When I went I met the Mayor of Bochum; government officials, etc. Dr. Samuel Ogbemudia, former governor of the defunct Bendel State, went with us on the trip.
So, which of the cities that you've been to do you love most?
I love most of them. So, I don't know which one that I love most. One of the cities is London. I used to like Britain; it is conservative. But, later, I went to America, which is not as conservative as is Britain.
America is fast. If you're what they call mumu you'll not go far in America. You've to shine your eye, as a Nigerian parlance says.
Otherwise, they'll catch you mugu. So, I like the American lifestyle of being fast, because from Lagos, I moved to America; and Lagos we know, is fast. Venezuela is another country I have been to. The place is nice; but what will attract you about their women is that they all look so beautiful. But, I found out that they do a lot of plastic surgery. That is why their women look like that.
Do you believe in reincarnation?
As a Christian, I don't know if I should say 'yes'. But, I feel that it may be true, because, there are instances where a father dies and the son has a child and say my dad is coming back through me. And the child has a birthmark of that late person and does a lot of things like him. It might be possible or impossible. Who knows? It is not for me to say.
If you believe in reincarnation and you have the opportunity of coming back to the world, what would you want to be?
If I come back again I want to come back as myself, Omorede Osifo, a woman of substance, a strong woman, a woman that can achieve anything she wants to achieve, a woman that sets goals and meets her goals and targets, a woman that so many women want to be like.

Original_Search_Title: Tension in Nnobi Over Traditional Stool, As Community Holds Parallel New Yam Festivals
Headline: Nigeria: Tension in Nnobi Over Traditional Stool, As Community Holds Parallel New Yam Festivals
Body_Text:
Awka — Two traditional rulers, Igwe Nick Obi and Igwe Ebubechukwu Etudo, are laying claims to the stool of Nnobi community in Anambra State, and have held a separate new yam festivals, thus causing confusion and tension among youths in the community.
Daily Independent recalls that Igwe Etudo was initially laying claims to the stool for long and was seen as the monarch until the government of Governor Peter Obi recognized Igwe Obi after he resigned as commissioner in the Obi cabinetAnd now that Nnobi woman is the Commissioner for Education, Prof Kate Omenugha, Igwe Etudo started again after being quiet for long and is engaging in a court process.
Igwe Obi has protested the continued activities of Igwe Ebubechukwu, who he described as an impersonator and called on the police to forestall the breakdown of law and order in the community.
In a petition by the leadership of International Society for Civil Liberties and Rule of Law (Intersociety), signed by Comrade Emeka Umeagbalasi ,addressed to the Assistant Inspector General of Zone 9, Usman Gwary, Commissioner of Police Anambra State, Hosea Karma and Chairman, Police Service Commission, Mike Okiro, the group called for the maintenance of law and order and the protection of recognised traditional institution and the entire Nnobi community.
Intersociety frowned at the activities of some contestants to the royal stool of the community which has been threatening the peace of the people in the area despite the fact that all litigations arising from the disputed chieftaincy have been resolved, which led to the government of Anambra State issuing a certificate of recognition to Igwe Obi on 4th July 2011 and presented him with a staff of office.
The group condemned roles played by the DPO of Nnobi police division and the area commande in charge of Awka area command in the chieftaincy tussle saga and called on th police hierarchy to ensure that government pronouncements are enforced at all times in the matter. Intersociety said: "Section 19 and 20 of the Anambra State Traditional Rulers Law of 1981 forbid those not recognised as traditional rulers from addressing or parading themselves as if they are. They are also barred from holding, sponsoring or participating in community activities under whatever names called in parallel with or outside the consent or knowledge of the community's recognized chiefs or traditional rulers. Contravention of the provisions of these sections attracts on conviction two years imprisonment without option of fine. We call on your good offices to enforce these provisions to the letter"
The idea of using or converting the dispute in the said community into money making venture by way of indiscriminate and seeming police invitation and arrest must be avoided," the group said.
Efforts by Daily Independent to get the reactions of the two claimants to Nnobi throne proved abortive as at time of filling in this report.

Original_Search_Title: Who Is Behind Confessions of a Bisexual Chick?
Headline: South Africa: Who Is Behind Confessions of a Bisexual Chick?
Body_Text:
A year ago, Ntombikayise Matyumza of Jeffreys Bay in the Eastern Cape started a Facebook page aimed at gay and lesbian youth. Today she has more than 14,000 followers across the country.
The Confessions of a Bisexual Chick tells the tale of a character called Phumeza and her lifestyle, sexuality and experiences. It has become a platform for readers to communicate with Matyumza and with each other.
Her readers call her Phumeza, Mamzo (mother), or Maroza (a nickname) and she refers to them as her "babies".
Matyumza says she started the page because she hated the way lesbians were portrayed as victims. "I am a lesbian and I started the page because I wanted to show people that we also live happy lives".
"I felt we needed to stop telling homophobic people how to treat us. We keep on telling them to treat us like human beings and that are we no different from them but instead they keep treating us as if we're aliens with some sort of disease. We just have to live life to the fullest and I preach this on my page", says Matyumza.
One of the main messages she wants to put across is that lesbians shouldn't behave like victims of crimes like homophobia.
Her page is also read by heterosexual people, like the reader who wrote to say she is straight but learning many things about life and social issues.
Matyumza says her parents separated when she was 13 and she and her brother lived with her grandmother in Port Elizabeth for a while, then with her mother and mother's boyfriend, before moving back to Jeffreys Bay where she currently lives with her father and stepmother.
Matyumza says in her high school years she realised that she was attracted more to women than to men. "I find women more interesting and I am happy with the sexual choice I have made."
She enjoys good music and she says that poetry is her first love. She is currently studying Environmental Health at Mahube Training and Development in Kouga Municipality.
Matyumza says she loves writing and hopes one day to write a book.
Her page attracts both applause and criticism. "I do not mind being criticised. It gives me a platform to improve and be challenged creatively."
One of her readers posted:
Yaz diary yakho ingath uyibhala ngam sum tyms ndizbona ndilila xandiyfunda cz bangath ubalisa ibali lam whn pumeza was raped n gt pregnant ingath wawuthetha nam cz i went thru the same shit bt owam mntana died,pumeza dated her sista mna i dated my czn,mtshato ka pumeza vele watshintsha nje spacin nam relationship yam of 2years vele yaphela nge 2weeks. better person nw cz of idiary yakho i can safely say u are a true blessing kum n nakwabanye abantu keep writting uThixo akwandisele kwinto yonke n Thank you so much..
(When you write your diary it seems as if you are writing about me. Sometimes I see myself crying when you write about Phumeza and her rape and pregnancy. It seems as if it's my story, but the difference is my baby died. My relationship of two years disappeared as if it had been for two weeks. Because of your diary I am a better person now and I can safely say you are a true blessing to me and to other people.)
Matyumza has written more than 100 episodes of the Phumeza "diary" and her readers are asking for more, but she believes that it is time for her to move on to write other things.
"I feel like the diary has reached its climax. But I am not going to forget where I started."
She says she is working on a new page where she will tell new stories about gay and lesbian youth.
"I will start by exposing my untold life stories".
"I can't reveal much about it as it's still early days", she says.
Read Confessions of a Bisexual Chick on Facebook.

Original_Search_Title: Adedamola... the Shining Face of the World
Headline: Nigeria: Adedamola... the Shining Face of the World
Body_Text:
ADEDAMOLA Cruz Aedebowal, a native of Ondo State, is the 2014 Face of the World (Nigeria). He studied computer science at the Obafemi Awolowo University, Ife. In 2008, Adedamola started modeling at the age of 21 and in 2011, he won the Best Model in Nigeria crown. He runs an event company known as Dream Cruz Entertainment Ltd, the organisers of Ikeja City Mall Music Show. He is also a choreographer, stage co-ordinator and catwalk instructor. In this chat with GERALDINE AKUTU, Adedamola who will represent Nigeria in Face Of The World Competition in United Kingdom, in March 2015, talks about his journey into modeling.
How has the journey been?
In everything I do, I put in my best. I'm a prayerful person and there's never been a dull moment in my career. It's been a great success for me, really.
What gave you the advantage in the competition?
I am bold and have that motivating spirit. I never believe in failure nor give up. Growing up, I had a mother that encouraged me and told me that I was the best. So, I grew up with that mindset.
When you wanted to go into modeling, what was your parents' reaction?
Well, when I told my mum about it, she prayed on it and encouraged me to put it into prayer. Later, she consented and asked me to promise her that I would go to school because for her, education is very important.
What makes you stand out as a model?
I think I have a big God and I'm a product of grace. I always look out for ways to improve and I'm very confident.
Your growing up
Growing up was fun and sweet. My siblings and I are closely knitted... my mum played volleyball for Nigeria, and she used to have a music band, way back then. We are always there for each other. In my house, it is either you know music or sports. I grew up singing, but now, I'm into fashion business.
Describe yourself in three words?
Fun, hard working and dedicated.
Role models
When I was growing, I always looked up to Tyson Bedford, an American model. As God would have it, in my first ever show, Tyson Bedford came to Nigeria. Everyone wanted to take pictures with him but the camera didn't work, I told them to snap me with him and the camera worked. Afterwards, the camera stopped working again. Andy Ogbechi is another role model that I admire so much because he taught me how to walk on the runway. In business, the likes of Aliko Dangote and Mike Adenuga comes to mind. Internationally, I love Steve Jobs for his creativity and Dr. Ben Carson, who is exceptionally gifted.
What kind of books do you read?
I read inspirational and motivational books, magazines, Christian journals, newspapers and magazines, as long as they make meaningful sense.
Describe yourself dress sense?
In every competition, I always win the award of best dressed. In Best Model of the World, I won the 'Best Designed Costume'. In Model of Africa, I won 'Mr. Style'. I love to look good and go the extra mile to achieve that.
Favourite designer
Royal House of Boa, Mai Atafo, David Bowler, Oswald Boateng, and Sheri Ngowi.
Your definition of fashion
Fashion is all about discovering your style and wearing what looks good on you.
How do you deal with female admirers?
I handle them with care and respect, but know my boundaries. I'm in a wonderful relationship with my fiancée. Hopefully, we are getting married next year.
Favourite food
Pounded yam with egusi soup made by my mum and Indomie Noodle prepared by my wife to be.
What do you think of modeling in Nigeria?
It's growing rapidly... we are getting there, doing proper shoots, competition, creative designers that are recognised globally, but I believe that more should be done to take it to the next level.
What areas of modeling should be addressed to make it better?
I think payment of models should be addressed. They should pay us the way they pay international models and we should be respected. Modeling is not for every Tom, Dick and Harry, but for smart and intelligent people. We are the definition of fashion because we show you what fashion should look like.
What do you enjoy doing?
I teach catwalk and choreograph shows.
Where do you see yourself five years from now?
I see myself as a force to reckon with in fashion and entertainment. I'm working on something now, which will be unveiled soon.
Any message for those aspiring to be models
Modeling is not about you having a fine face or body, but takes the grace of God. Pray, don't rush, try to learn from people before you and be focused.

Original_Search_Title: Lifeless Body Dumped in Pastor's House in Kaduna
Headline: Nigeria: Lifeless Body Dumped in Pastor's House in Kaduna
Body_Text:
Kaduna — A lifeless body of a man identified as Najir Yusuf was dumped on Friday in the compound of Pastor Solomon Bida located at Malali village in Kaduna North Local government area. The pastor, while speaking to our correspondent, said he woke up around 6.30am and noticed the lifeless body lying down at the uncompleted part of his compound.
"I thought it was a man sleeping due to darkness. But that notwithstanding, I raised alarm to attract other neighbours around. My son with my wife joined me. I did not touch the body. But the police officer who came said some parts of the deceased were removed by unknown persons.
"There is a police man who lives close to my house. I quickly sent for him. When he came, he confirmed that it was a lifeless body. Immediately, he alerted Malali Divisional Police Officer (DPO) who came and removed the corpse. Some suspects had been arrested, including the elder brother of the dead man," he said.
Investigation revealed that the late Najir had always assisted his elder brother Abdullahi at his shop located at their main house where he sold yams and Irish potato.
It was also gathered that the deceased disappeared about three days ago. All efforts to locate him had proved abortive until his corpse was found where it was dumped at the pastor's compound. The Pastor, Solomon Bida, who presides over Cherubim and Seraphim church located at Niger Street, Malali Federal Low-Cost, said he was still in shock becaused of what had happened.
He prayed to God to expose the perpetrators.
Our correspondent attempted to speak with the elder brother of the deceased, who was being held at the Malali police station as at the time of filing this report. The attempt was not successful.
The Police Public Relations Officer, SP Aminu Lawal confirmed the incident and said investigation had commenced into the matter.
In a related development, the residents of Ungwan Dosa in Kaduna state woke up on Friday morning to find the dead body of a teenager alleged to have been electrocuted while trying to steal an electricity cable near a refuse dump in the area.
An eye witness, who gave his name as Abubakar Mohammed and a resident of Ungwan Dosa said "I came out of my house around 7.15am to drop my children at Research School, on my way back, I came in collusion with a large crowd at a refuse dump near the transformer located within the Sardauna Memorial College premises where an MTN mass is located.
"I parked my vehicle and came down to where the crowd had gathered, upon getting there, I saw the dead body of a boy laying under the transformer, I started to ask people around what was was going on and someone showed me an electricity cable which was hanging and said they think the boy is the one who cut the cable and in the process, he got electrocuted," he said.
Another witness, Aminu Mohammed said "the boy's name is Lawi, as he is popularly called and lives in Badarawa. But his parents live at Mararaban Jos. I have known him for quite some time because he is my brother's friend. He used to repair tyres in Ungwan Sarki before he started refuse collection in Ungwan Dosa."
He said the relations of the boy came in the morning and took the corpse for burial at Mararaban Jos.

Original_Search_Title: Ndigbo in Lagos Celebrate New Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Ndigbo in Lagos Celebrate New Yam
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ndigbo residents in Ikeja on Saturday celebrated this year's new yam festival with a call for peaceful co-existence of Ndigbo and their Yoruba hosts which started in the past.
The Eze Ndigbo of Ikeja, Eze Uche I.C. Dimgba, who was the chief host of the occasion made the call in an address during the Iriji Ndigbo Unity Place in conjunction with Ohaneze Ndigbo Ikeja which took place at Victory Home School, Toyin Street, Ikeja Lagos.
Dimgba who is also the Opkotemba of Igbere in Bende Local Government of Abia State said the Ndigbo had been living in Lagos and are as old as Lagos itself and "we have been living peacefully with our Yoruba brothers and sisters without much problem and will want the peaceful coexistence to continue."

Original_Search_Title: Iwuanyanwu Eulogises Madumere As Possessing Leadership Qualities
Headline: Nigeria: Iwuanyanwu Eulogises Madumere As Possessing Leadership Qualities
Body_Text:
Ibrahim Shuaibu in Kano Chief Emmanuel Iwuanyanwu has eulogised the Deputy Governor of Imo State, Prince Eze Madumere, as humble and exhibiting leadership qualities and cultural values.
Chief Iwuanyanwu made the statement during the new yam festival at Ikeduru at a ceremony held at Eziama, Ikeduru Local Government of Imo State.
The PDP BoT member showered encomium on Prince Madumere for his strength of character with high level of positive values. He went further to describe him as the best deputy governor the state ever had.
Iwuanyanwu, who said he made the statement devoid of political sentiment, assured the people of Mbaitoli-Ikeduru Federal Constituency of his unwavering support for Prince Eze Madumere in whatever project he might want to embark on as far as leadership is concerned.
