The encomium by Chief Iwuanyanwu reinforces the recognition given to Madumere from a recent survey as one of the ten Most Outstanding Deputy Governors in Nigeria.
Reacting to Chief Iwuanyanwu's comment on Madumere, former Chairman of Peoples Democratic Party, Chief Marcon Nlemigbo affirmed the position, stating, "My brother Madumere has history and has proudly added so much value to the younger generation. He has remained consistent in his chosen lifestyle of simplicity, humility and a believer in team work with sincerity of purpose."
Chief Iwuanyanwu added voice to other respectable Nigerians and validated all that have been said about the "Most Dependable Deputy Governor in the South East.
The politician would have been moved by Madumere calmness and decorum with a well couched words during his address to people of Ikeduru, the people he described as his 'siblings' due to the ancestral and political relationship.
In what seemed to be a hostile environment owing to the opposition who had besieged the venue of the event, Prince Madumere, who was recently recognised with the African Personality Award proved his mettle he calmed the over bloated nerves and respectfully addressed the crowd in a manner people were rose to cheer him.
He used the opportunity to assure the people Ikeduru to continue in their support for Owelle Rochas Okorocha led Rescue Mission Government while promising Government's resolve to rehabilitate some economically viable roads in the Local Government Area.
It is being mooted by various sections of the public that Governor Okorocha and his humble deputy have shown Nigerians what it means to work together as a team as they remain among the few most celebrated twosome at the helms of affairs at the State level governance.

Original_Search_Title: See Me Wahala!
Headline: Nigeria: See Me Wahala!
Body_Text:
One year has gone by since the Editor of Saturday Independent, Mr. Charles Okogene, 'pressured' me to write a weekly column in his newspaper. It was with some trepidation that I agreed and began the conversation that has over flown to Facebook. I am persuaded to repost my first article published one year ago in the Locomotion series because it captures my state of mind at that time. Please read on:
I once wrote a small column for The Punch. I was a lot younger with a lot of fire in the belly. Despite the mad tussle with deadlines and the once in a while writers block, it was fun. I had very strong opinions and could not wait for people to hear them. Of course, when you are young, you think that you are the only one who has an opinion that counts. As you get older however, you begin to realise how stupid you have been. Everybody has an opinion and everybody thinks that their opinion counts.
A 29-year-old American called Mark Elliot Zuckerberg, found out how much everybody wants to express their opinion. As a result, Zuckerberg has become a billionaire several times over. On Zuckerberg's Facebook, everybody is expressing their opinion to everybody whether they want to hear or not. Dirty linens are being washed with the whole world watching and the stinking water splashed around with complete abandon. Girls are stalking guys on Facebook and guys are enjoying being stalked. You can't be on Facebook and not say something. It is like a huge Oyingbo market. So, everybody is talking, everybody is lying and everybody is posing. In the new world of Facebook, Twitter, Instagram and BBM, everybody is a publisher, everybody is an editor and everybody is a columnist.
Several times recently, I have been asked to write a column for a newspaper. Several times I have said no. Why? As I have grown older, I have taken more to heart the Igbo saying that a palm wine tapper does not reveal everything he sees from the top of the palm tree. Just imagine what would happen in the kindred if the local palm wine tapper had to describe the luscious naked women who thought they were bathing safely away from prying eyes or the illicit love affairs where the lovers thought they were in a world of their own and that apart from the skies, their secret was all theirs or the big tubers of yam thought to be secure from all and sundry in the rich man's barn. Like the local palm wine tapper, I have seen quite a bit and I have thought it better for me to stay away from the babble.
The other matter is: Is anyone really interested in what I have to say? Is anyone going to read what I write when all the kids are busy pinging and charting away on their Blackberry or playing computer games? Have you noticed that it is almost impossible these days to hold the attention of any young person for five minutes except of course you are telling him of a new cheaper and faster data plan?
Then, there is the question of how I will find the time to write anything in the midst of a crushing schedule. How do I write a column when Tee Mac is busy telling everybody that I am a fraud and the people at BON are looking for my head and every artiste wants more royalties? Pray, how do I write a column when Chinedu Chukwuji, my oga at the top, warns me that I need a break urgently but goes ahead to schedule back to back meetings for me with lawyers and impossible Nigerian broadcasters who look for every reason under the sun to avoid paying small royalty for the music that sustains their operations?
As if I have not stressed my marriage enough - how do I tell madam that with my eyes open, I have added to the wahala she has to tolerate - night after cold nights when at 2 am, 3 am and 4 am, the whole world is sleeping and she tiptoes into the study to find me hunched over, hammering at the keyboard of my long suffering lap top? I still do not understand how I was trapped into saying yes to writing a column for a newspaper when the whole world is writing columns on Facebook. Is it the old school guy in me clinging to a past that is fast disappearing? While a lot of people I know get all their news these days on the Internet, I still read newspapers the old fashioned way. I still cannot manage a day going by without me caressing some newsprint.
You know, I used to read Rueben Abati like mad. Outside of Ray Ekpu and my 'paddy', Sonala Olumhense, I thought Rueben was the real deal. He has style. Till today I cannot figure out how Rueben could give up the best job in the world.
For what?! Seriously, how much does GEJ pay Rueben to write those drab government press releases? It takes a peculiar kind of talent to be a good government propagandist and I do not think that Rueben has that kind of talent. Seriously, there has to be something in the constitution that makes it a capital offence to take a great guy like Ruben Abati and make him the writer of government press releases.
That was the same way I felt when someone whisked Funke Egbemode to Abuja to go and chop with Patricia Ette. When Mrs. Ette got removed as Speaker of the House of Representatives, I celebrated big time, not because I had any issue with the woman or that I thought that the person who would replace her would do better. I celebrated because I knew that Funke would come back to Lagos and write her columns without which my weekend is incomplete. And come back, she did.
You see the trouble with writing a column - I have already started poking my nose into other people's business as if I do not have enough trouble. Very soon, I will be made Chief Launcher at every event in my village. After all they see my face every week in Saturday Independent and that must mean a lot of money. See me wahala!
See you next week!

Original_Search_Title: Kukah, National Question and Interfaith Dialogue
Headline: Nigeria: Kukah, National Question and Interfaith Dialogue
Body_Text:
For our purpose here, we will downplay the political undercurrent of terrorism in the guise of the raging war in the name of God, whatever philosophers would take that to mean as error of logic. Indeed, there is this astounding reflection by Martin Luther King (MLK), Jr. concerning the horizontal and vertical significance of religion.
On the one hand, one of the successes of religion derives from its capacity to open up the mind of men to the beauty and mysteries of the universe and hold them in awe of the transcendent. On the other hand, religion, according to MLK, ought also to achieve a horizontal purpose: 'At the centre of any worthwhile religion therefore is a deep spiritual awareness of our lives as a conduit for the intervention of a transcendent force in the betterment of the world and the lives of fellow humans.'
Unfortunately, one of the sad experiences of our modern existence is that the dynamics of religious affiliation seem to justify Karl Marx's and the modernisation theorist's dark perception of religion than the bright requirement of Martin Luther King. Religion, according to modernisation theorists, is negatively atavistic; and that atavism, for Marx, serves a dark political purpose in the hands of unconscionable political elites. And they both seem to be right. The evidence is the strong wave of religious resurgence all over the world. Unfortunately, this resurgence has nothing to do with horizontal human interest everywhere. Fundamentalist religion is not concerned with aligning transcendence with humanity; the knowledge and worship of transcendence has not awed us into a humble love of fellow humans who are equally lost in the mysteries of the universe. Rather, fundamentalism wants to annihilate humanity to preserve the transcendence. Al-Qaeda and Boko Haram are two recent examples of how bad religion has become.
I suspect that most of us ageing Nigerians came to know religious violence as adults. That is my own experience, and that is why I strongly believe that inter-faith dialogue must ultimately become a solid block within our attempt to build a strong and virile nation. My growing up was almost idyllic. I am a child of what Professor Ali Mazrui refers to as Africa's triple heritage comprising of the Islamic, Judeo-Christian and traditional religious and cultural dynamics all confluencing within one continent. My experience was exciting. I had a Christian grandfather and a Muslim grandmother. So: I went to the church regularly; I visited the mosque even more regularly carrying my grandmother's praying mat; and I participated in the usual cultural festivities of masquerades and the new yam. It was simply impracticable not to eat the delicious Salah meat from my Muslim cousins who were the only brothers and sisters I grew up with! As the two religious faiths got more entrenched within our communal space, adherents began defining their relationship to the traditional beliefs around the imperative of values and culture as the conversation platform that supposedly excluded the "idolatrous" practices defined by traditional rituals.
As a convivial confluence, Aáwé, as I recently expounded, poses a significant challenge to Nigeria's national project. And just as values and culture served as the mitigating framework for tempering volatile religious temperaments, so also must civil secular virtues temper the violence of religious fundamentalism in Nigeria. No one understands this urgent necessity more than Bishop Matthew Hassan Kukah, the charismatic Bishop of the Catholic Diocese of Sokoto. Bishop Kukah is a lone voice crying in the wilderness. And his message is untiring: Repent and be patriotic! He is unique in the sense that he has remained consistently singular in his adroit combination of priestly and civic energies. He is conveniently at home in religious as well as civil society circles. He has no reason at all, like most clergies, to retreat into the obscurity of Episcopal responsibilities; rather, he sees the priesthood as a robust platform to launch the will of God onto the national stage. The Kukah Centre for Faith and Leadership Research is a committed vision of inter-faith dialogue in Nigeria. Founded in 2008, the Centre has three-pronged strategic objectives: (a) to deepen the quality of national discourse on coexistence in Nigeria; (b) to strengthen the capacity of citizens to engage government at various levels and promote inclusive governance processes; and (c) to expand the relationship between faith and public policy.
As he said recently at the Soyinka at 80 lecture, he is always very glad to confound those who consider that he has no business with 'unbelievers'--politicians, Muslims, and atheistic writers. What they superficially fail to see is the underlying energy that drives Rev. Fr. Kukah. Combining priestly and activist functions immediately revealed the liberation theology credentials of this man of God who believes that religion serves a political function of mobilising against injustice anywhere.
In order to justify our faith in the possibilities of democracy, Fr. Kukah seems to be saying, we need to bring the various manifestations of faith to a democratic rapprochement. Democratic excellence does not come easy; as President John Kennedy once remarked 'Democracy is a difficult kind of government. It requires the highest qualities of self-discipline, restraint, a willingness to make commitments and sacrifices for the general interest, and it also requires knowledge.' Democracy can benefit immensely from the spiritual capital embedded in religious energies of the likes of Fr. Kukah. The spiritual should first be charitable; charity considers others first. Shouldn't the spiritual therefore instil a patriotic feeling in religious adherents? What is the point of spirituality and a religious craving for God if it doesn't decree living at peace with all men, as the Scripture prescribes? The Bible indicts our religiosity: If you don't love this fellow Nigerian you can see, how can you love God that you can't see? Patriotic love is the manifestation of spiritual humility.
Kukah took his extended mission from a conviction that religion will not wither away as modernisation theorists posit; yet, it is still violently atavistic. It instinctively fuels hatred rather than love and compassion in us towards fellow citizens, and it challenges our secular foundation. The fundamental question at the heart of the mission of Kukah et al therefore is: Why can't we see eye to eye in the name of the same God? Why does spirituality excite violence rather than compassion?
We can take the interrogation further by asking: In what sense can spirituality ensure that a person becomes a good citizen? In what sense can inter-faith dialogue become possible in spite of the absolute nature of our basic religions? If collective interaction stands at the basis of citizenship, how can inter-faith dialogue provide the framework for such a national conversation that will activate religion as a positive contribution to national development? If we are the sole owner of God, then what relationship can we have with the so-called 'non-God people'? What would be the point of our conversation?
The best argument for inter-faith dialogue in Nigeria is therefore a simple one: We are all Nigerians, and we all rise or fall together in spite of our different religions. The Boko Haram insurgence is actually not favourable to Muslims and Christians. We are all under fundamentalist fire. By this fact, it implies that as Nigerians we all have an overriding interest in speaking to one another in a bid to provide the framework for a viable coexistence. The alternative, as Boko Haram is teaching us presently, is nationally unpalatable. To be truly spiritual, we all have an obligation to reproduce heaven in Nigeria. Heaven is reciprocal affection for the other person as a human creation of a humane God.
It is in that sense that inter-faith dialogue begins from the necessity of spiritualising our existence as Nigerians. This essentially requires that we secularise the religious values central to each religion and transform them into civic virtues. Islam springs from a fundamental 'Submission to the will of Allah,' and Christianity implies the followership of the 'Prince of peace.' The traditional religion prides itself on its non-violent and humanist foundation. All three further pride themselves as the repository of cardinal virtues that make for spirituality--love, compassion, mercy, hospitality, temperance, justice, prudence, diligence, kindness, humility, etc. It is not difficult to see how these virtues can become civic. Civic virtues are those behaviour, temperaments, attitudes and habits that are critical for the survival of any community. They include: trustworthiness, reciprocity, friendliness, politeness, cooperation, political participation, honesty, brotherhood, and so on.
As Rick Warren once noted, 'A truly free society protects all faith, and true faith protects a free society.' I read secularism as a framework that allows for the flourishing of several religious expressions of human beings to coexist with a democratic context. Yet, secularity is not enough for dialogue; the various religions must secularise. This simply implies, without any deep philosophical analysis that the various religious leaders must work assiduously to moderate their theological absolutism in favour of civic peace and national coexistence. If Providence and historical necessity have placed Muslims, Christians and Traditionalists together within the national space we call Nigeria, then it stands to reason that we either move on together or we fall together. One outcome is obviously better than the other.
James Baldwin noted poignantly that 'The future is like heaven--everyone exalts it but no one wants to go there now.' I revel in the optimism that the Nigerian national project is an attempt to create a future that is heavenly for us and future generations of Nigerians. Moving on together to that heavenly future requires an active cultivation of those civic virtues that do not in any way detract from whatsoever way Muslims, Christians and traditionalists conceive of the dynamics of their own heaven. The idea is to begin creating the possibility of heaven in Nigeria first.
If we must achieve unity in diversity, we need to harness our spiritual energies. We need to arrive at the egalitarian conviction that we are all created equal by one God. Heaven is harmony; and I doubt anyone can draw near the Pearly Gates if they fail to live peaceably with others right here and now. Mahatma Gandhi once said, 'God has no religion.' Once we grasp the profundity of that simple statement, we should be right on the way to a national spiritual awareness that can enlarge our sensibility and bring us to a patriotic understanding of our collective responsibility as citizens of this great country and our shared destiny.

Original_Search_Title: GEPA Supports Handicraft Exporter
Headline: Ghana: GEPA Supports Handicraft Exporter
Body_Text:
Ghana has re-entered the US market as at August 2014 by supporting 12 handicraft exporters from three different associations to participate in the Artisan Resource at the New York Craft Exhibition.
This was part of efforts of the Ghana Export Promotion Authority (GEPA) to revive the handicraft sector of the Ghana Export and Promotions Authority.
Chief Executive Officer of Ghana Export Promotion Authority, Mr. Gideon Quarcoo, made this revelation at the 24th National Awards for Exports Achievement, 2013 in Accra.
Mr. Quarcoo said GEPA undertook a number of human resource development activities to improve the services and activities of exporters and trade related organizations to enhance the quality of products suitable for the export market.
He mentioned that two workshops on awareness creation on professional services export were held at Cape Coast and Koforidua with one-hundred and twenty (120) participants from both public and private sectors.
A Niche Cocoa Industry at Tema took the exporter of the year award while the woman exporter of the year went to Margaret Tweneboah- Boateng, Chief Executive Officer of Excel Industries Limited.
The most diversified company award went to Nestle Ghana Limited in the production of assorted processed food; Joekopan enterprise producers of Asian vegetables, Latex Foam Mattress and Nestle also went for the gold award in the exportation
K Last Company Limited, producers of fresh yams, Barry Callebaut Ghana limited producers of cocoa paste and Western Rod and Wire who produce Aluminum rods all went for the silver awards.
The platinum awards went to Interplast Limited, producers of PVC pipes, Tubes and fittings, Tema Steel Company Limited producers of Remelted Scrap Ingots and Kasapreko Company limited.
Source: ISD(Faith Junko-Ogawa & Edem Agblevor)

Original_Search_Title: Why I'm Seeking Re-Election Into Senate -  Ndoma-Egba
Headline: Nigeria: Why I'm Seeking Re-Election Into Senate -  Ndoma-Egba
Body_Text:
Abuja — Senate Leader, Victor Ndoma-Egba, has explained his decision to seek a re-election to represent Cross-River Central in the National Assembly in the 2015 poll.
Ndoma-Egba, who spoke in a media chat with National Assembly newsmen at the weekend, said he was re-contesting his senatorial seat in the next year's election because "it will be in the interest of the polity for us to continue with the stability, and fortunately, I have been part of the leadership that has provided this stability."
He stated that he is equally desirous of building capacity in the institution of the national legislature and therefore want to maximize institutional memory, stressing that, "In any case, and in all humility, we have been doing well".
On the alleged mastermind of an attack on another aspirant contesting his seat in Cross River, the Senate Leader exonerated himself of any complicity in the alleged attack, explaining that, "I was not there when the attack was allegedly carried out by the said people during the new yam festival, which happened to be one of the very few celebrations I have missed in many years.
"I had arrived Abuja from the United States on the evening of the day preceding the purported attack and was fully exhausted such that l couldn't make it to the village.
"The village clan called me on the day of the festival that some youths blocked the road leading to the village and prevented the aspirant, who is currently a member of the House of Representatives.
"I condemned the act of the youths because every Nigerian has freedom of movement and in any case, it's a thing of joy for visitors to celebrate the new yam festival with us.
"I immediately put a call through to few people to persuade the youths to remove the blockage and that was the end of the matter. In any case, the action of the youths, in the first instance, is unfortunate and I strongly condemn it. It is a development that should not happen and should not be encouraged."

Original_Search_Title: Why I Want Ndoma-Egba's Seat - Rep Enoh
Headline: Nigeria: Why I Want Ndoma-Egba's Seat - Rep Enoh
Body_Text:
MR John Owan Enoh is a member of the House of Representatives representing Ikom Federal Constituency in the House of Representatives and also the chairman House Committee on Appropriation. He in this interview explains reasons for his quest for the Cross River Central Senate seat. Excerpts:
What happened recently during a yam festival where it was reported that your group and that of the Senate Leader, Ndoma-Egba were involved in a fracas?
The seat that I want is occupied by the Senate Leader, Senator Victor Ndoma-Egba. I am not just a rookie in politics. In 1999, I got elected into the Cross River State House of Assembly and in 2003, I was elected into the House of Representatives and so, I am actually serving my third term. I believe that more than 90 per cent of the people in my district want me in the Senate.
I live in Ikom and Ikom is the headquarters of the Senatorial district. The current occupant of the Senate seat comes from Ikom local government area. Normally, we celebrate yam festivals every September and because of the positive work I do in my constituency, there is a positive perception across.
This particular yam festival, I got a lot of invitation from many people especially from three different wards, who were celebrating the new yam festival and it included the Senate Leader's ward.
Ordinarily, if I did not go to the ward, it would look like a letdown. It was supposed to be on a Saturday and I arrived Ikom on Friday.
One of my aides had come to inform me that the DPO of Ikom had come to warn me not to go to Akparabo because the security indications were negative. It turned out that the first strategy was to scare me security wise. I told the DPO that I was going to the town.
And so, I went to bed but the first thing I was told in the morning was that the road leading to the town had been blocked. If the road leading to the town gets blocked by aides of the senator, who had boasted that I cannot enter the place, it meant too much to the Senator's aides that I was going there.
Eventually I got in anyway because we used the military and when I got in, it became apparent to me why they did not want me to get in because the jubilation in the community was like a triumphant entry.
We have a good relationship. For the past several years, Ndoma-Egba has been the chairman of the Cross River State National Assembly caucus and I the secretary. In 2011, I resisted to the end, the pressure to run for Senate. My governor comes from our senatorial district. In 2011, when my governor lifted his hand in his (governor's) village, to run for a third term, my governor was booed.
Why?
Because people did not want him (Ndoma-Egba). So, for the three times he has gone to the Senate, it is the governor who has actually insisted that people should support him.
Is the governor disposed to your aspiration?
Well the governor is not running and I am sure it is unusual. In a system where every second term governor wants to return to the Senate, Liyel Imoke decided not to, perhaps, it is also the measure of the character of the person.
He said he has gone to the Senate before but even that is not enough. He has been a governor for two terms and also a senator. He is concerned with the candidate who can win because he believes that is going to make his work less. He is concerned about the re-election of President Goodluck Jonathan and therefore, won't want a situation whereby somebody who runs, is not able to excite the electorate.
He wants someone who the electorate is enthusiastic about. With such enthusiasm, everybody will come out and don't forget that the presidential election is the same day with the National Assembly elections.
So, the governor has said there is going to be a level playing field for who can win to win because he is leaving office, he is not running for any office and so, he needs to give the opportunity to those who can win. The governor appears to be very concerned about his experience in 2011 and he is also concerned about the electability, that is why he is saying so. So, if saying that the governor is insisting that there must be a level playing field, if that amounts to supporting me, I don't know, but that is what it is.

Original_Search_Title: Enoh Is Pushing His Luck Too Far - Ndoma-Egba
Headline: Nigeria: Enoh Is Pushing His Luck Too Far - Ndoma-Egba
Body_Text:
Senate Leader Victor Ndoma-Egba (SAN) in this interview rebuffs claims by Rep. Enoh and makes a strong case for institutional memory in the Senate.Excerpts:
What actually happened during the New Yam festival in your village because it's alleged that you instigated youths to bar him from participating in the ceremony?
Well, I was not there. This is one New Yam festival I missed in many, many years. I wasn't there during the New Yam festival because I had arrived from the United States to Abuja on a Friday evening in the first week of September, so, it was practically impossible for me to go home but I remember getting a call on that morning from the clan head to the effect that there was an issue that some youths blocked the road because the honourable member representing
Etung/Obubra Federal Constituency wanted to go for New Yam in my village and I told them: 'why should that be an issue'? It shouldn't be an issue because, one, it is his right, he has freedom of movement; it's his right to go anywhere he wishes. I asked that the youths be persuaded that the peace was not breached; that every Nigerian has a right of movement and it was his constitutional right.
But having said so, in this our business, being stopped on the way is normal. I remember that I was going to a community in Southern Etung and to stop me, the youths removed the bridge! Yes! To stop me from reaching there, they ripped off the bridge; just to stop me from going. In another community, there was a bonfire across the road. I've been stopped three times: one in Abi Local Government and twice in Etung. It's part of the game. They are seeking your attention and so, you give them that attention and on each occasion, I negotiated with them and they let me through! It's normal in politics. In politics, there are different strategies, different methods.
It's alleged that you are not popular in your senatorial district and that you were booed when Governor Imoke raised your hand in his village in 2011.
There was no such incident. He never raised my hand in Itigidi. It never happened. So, it's a figment of Enoh's political imagination; there was no such incident. And then, when you talk of not being popular, let me just by way of introduction tell you a little about myself.
I became a graduate before I was 21 years, I became a lawyer before I was 22 years, I served on a federal board during the administration of former President Shehu Shagari at the age of 24 years, I built my first house in my home town at the age of 24 years. I became Commissioner in the Old Cross River State which is, today, divided into present Cross River and Akwa Ibom States at the age of 27 years.
I was Commissioner for Works and Transport and that ministry is today, divided into eight ministries. I ran for governor of my state in 1992; I practised law for an unbroken 26 years across Nigeria. If you check the Law Reports, you'll see my cases there; they use my cases to teach students in the Law School. I'm a Senior Advocate of Nigeria, I'm a member of the Body of Benchers.
I was a three-term Chairman of the Nigeria Bar Association, Calabar Branch. That's unprecedented. I was a three-term President of the Calabar Chamber of Commerce. I say this because today, they want to re-write history as if some people made me. I was made long before they all ventured into politics; long before them. I was a successful legal practitioner; known across Nigeria.
Do you see this as a smear campaign?
First, let me say that I regret the tone of Hon. Enoh's interview. The family is a decent, polished, educated family and cultured as well. But, then, you should know that in every situation, there are exceptions, deviants. I think he's just carried away by his luck. He's a young man who's been lucky and he's pushing his luck too far and he shouldn't take God's mercies for granted.
You stand accused of doing things for your people because you saw Enoh doing projects...
(Cuts in) I'll get to that in a bit. As I was saying, I have 39 bills to my credit. I also publish a constituency magazine called The Report which has been published since 2003 and there, I publish my bills, projects, scholarship beneficiaries, with their names.
The only thing that is not in this magazine is charity! I don't dramatise charity because the moment you do that, it's no longer charity. So, if he wants me to dramatise charity... and they accuse me of being stingy, that I'm selfish, well, my driver lives in his own house in Abuja.
Every staff I have owns a plot of land in this same Abuja, paid for by me. A lot of my staff live in their personal houses. I send people to India for treatment. Let him show me just one person whose life he has touched. Must I go and dramatise that? There's an allegation that you began to have problems with the governor when you introduced Goddy Jedy-Agba to President Jonathan as a governorship aspirant in your state.
No such thing happened and I have said it before the president and the national chairman of our party. The president has even confirmed that nothing like that happened. That is one. Two, let us even assume that I did. Is the governorship of Cross River State an appointment?

Original_Search_Title: You Made Kashamu, Ex-Senate President Tells Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: You Made Kashamu, Ex-Senate President Tells Obasanjo
Body_Text:
A former President of the Senate, Senator Ameh Ebute, has faulted the former President Olusegun Obasanjo's claim that he cannot be in the Peoples Democratic Party under the leadership of Chief Buruji Kasahmu as the party leader in the South-West.
Ebute, in an open letter addressed to the former President, copies of which were made available to journalists in Abuja on Monday, accused Obasanjo of double standard.
He said Kashamu was a creation of Obasanjo, who he said was used to cause trouble for the former Governor of Ogun State, Chief Gbenga Daniel, while serving as the governor of the state.
Ebute said it was Kashamu, who he claimed Obasanjo used to obtain a court injunction against the PDP faction led by Daniel, before the national leadership of the party recognised the faction that he said was loyal to the former president.
The former Senate president was reacting to the response made by Obasanjo when the national leadership of the party begged him and asked him to return to the party and lead it.
In his reply, Obasanjo stated that one of the reasons why he was angry with the party was the fact that a certain drug baron was made the leader of the PDP in the South-West geo-political zone.
He had said, "For instance, as a former President of Nigeria, the chairman of West Africa Commission on Drug and a member of Global Commission on Drug, I cannot accept that the zonal leader of my political party and, worse still in my zone, will be an indicted drug baron wanted in America.
"How do I explain that to friends outside Nigeria? This is only one of the many issues that I have pointed and still pointing out. I have national and international standard to maintain and reputation to keep and sustain."
In his letter, Ebute said without Obasanjo, maybe no one would have known Kashamu, adding that it would be wrong for the former president to turn around and be criticising Kashamu.
He said, "I wish to recall, sir that when the former Governor of Ogun State, Otunba Gbenga Daniel, seized the PDP structure from you in 2008/2009, it was this same 'drug baron' whom you aligned with to create parallel party structure and institute legal action against the party.
"It was this same man, who secured the court judgment that eventually got your faction of the PDP recognised by the national leadership ahead of the 2011 general elections.
"Your daughter, then Senator Iyabo Obasanjo, worked closely with this same Kashamu to protect and defend your sagging and diminishing political interest and relevance in your local turf in the state.
"This was a period when Buruji's frequent visit to your Abeokuta Hill top residence was always greeted with celebrative feasting on bowls of pounded yam and egusi soup.
"The question is what were you telling your 'foreign friends' about this man then?"

Original_Search_Title: Fiiro Seeks Removal of Impediments to MSMEs' Growth
Headline: Nigeria: Fiiro Seeks Removal of Impediments to MSMEs' Growth
Body_Text:
Lagos — Director General, Federal Institute of Industrial Research, Oshodi (FIIRO), Gloria Elemo, has said Micro Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) have failed to assume their rightful place despite the resources by government due to some inhibitors.
Speaking on the theme, "Sustainable Job Creation through FIIRO Technologies" at the Investment Day of the FIIRO organised Technology Week; the DG stated that emphasis must be placed on the MSMEs in order to harness Nigeria's sizeable domestic market.
She added proper harnessing of the abundant resources will ensure steady supply of products vital to the nation and economy as a whole.
Advocating for full implementation of all programmes and policies evolved, with respect to MSMEs development, she said, "Bodies or agencies, especially financial institutions, among others, that government put in place to support and respond to the needs of MSMEs to drive the growth band development process of her economy, must also be fully involved".
Emphasising the role of MSMEs as the engine room for industrial growth and sustenance of any economy, Elemo said FIIRO has continue to blaze the trail in the promotion of its research results and transferring same to the investing public.
She stressed that synergy is required to create wider awareness for existing and would-be micro, small and medium entrepreneurs (MSMEs) through unveiling investment opportunities and services available at FIIRO.
"I strongly believe that that our working together will produce useful results, thereby enabling SMEs to bring their potentials to the fore, and manifest their abilities to generate sustainable supply of products in abundance and offer services to the people of Nigeria, and raw materials to industry", she added.
Chairman of the occasion and Director General, National Office for Technology Acquisition and Promotion (NOTAP), Umar Bindir, charged stakeholders, through the MSMEs value chain, to internalise research and development in order to create Nigeria's voice in the committee of nations.
He lamented that many of technologies like the hydra-form, yam flower, among others, that are inventions from Nigeria, have now become international products due to lack of necessary push.

Original_Search_Title: Obosi Community Laments Bad Roads
Headline: Nigeria: Obosi Community Laments Bad Roads
Body_Text:
Onitsha — The residents of Odume Layout, Obosi, in Idemili North Local Government Area are lamenting the dilapidation of three major roads in the area, Anyeji, Vandaz and Anyeji-Omenwa, and have under the aegis of Obidike Central Forum, led by Chief Sunday Nriagu, called on Governor Willie Obiano to come to their aid even as they celebrated maiden new yam festival in tears just as they appealed to Governor Willie Obiano, urging him to come to their rescue.
Making the urge with tears during the celebration held at the play ground there, Nriagu who was flanked by the patron of the forum, Chief Ikechukwu Okoye, and two other members, Walter Okafor and Dr. Chukwuemeka Donald Nwacha, regretted that the poor state of the roads hindered their guests from attending the new yam festival.
According to Nriagu, "We voted en mass for Governor Willie Obiano during the governorship election and he should reciprocate as one good turn deserves another.
"The 2015 general election is around the corner and it is APGA that we know. The governor should please save us from this bad road which has resulted in our being marginalized as visitors and road users no longer come to our area.
"This new yam festival binds us together as philanthropists living together and we hope to visit the less privileged by December but the problem is that we are not being encouraged by the government we voted for.
"We have other teething problems like lack of water and light; some times they say load shading, the worst is the poor state of these three major roads, the government should help us, please", he pleaded. In their various remarks, the patron, Okoye, Okafor, and Dr. Nwacha, recalled that the governor is working on the Peoples Club road presently and urged him to extend the Olive branch to the residents of Obidike Central Forum by working also on the said three roads.

Original_Search_Title: Christine Osifuye - With Love From South Africa
Headline: Nigeria: Christine Osifuye - With Love From South Africa
Body_Text:
Christine Osifuye is a Nigerian gospel artiste based in South Africa. She was recently in Nigeria for a concert when ESSENTIALS caught up with her. Here, she chronicles her journey into and through the world of singing, how it has changed her life in the 'Rainbow Country' and the need for Nigerian singers to more intelligent.
Can you put South African gospel music and the Nigerian variety side-by-side?
That is a little tricky, because there is a standard market for the South African gospel music. There is a market for the gospel music and another for the circular music. We don't have that here in Nigeria. Here, everyone hustles to get it out there - that's not to say you don't hustle over there, as well, but at a point, the burden of selling yourself is taken off you.
Over there, there are studios where you can't record circular music, just the gospel genre. You have marketers, promoters and others who just specialise and focus on gospel music. They don't criss-cross. It helps the market to stabilise and gives it an identity.
Before the release of your albums, didn't you release any singles?
I really do not release single before the album. Prior to the first album, I did some collaborations with some artistes, but those works did not feature on the album.
There is this Nigeria Gospel singer who is really making waves in South Africa - Uche, Yes, that's his name. True, it may not have been easy for him, but he and many other must have broken some boundaries to go that far. Why do you think foreigners don't easily break into our own music market?
Actually, as much as I'll love to, I have never met Uche. Then, as easy as it seems, I do not think that the South African gospel music market is an all-comers affair. Why it may seem that way to us on this side may be because they have a lot of media houses which make it easy for one to be out there, whether you are a foreigner or not. I think that's a plus.
Uche collaborated with the choir he was with at that time and that is one of South Africa's biggest choirs. The exposure did him a lot of good. For other foreigners who are trying to break in, it has not been easy, because it is kind of closed; though they are open to people.
I think it is more difficult over here, because though the Nigerian market is more receptive, you may not understand the language in which they may sing. Nigeria is truly diverse - you have to travel a lot to understand how disverse Nigeria is - and though you may not understand all the languages, you have to learn a lot of songs in these languages to reach the people. So, the workload that comes with pleasing the various Nigerian languages might just be a put-off for most foreign artistes in Nigeria.
When you watch One Gospel, most of the songs are South African; the videos are crisp and recorded during concerts. How do you feel, as a Nigerian? Do you think it's the packaging or the cost of production that is the limitation in the Nigerian sense?
That is part of the structuring. A haphazard, all-comers market cannot do that for you. Also, the government makes periodic investments in the industry to support the artistes and the people understand the role the genre plays in their lives and their spiritual being.
If, however, you have to do that yourself; provide your lighting, the stage, the sound system and everything that comes with it, you may be staring bankruptcy in the face and that can be frustrating for anyone, not just a singer.
Also, over there, they do not believe that gospel music concerts should not be paid for, because it is gospel. No. If it is worth the while, then it is worth paying for. They don't have to know you; all you have to do is to be good and know your stuff as it relates to the environment over there. In stark contrast, over here, when people hear that it is gospel, they say 'ahn, ahn, it's supposed to be free na'. Most times, the church that invited the artiste is the only one paying; the audience does not.
Then, there is the issue of sponsoring. Both private individuals, churches and the government throw in some amount of money to ensure that growth is recorded and the gospel artistes are encouraged. Our understanding over here is different. The bottom line why the investment in gospel music is poor here is because it is deemed 'unprofitable'.
Why do you think most artistes fail?
I'll speak as a gospel artiste. In a place like this, why most gospel artistes fail is because they expect too much from their pastors, congregation, mentors and their environment. Also, they are unsure of their identity. This identity issue has caused many to remain on the border of both genres while most veer off into commercial music because they discover that gospel music is a call; not an all-comers affair.
Most times when I begin to expect too much, I caution myself by saying 'I am not on this platform because of a pastor. I am doing for God and myself. When you work with that understanding, you don't expect the millions to start falling your way. You only consider it a blessing when it comes. But wise ministers consider the gospel artiste as the John the Baptist of every programme, that is why they cannot do without them in their programmes.They know that some good, directional music can make a huge difference before the actual word comes. In the end, one's story cannot be told without the other. If a preacher understands that 'in building others, I build myself', then he will have no fear of the gospel artiste.
For the singer, being a gospel artiste is not a one-off thing; you need to be intelligent and think of how to push yourself to get what you want. You song-writing and message carriage should be approached intelligently
Where did you study engineering?
I did my first degree at the Federal University of Technology Minna and my second degree at a federal university of technology in Pretoria.
What else do you do, besides singing?
I also act in movies and soaps. At some time, I was even a presenter. In a nutshell, I think I am just drawn to the entertainment world like a moth to fire.
When did you begin to think about singing?
When I discovered that I had a lot of passion for it and that goes way, way back. I'm stuck to it and I will do for as long as I live.
I started out singing in the church choir as a toddler, then I progressed to doing choreography nationally (still in church). When I was an undergraduate in FUT Minna, I was the leader of a 300-man mass choir. After then, I knew the die was cast. Look at me now.
Music is my way of reaching people I do not know. It is my way of uplifting people and getting them to that place where mere words cannot take their spirit. As far as I'm concerned, I've been called to do this and it makes a lot of sense to me.
My childhood and up-bringing as a pastor's child affected my singing quite positively. If my parents had been different, I probably will be singing but not with the same degree of satisfaction and, maybe, not gospel music.
Now that you've become an entertainer, when do you hope to return to engineering?
Though music is my first love, I have not abandoned engineering altogether. I just finished my master's degree programme. It felt good going back to engineering, because engineering is fun.
Tell us about Graced.
Graced is, actually, a 12-track album with a bonus track (the 13th one). Graced is a fun album, an upgrade on what I did previously. There are a couple of collaborative efforts in it with some Nigerian artistes based in South Africa.
Grace is a dynamic work which does not just imbue a particular style. It is made up of a number of components; Chado, R& B, Jazz, Negro Spiritual, Raggae and a number of other styles therein. Whatever your music preference, there is something for you in Graced.
The idea behind it is to tell people that, as much as they may not believe it, they have been graced with grace for all situations and for all things.
Does this translate to the fact that you don't have a particular genre?
Perfect. That is it. I always want to do something for everyone. No preference.
Which gospel singers will you love to work with, home and abroad?
Home... that's difficult, but I can take Lara George and Kore; they are talented, intelligent singers. I love our music and all that, but I think some people need to be more intelligent. Abroad, Yolanda Adams is good for me. It's not as if there's someone I want to pattern my style after. No.
As a Nigerian singer in South Africa, how has the reception been?
It has been great. Sometimes, I feel that they are even more receptive than Nigerians. People have been supportive; buying the album, granting interviews and all that. We want people to buy the album first before pushing it onto the Internet.
But pirates rule supreme everywhere...
Over there, people hide to do stuff like that, unlike here where it is brazen and open.
Trivia
Oshifuye has just released her second album titled Graced. It was released on August 23,2014, in Pretoria, South Africa.
Do you miss spinsterhood?
I'm married with two kids... I don't; not wholeheartedly.
What did you find so fulfilling about music which your course of study did not give you?
I'm an chemical engineer. Music is my passion and it is the most ready form of expression. It is a pure form of art which no one teaches you about. Engineering, on the other hand, is a learned thing which has got its own perks as well.
What is the title of your first album?
Face to Face and it has five songs.
Abuja Pretoria; which do you prefer?
I'll pick Pretoria because that is where my family and I call home for now and where my husband and children are is home. Johannesburg is always compared to Lagos while Pretoria is always compared to Abuja, but as sleepy as people think Abuja is, Pretoria is more laid-back, despite the crime-rate. The presence of every form of infrastructure also makes a huge difference.
What do you miss most about home?
Food. Nigerian food. All of them.Yes, we have got a rich culture and this gives us lots of options food-wise and these are the things I miss. I could go to a place and desire to eat this and be at some other place tomorrow and decide to have something else. That is great, but you never know it until you go far from home. I've been in South Africa for four years and that choice does not exist.
You are saying you have not had time to fully acculturate?
No, it's not even about that. You can't make choices because there are no varieties; and you know variety is the spice of life. Over there, the meals revolve around chips, rice and pap (not the pap we know here) - that's just about it.
So, will you go for 'semo' with a soup of your choice or the pap?
'Semo' and any of ogbono, vegetable or fish soup will do. But I love ogbono soup to a fault. Then, I love pounded yam and banga soup. That is blissful and correct. See the goodness of having variety now?
What's your priority; passion, respect, work, God or family?
God, family, respect, passion and work, all in that order.
Fred Hammond, Curt Carr, Don Moen or Cece Winans?
Fred Hammond and Curt Carr, any day. Though I love all four of them, these two are so vast that anyone who listens to their albums comes away with something. They are also wonderful, sensible song writers.

Original_Search_Title: Adwoa Safo Provides Mechanized Boreholes for Constituents
Headline: Ghana: Adwoa Safo Provides Mechanized Boreholes for Constituents
Body_Text:
The Member of Parliament for Dome-Kwabenya, Sarah Adwoa Safo, has handed over five fully operationalised mechanised boreholes to some identifiable groups and institutions within her constituency to end their difficulties in searching for potable water for domestic use.
Among the groups and institutions are the Old Ashongman D/A Primary School, Yam Market, Taifa Community Clinic, Agbogba Anglican Basic School and Dome Mosque.
Neighborhoods within which these groups and institutions are located are areas where cleanliness is an issue.
Communities in the Dome-Kwabenya constituency have over the years faced lots of challenges in getting portable water for domestic use as pipe-borne water running through the area are unable to supply the needed quantity to the residents, living majority of the population to wander about in search of the commodity.
Residents not connected to the community water system had to walk an hour distance to the nearest neighborhood where the commodity is available to buy. A bucket of water in such communities sells between Gp70 and Gp80.
The situation even becomes worse during the dry season where a bucket of water is sold for GH¢1.
The law maker in an interaction with journalists said her motivation in dolling out the mechanised boreholes was to ensure cleanliness in her constituency, especially, where water was very scarce.
"This is to ensure that my people, especially, the children are clean, they have good drinking water and are saved from this Ebola and Cholera epidemic.
"Children are my priority - their education, their health and their livelihood. I believe the facilities are going to help the schools and entire community because they wouldn't have to buy water anymore", said Ms. Safo in a cheerful mood.
She said five other neighbourhoods and institutions were also in the pipeline to benefit from the mechanised borehole initiative. Each of the facility which comes with a polytank reservoir, the MP noted, cost GH¢25,000.
Commenting on the initiative, a Headteacher of the Old Ashongman D/A Primary School, Vinolia Bediako Madogo, said the facility had come at the right time to save the school from the difficulties they go through when the commodity was in short supply.
She said the school had over the years been dependent on irregular water supply from the Municipal Assembly at an average cost of GH¢40 per month to serve over 900 pupils.
However, she argued that the gesture from the MP for the area would help save them some money and also improve on the sanitation situation in the area. "This is a blessing," noted Madam Madogo.
A class four pupil of the Old Ashongman D/A Primary School, Hannah Abedu Fosu expressed her profound gratitude to Ms. Safo for her kind gesture noting "the borehole will give us potable water to drink and help us not to get cholera and Ebola."
Headteacher of the Agbogba Anglican Basic School, Mrs. Joana Adwoa Brukum, also commenting on the initiative lauded the MP for bringing life to the school.
"Water is life and he who brings water brings life. This borehole donated will go a long way to relieve us of the difficulties we encounter in getting water on daily basis. It will also improve on our sanitation issues in the school, especially, with the outbreak of cholera.
"We promise to take good care of the facility and use it for the purpose that it has been given", she noted.
Leaders of the Dome Market, Taifa Community Clinic and the Yam Market all welcomed the gesture done them by the MP and promised to take good care of the facility.

Original_Search_Title: Ndigbo - Celebrating New Yam With Glamour in Ibadan
Headline: Nigeria: Ndigbo - Celebrating New Yam With Glamour in Ibadan
Body_Text:
The Ezendigbo of Ibadanland and Oyo State, Eze (Dr.) Alex C. Anozie, and the Oyo State branch of the Ohaneze Ndigbo last weekend hosted hundreds of members of the Igbo community in the state as the 2014 New Yam Festival was celebrated in grand style.
During the colourful event that took place at the Ososami, Ibadan palace, of the Ezendigbo of Ibadan and Oyo State, the paramount head of the Oyo Igbo community prayed for long life, good health and progress of Ndigbo and all other Nigerians living in the state.
Eze Anozie also used the opportunity to pray for Nigeria as the country marked her 54th independence anniversary, and as the 2015 general elections draw nearer. The paramount head of the Oyo Igbo community specifically called on Nigerian politicians to play politics without bitterness, and to avoid any act of violence, no matter the provocation.
He admonished the Federal Government and state governments to ensure that Nigerians of different ethnic and religious groups live peacefully in any part of the country they find themselves.
Eze Anozie also called on the government to ensure that all Nigerians enjoy dividends of democracy, "for us to see the country as belonging to all of us."
He, however, used the medium to commend President Goodluck Jonathan for the "progress that is beginning to show face in every area of the country."
Eze Anozie congratulated and commended the President on the award given to the man who designed the Nigerian national flag, Pa Taiwo Akinkunmi, and the way the Jonathan administration revived the life of the once uncelebrated hero by placing him on reasonable salary. He maintained that by extending the national awards to Nigerians of lower class, President Jonathan has won the heart of many Nigerians, including himself (Anozie).
Anozie also commended Governor Abiola Ajimobi of Oyo State for peace that has continued to reign in the state since 2011 when the governor assumed the office.
He specifically commended Governor Ajimobi for the massive infrastructure development that is being witnessed in every nook and cranny of the state.
The Oyo Igbo leader also called on the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to do everything possible to ensure that all adult Nigerians are encouraged to get their Permanent Voters Card (PVC) before the 2015 general elections.
According to him, this is the only way to ensure that millions of eligible voters across the country are not disenfranchised in next year's elections.
His words: "If it means extending the registration period, that should be allowed in order to see that no one is disenfranchised during the elections. Some people are complaining that there was no registration exercise in their areas, others said there were not INEC officials in their areas, others said there were not registration materials in their areas. All these should be looked into and addressed. INEC should please do something about all these without delay."
The Ezeigbo of Oyo State urged all Nigerians, including the Federal Government, INEC and politicians to make sure that the prediction by the United States of America that Nigeria may not exist beyond 2015 to come into reality.
According to him, this can only be achieved if the 2015 general elections are free and fair and conducted peacefully to the satisfaction of all and sundry.
"Losers must be ready to accept defeat as long as the elections are free and fair, while winners must try and be magnanimous in victory. If Nigeria breaks up, the whole of Africa will be in serious trouble and the impact will be negatively felt all over the world", he added.
Members of the Oyo Igbo community, in their hundreds, attended the New Yam Festival to show their love, respect and support to Anozie, who in turn ensured that everybody present at the occasion enjoyed himself or herself.

Original_Search_Title: My Mission Is to Aid the Masses - Alao-Akala
Headline: Nigeria: My Mission Is to Aid the Masses - Alao-Akala
Body_Text:
Ibadan — Former governor of Oyo State, Adebayo Alao-Akala, has said his second time coming to govern the state as from 2015 "is to aid the suffering masses from all the nooks and crannies of the state.
"By this, I mean the common man on the streets of Ibarapa, the yam seller from Ogbomoso, the market woman
in Oyo, the displaced traders in Oje market who are the worst hit by the anti-people policies of the All Progressives Congress (APC) led government in Oyo State."
He spoke at the commissioning of the Operation van and Operations' Office of 'Team Akala', an arm of the campaign organization of the 2015 governorship project of the former governor.
Alao-Akala recalled that the last three and a half years in Oyo State had been that of serious pain and anguish in the lives of most of the people.
"So many lives have been lost, others are jobless, while a whole lot have no means of livelihood.
"This is a clear indication that the present administration has failed the people. The big challenge for us as an incoming administration come 2015 by the grace of God is to make our number one priority the mission to restore that lost confidence", he added.
Represented by his wife, Oluwakemi, at the event held at the office situated on Oyo road, Mokola, Ibadan, Alao-Akala said the epoch-making event "is another clear indication that our coming back is indeed an 'Amazing Restoration'.
"This is so because, this is the first time ever in the history of Oyo State that a team of professionals will publicly come together to throw their support behind the candidature of an aspiring gubernatorial aspirant and work conscientiously towards the realization of such goal", he said.
In his address, the Coordinator of 'Team Akala', Niran Adeyoju, said the team was conceived to provide a unique platform to actualize and evolve a frame work through which "we shall all lift Oyo State to greater and glorious height".
He noted that there had been clarion calls by the people of Oyo State from diverse backgrounds including artisans, market women, students, professionals, Okada riders among others on Alao-Akala to stage a come back.
The coordinator recalled that the former governor had proved to be a man of the people, a compassionate politician, grassroots mobilizer, who made tremendous impact in the lives of the people of Oyo State.
This, he did while in office between 2007 and 2011 which witnessed tremendous development of both physical and mind through eradication of poverty and as an administration that created an enabling environment for commerce, that engaged local contractors, who in turn patronized residents of the state, leading to a buoyant economy.

Original_Search_Title: Onitsha Tanker Fire Kills Pregnant Woman, Three Others, Injures 20
Headline: Nigeria: Onitsha Tanker Fire Kills Pregnant Woman, Three Others, Injures 20
Body_Text:
No fewer than four persons, including a pregnant woman, were feared dead yesterday, while 20 others sustained various degrees of injuries as a gasoline-laden tanker somersaulted and exploded on the New Auto Spare Parts Market Flyover, Nkpor, near Onitsha, Anambra State.
The accident, which occurred in the afternoon as a result of what eyewitnesses said was brake failure and loss of control on the part of the driver, also wiped out thousands of livestock, tubers of yam, second hand clothing and other items worth millions of naira as some quantities of gasoline (diesel) flowed down to the livestock and yam section of the nearby Nkpor Main Market.
An eyewitness said by the time men of the state fire service arrived the scene, the tanker, birds in the livestock market and yams had been burnt.
According to the witness, the fire fighters prevented the fire from extending to more kiosks housing livestock, yams and clothing materials.
At press time yesterday, the actual estimate of the livestock, yam tubers, clothes and sweet potatoes roasted by the fire could not be ascertained as the owners were still lamenting their misfortune, even as the fire fighters were still pumping water into the burning market.
The driver of the burning tanker could not be located among the crowd that thronged the scene to assist in putting out the fire and rushing the injured victims to a nearby hospital for medical attention.
Mobilisation of agencies
Men of the Federal Road Safety Commission, FRSC, arrived the scene later, while officials of Anambra State Traffic Agency, ASTA, police and Nigerian Security and Civil Defence Corp, NSCDC, were seen trying to divert vehicles from the accident lane to the other lane of the expressway.
This resulted in heavy traffic from Nkpor to Onitsha and Nkpor to Ogidi.
The Director of Operations of the fire service, Ikechukwu Nzegwu, told newsmen that some people had been rushed to hospitals for medical attention, but the Divisional Police Officer, DPO, at Ogidi, Mr. Musa, who also confirmed the incident, said he was not yet aware of any casualty.
He added that those who sustained injuries had been rushed to Onitsha and St. Charles Borromeo hospitals for medical attention.

Original_Search_Title: Asogli Celebrates Yam Festival
Headline: Ghana: Asogli Celebrates Yam Festival
Body_Text:
The Vice President, Mr. Kwesi Amissah-Arthur, has called on traditional authorities to re-focus on the relevance of traditional festivals and stop using such platforms for merry making only.
To him, festivals should be used to create opportunities that would help the people to improve upon their living standards.
It must also be used in mobilizing both human and material resources that would facilitate development, as well as showcasing the rich cultural practices and values that distinguished them as citizens of Ghana.
The Vice President, who was addressing a grand durbar of chiefs and people of Asogli to climax 30 days of activities marking this year's Yam Festival in Ho, commended Togbe Afede XIV for his leadership qualities, which he noted had helped in promoting peace and stability in the traditional area.
Mr. Amissah-Arthur announced that government had released funds for the continuation of the eastern corridor roads and other projects, and assured the people that government would not fail the region in terms of development.
The Vice President told the chiefs and the people that the desire to develop the region motivated the government to establish a state university in Ho. According to him, structures were being provided at the permanent site of the university to befit its status.
Mr. Amissah-Arthur ceased the opportunity to educate the people on the deadly Ebola disease and cholera, stressing that Ebola posed a danger to the very existence of the nations affected in Africa. He advised Ghanaians to be very vigilant and accept professional advice from health authorities.
The Paramount Chief of Asogli traditional Area, who is also the President of the Volta Region House of Chiefs, Togbe Afede XIV, stressing on the theme of the festival: "Let's make our nation great and strong", pointed out the need for the citizens to show interest in government activities, be aware of detailed development plans and demand for accountability.
Togbe Afede advised citizens of Ghana to realize the fact that development was a collective responsibility of all. To him, people should not always be demanding their rights and privileges as citizens only, but their duties and responsibilities towards the development process of the nation.
He noted that the increasing spate of strikes would not promote national development and that the practice of paying people while they were on strike creates a win-win situation that emboldens them to carry on with the strikes.
Togbe Afede observed that inflammatory, divisive, and hate-filled ethnic biased speeches were harmful to the peace and development of the country, stressing that in order to build a strong nation, there was the need to tolerate each other and appreciate as well as accept our diversity.
"I wish to remind all of us about our pledge to the motherland, our pledge to be faithful and loyal, and our pledge to serve the motherland with our strength and with all our hearts. I believe that together, and in unity, we can make our nation great and strong", he stressed.
The King of the Ewes in the Republic of Togo, Togbe Agorkorli, who chaired the function, called for unity among the Ewes in Ghana, Togo, Benin, Nigeria and Cameron, and advocated for a common festival for all the Ewe nations as a way of deepening friendship in the respective nations.
Present at the function were the President of the Central Region House of Chiefs, Nana Kwebu Ewusi, the Kayoro Pio, Oscar Tiyiamu in the Upper East region, the Minister for Chieftaincy, Dr. Henry Seidu Dana, Deputy Minister of Defence, Mr. Alex Segbfia, and the Ho Municipal Chief Executive, Mrs. Fafa Adinyirah, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Nwagwu - Don Alvaro: Mansion of Humility
Headline: Nigeria: Nwagwu - Don Alvaro: Mansion of Humility
Body_Text:
ON September 27, 2014, Bishop Álvaro del Portillo - familiarly called Don Álvaro - will be beatified in Madrid (Spain), his hometown. He was the Prelate of Opus Dei (1982-1994), first successor of the Founder, St. Josemaría Escrivá, at the head of that institution of the Catholic Church.
The question is often asked, why we need saints, more so in this year of the three Popes, St John XXXIII, St John Paul II, and to-be-beatified Paul VI. Interposed in their midst is amiable, serene, humble, and perspicacious Don Álvaro. Yes, why do we need saints? Shakespeare tells us in Julius Caesar, 'the evil that men do lives after them; the good is oft interred with their bones.' And many of us spend their life trying to reverse this order. Well, you need not worry even for a minute: if you live a saintly life, recognized by all who knew you, the good you did will be announced and celebrated all over the world when you are canonized a saint. You will be presented as a model for all persons; the whole world will be invited to come to you, to know you, to seek your help.
Don Álvaro incarnated perfectly and faithfully transmitted the spirit of Opus Dei. A child from a good Christian home, he grew up loving the world of learning, sports and had a healthy interest in all that was good and worthy - reading, drawing, the outdoors, animals, paintings, art. In all these, he was known for his simplicity and extraordinary humility, which reigned supreme in his character all his life. Talented and astute, with a loving sense of humour, he started secondary school in 1924 and finished in 1931. What did he really want to be when he grew up, a bullfighter or a bishop! But when one day he saw the body of the archbishop of Burgos being carried with a military escort, and now realizing that bishops also die, bullfighting was his final choice.
All this changed in time, as they often do in the lives of many children: after secondary school Don Alvaro decided he would be an engineer with a strong interest in civil engineering. His keenness took him far in the discipline and it was within and through this course of study - and also due to his natural affinity to the care of others - that he discovered Opus Dei. Don Alvaro attended one of St Josemaría's talks, then another on the same day, on love of God and love of Our Lady. And listening to the supernatural promptings of his soul, he immediately and resolutely saw his vocation: on July 7, 1935, he became a celibate lay faithful of Opus Dei.
St. Josemaría beamed the lights of the Holy Spirit on Don Álvaro when in1940 he invited him to be a priest. But please bear in mind that Don Álvaro at this time was a fulfilled lay man, loving his work, actively practising his profession as an engineer. In fact, the Bishop of Madrid, who would later ordain him would say, 'Alvaro, do you realize that this means you are going to lose your personality? Now you are a prestigious engineer, but afterwards you'll be just one more priest!' 'Your Excellency', Don Álvaro replied, 'I gave up my personality to Jesus Christ a long time ago'. He carried on living the ordinary life with its hustle and bustle, while receiving formation for the priesthood and, along with two others, he was ordained as priest on June 25, 1943.
From the time he was named Secretary General of Opus Dei in 1940, Don Álvaro would be the pillar of support and strength for the founder. St. Josemaría was chosen by God to raise ordinariness to extraordinary holy and saintly heights in the humdrum of life. Don Alvaro would thus be in all its fullness the masterpiece of Opus Dei, the apostle who takes the message of Opus Dei to the hearing of the dust and sands of human ordinariness. I would say that whatever passed on in the mind of St. Josemaría would flow in the heart of his son, Don Álvaro, and whatever passed on in the mind of Don Álvaro, would strike at the heart of his Father, St. Josemaría.
On moving to live in Rome with St Josemaría, Don Álvaro would be involved in various tasks for the Holy See, tasks that intimately involved all three Popes, St. John XXIII and St. John Paul II, and Pope Paul VI, who would be beatified on October 19. His principal tasks in the Church are best known for his immersion in the Second Vatican Council convoked by St. John XXIII. In particular, on November 8, 1962, he was appointed Secretary of the Commission for the Discipline of the Clergy and Christian People that was charged to synthesise into a single decree what would become Presbyterorum Ordinis. To some extent it was Don Alvaro's decision that a text be drafted. He argued so persuasively that the priesthood was so important in the Church that it well deserved a decree of its own... The drafting of the decree was very hard work, especially because of the tension there was at the time over the issue of priestly celibacy. That conflict, in fact, got so bad that Pope Paul VI himself had to intervene. It discussed the presence of the priest in the world, and why he needed a good formation in the basic human virtues in order to serve the men and women of his time.
Don Alvaro came to Nigeria on November 8, 1989 and departed on November 20, 12 days later. From Lagos, he went to Ijebu-Ode, Ibadan, Enugu. Our daughter, Onyema Eseka, had been ill from a carcinoma and from Ash Wednesday of 2006 until her death, we had been praying through Don Álvaro for her total cure. A member of Opus Dei, who had given us this prayer card told us, 'the miracle would come through Don Álvaro'. We prayed accordingly and had absolute hope she would be well. In fact, when on a certain night her case had taken a course for the worse, we included this aspiration at the end of each decade of the Rosary: 'Onyema is going to be well we know, Onyema is going to be well we pray'. And our faith was rock solid that one day, she would just get up and walk out of her bed. Onyema died in the early hours of November 20, 2006, 17 years to the day Don Álvaro left Nigeria. She went into hospital on November 8 and was there for the entire period Don Alvaro had been in Nigeria. We believe he had taken Onyema with him on the anniversary of his visit and we believe Onyema is well as we had prayed.
Don Alvaro celebrated his last Mass on the last day of his visit to the Holy Land on March 22, 1994, at the Cenacle, the site where Our Lord is believed to have held the Last Supper. He died the following day, March 23, in Rome. He had a tremendous affection for His Holiness John Paul II, an affection that dates back to their days at Vatican II. His Holiness always responded and had a number of audiences with his dear friend. St John Paul II could not have shown this unique oneness any better than his deep grief at the death of his friend. At 6.15 p.m., on the afternoon of Don Álvaro's death, John Paul II arrived at Opus Dei headquarters in Rome to pay his last respects to his dear friend. As he entered he said, "Sia Iodato Gesu Cristo! (Praised be Jesus Christ). He then knelt down with his head deeply bowed down in prayer, his hand cupped over his face.
My dear wife's birthday is on March 22, the date Don Alvaro said his last Mass in the Church of the Cenacle in Holy Land. This year, 2014, the Mass for the centennial of Don Álvaro's birth was said on March 22. My dear wife, Helen, and I were at Mass at Whitesands Secondary School, Lekki, Lagos for this magnificent celebration. Before this date, we had received what we believe is a favour from Don Álvaro. Helen was healed of radiculopathy that had made walking extremely painful following an accidental fall at home. After saying the Don Alvaro prayer card for five days she just started walking without any pain!
Don Álvaro was a man of enormous fortitude, our Founder called him Saxum (rock). Unflappable, solid, serene, warm and friendly, compassionate and demanding, Don Álvaro was ever by the side of his Father like a devoted, faithful son. A man of immense love for the ordinary life, he makes you want to invite him home to a meal of pounded yam and egusi soup; for me, it would be Owerri soup (Ofe Owerre) garnished with robust stock fish and dry fish. Oh, I would like to have him home for a meal. He does not drink wine, so I will not serve him one: I shall just dream we shared a great La Malkerida Blobal.
- Professor Nwagwu is of Paul University, Awka, Anambra State.

Original_Search_Title: Edozien Confers Chieftaincy Titles On Dignitaries At 'Iwa Ji'
Headline: Nigeria: Edozien Confers Chieftaincy Titles On Dignitaries At 'Iwa Ji'
Body_Text:
Asaba — THE Asagba of Asaba, His Royal Majesty, Obi (Prof.) Chike Edozien, has bestowed traditional chieftaincy titles on some dignitaries, captains of industries and top government officials.
The conferment occured during the 2014 Iwa-ji New Yam Festival involving the symbolic cutting of the new yam where residents thank God that they tilled the ground and it yielded its fruits in abundance for them this year.
The highly elated Asagba, who spoke from the 'throne of grace', said the festival was a platform to appreciate Almighty God, who is the creator, ultimate provider, protector and man's (in the generic sense) final arbiter, for letting the ground to yield its fruits.
Secondly, according to him, the occasion was to "enjoy the fruits of our labour and take stock of the progress made so far in the year under review, in the areas of peace and security, human-capital and infrastructural development."
He listed the budding Asaba International Airport, one of the star projects of Governor Emmanuel Uduaghan's administration, the prevailing peace and security enforced by the out gone Commissioner of Police in the state, Ayo Ikechukwu Aduba.

Original_Search_Title: Another Game Without Rules
Headline: Nigeria: Another Game Without Rules
Body_Text:
The game is up. We are midway into the climax. But there appears to be no rules for the game. Yet the players are jostling for action and attention. What manner of game is this? Is it another game of blame? What evidence do we have on ground to prove that this game is free from equivocation and compromise? Are the players out to play yet another terrible joke on the collective destiny of the innocent spectators of this game?
Now, let us talk about the game itself. If the stage is set, as long as the defending champion is still defending his adorable trophy, and as far as his fellow competitors are willing to play, let us talk about the rules of the game in question. Let us talk about the referee of the game, his background, his preparedness and his shortsightedness being our departure points. Let us talk in whatever language we can understand and in whatever style and form we can discern.
But what, then, is the position of the referee in a competitive game or sport? What is his peculiar status in relation to the players? The usual answer is that in a competitive game or sport, the referee represents the rules of the game. This answer presupposes, indeed, that the two sides recognize the rules as valid for the game being played. If not, either they would not be playing it at all, or that they would be playing a different game altogether.
Such a recognition naturally reflects a decision, both personal and collective, to observe the rules and to submit to it in the course of the game. These rules define the limits of the competitors' freedom of play and their mock combat. In this sense, they prohibit a certain degree of acts and moves, but at the same time circumscribe, negatively, the acts and moves permitted, that is to say the relatively interdeterminate field of action in which acts occurring during the competition may be considered lawful.
"It is forbidden to buy votes at the polling booth", signifies that all votes bought outside the polling booth are legal. The question is: what does the constitution say about vote-buying? Do we know what the rules say?
