By what they forbid the rules give focus to the struggle; the competitors must strive to win within the field defined by the rules of the game, which thus close it off, giving it a relatively determined and systematic form within which they are free to act as they choose.
There is, of course, a constant danger of the rules being broken- the latent risk that the players may be carried away and their desire to win become stronger than their original commitment to observe the rules defining their ultimate participation in the game. Unfortunately, in our kind of game, there are practically no rules on the ground to dictate the actions of the players. And the referee is helpless as to which rules to adopt in the performance of his role as a mediator in this game of numbers.
The risk involved is, then, that, one side might win without observing the rules of the game since after all there are no rules to observe. This reminds us of the fable of the cheat who wins a different game from the one that he is playing, to the extent that he chooses deliberately and consciously not to play the game as defined by the set rules. He plays the game as though there is no referee. And in this case the spectators ought to raise the alarm.
In the first instance, must the defending champion be allowed to appoint the referee? If yes, what judgment do we expect from the referee? The news around town is that the referee could influence the outcome of the game. Alas! At 53, Nigeria has ironically been reduced to a cocoa-yam republic as the giant seems mocked by history itself.
The referee, as we have said, represents the rules of the game. By his presence he incarnates the pure form of the agon by which his particular game is defined. We could conceive of a "pure referee" who would in a sense be the rules in themselves as they give form to the whole set of actions constituting a "match" by closing them off in a system of play. The referee can and must be no more than the rules as pure form; otherwise he would cease to be a referee. No doubt, subjectively, he may have his own feelings and emotions, his desire that one side should win rather than the other. But in this, he is no referee.
This, he can be only if the representation of the rules of which he is the signifier and his own subjectivity, are effectively kept apart from each other. At the same time, however, this force of the rules exist only by virtue of the polemical action of the forces of the players, that is, only because those forces confront one another within the framework and according to the schema outlined by the rules and ultimately only because the game is played.
If there were no actual game, if it existed only as the possibility of play, there would be no need for a referee. Let us produce a referee who will formulate the actual rules of the game, scrutinize and approve them before the actual game commences. This would guide us against embarking on yet another game of blame. For, our kind of game is one that involves the destiny of over 150 million people.
The game must have rules. For, although vast, fearful, capricious like an ocean, life hinges all its activities on laid down rules and regulations. The polity itself, I mean, the country, must also have agreeable rules as to how power must be shared amongst the brother nationalities that constitute it. We must come together to formulate these rules.

Original_Search_Title: NYSC Allays Fears of Staff Lay-Off
Headline: Nigeria: NYSC Allays Fears of Staff Lay-Off
Body_Text:
Abuja — The National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) has dismissed fears that the full computerisation of the mobilisation processes of the scheme would not lead to the retrenchment of some staff of the organisation, assuring that no member staff would lose his job.
The Director of Corps Mobilisation of the scheme, Anthony Ani, who gave this clarification in a press briefing in Abuja, explained that the new system would rather offer the management opportunity to train the entire staff, majority of who are computer illiterate, in order to meet up with the demands of the new arrangement.
"There is no need for jittery. Nobody would be retrenched. The staff would still do their jobs because there are so many things to be done, including sensitisation. The machine can't do everything, the staff are still very relevant," he assured.
The Director further disclosed that the N4,000 fee being charged on prospective corps members for the on-line mobilisation processes is optional, explaining that those who could not afford the fee could still access their call-up letters and other documentations manually.
"If you don't want to enjoy the benefits of the computerised platform, you don't need to pay. Your inability to pay the fee will not stop you from registering. We'll just print the call-up letters manually. We're putting both amala and pounded yam on the table for you to choose," he stressed.
He said the management is not unmindful of the financial burden the new system has on the corps members, adding that as a highly responsible organisation, it has no intention of defrauding anybody, insisting that the platform would guarantee sanity and credibility.
Enumerating the huge benefits that accrue from the computerised system, Ani said the decision to go digital was borne out of management's concern over the frustration the old system had subjected the corps members to, insisting that the automation makes the entire process faster, less rigorous and stress-free.
He disclosed that the Messrs SIDMACH Technologies Nigeria Limited, which won the project under the Public Private Partnership (PPP) arrangement after a transparent selection, would be providing the scheme with the entire infrastructure across the nation which would be serviced annually.
"The company is providing all the massive infrastructure, including the hard and softwares at the NYSC headquarters, states secretariat, 37 camps and the 774 Local governments offices across the country," he added.
Ani said the scheme had taken steps to educate all stakeholders on the nitty-gritty of the system, in view of the sharp reactions the sudden change would elicit from the public.

Original_Search_Title: Senate Leader - All Is Not Well With Cross River PDP
Headline: Nigeria: Senate Leader - All Is Not Well With Cross River PDP
Body_Text:
The Senate Leader, Victor Ndoma-Egba (SAN), has admitted that serious crisis is currently rocking the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Cross River State.
In Ndoma-Egba's views, the problems in the party stem from attempts by its leadership in the state to run the affairs of the party by creating policies that contravene the party's constitution.
Ndoma-Egba, who is the senator representing the central senatorial district of the state, said any attempt to use measures not recognised by the party's constitution to conduct its affairs at any level would be resisted by the law-abiding members of the party.
The Senate Leader, who spoke with journalists at the weekend in his residence in Ikom, about 300 kilometres from Calabar, stated this in apparent reference to attempts by the leadership of the PDP in the state under the chairmanship of Ntufam John Okon , to use persons appointed into the party's caucuses at all its tiers of administration to screen aspirants seeking elective positions on its platform in 2015 as well as zone the elective positions for the state House of Assembly to the Office of the Governor.
The stance of the Senate Leader came a few days after a group of professionals operating on the aegis of PDP Conscience Group, raised the alarm accusing the party leadership in the state of deliberately violating the party's constitution so as to deny some members the right to vie for elective offices during the 2015 general election.
Ndoma-Egba, who has faulted the move said the policy is illegal, unacceptable and unknown to the constitution of the PDP which he is a highly placed member. "I have heard of caucuses meeting to zone positions and to screen aspirants. The party's constitution is very clear. The caucuses have no such powers; no such powers. So, if they begin to go outside the clear provisions of the constitutions some of us will challenge them.
"I will not run away from the truth that all is not well today with the PDP in Cross River State. I won't deny that. I can't live in self-denial. We must come to the reality that we have very serious issues in PDP in Cross River State and time has come for us to begin to address those issues," Ndoma-Egba said.
He gave reasons why he had remained silent in the eyes of the public before now over the alleged irregularities perpetrated by the party's leadership saying, "There is a time for everything. When situations involve you, we give everybody the benefit of doubts. You believe that these are honest and genuine mistakes, you use back door roots to do that; and I operated in the back door. Every time an issue came up, I called the necessary functionaries of the party to say look these are my views on the issue, this is what I think the constitution provides and that is what we are supposed to do. But, if it becomes a pattern and it becomes persistent, then you know that clearly a point must be reached where the issues must be addressed and I believed that we have reached that point."
However, the Senate Leader in expressing hope that the issues can be resolved by the party said: "The PDP is very strong, is very versatile and it has very efficient conflict resolution mechanism. I believe that the polar positions, as you have put them, will be resolved using our conflict resolution mechanism."
Ndoma-Egba also absolved his supporters of blame over the alleged blockade of the Chairman of the House of Representatives Committee on Appropriation, John Owan Enoh on Saturday, September 7.
Enoh, who is believed to be eyeing the Central Senatorial District seat in 2015 was allegedly blocked from entering Akparabong community, Ndoma-Egba's homestead in Ikom Local Government Area, where he had gone to celebrate the community's new yam festival.
It is alleged that youths from Akparabong had barricaded the road leading to the community when they heard that Enoh,who represent Obubra/Etung was on his way to the event. Though Ndoma-Egba was not in the state when the alleged incident happened, his perceived opponents have blamed his supporters for barricading the road. Exonerating his supporters of blame over the incident, Ndoma-Egba said: "Let me start by expressing my regrets about the incident. I don't have the details. As a lawyer, I know that certain freedoms are guaranteed by our constitution. You have the freedom of movement, you have the freedom of association, you have the freedom of speech, and for me those freedoms are almost like scripture because they are fundamental rights and under no circumstances should those rights be circumscribed by anybody.
"I was not around because I was still on my way back from out of the country so; I couldn't make it to the new yam festival. But, what I find sickening is the strenuous attempt to link it to my loyalists. Do they have a registry of my loyalists?"
Ndoma-Egba said he has been a victim of blockade in the past and that on every occasion he was blocked, the issued raised by his antagonists were resolved amicably.

Original_Search_Title: My Supporters Not Responsible for Enoh's Blockade, Says Senator Ndoma-Egba
Headline: Nigeria: My Supporters Not Responsible for Enoh's Blockade, Says Senator Ndoma-Egba
Body_Text:
The Leader of the Senate, Victor Ndoma-Egba(SAN) has absolved his supporters of blame over the alleged blockade of the Chairman of the House of Representatives Committee on Appropriation, John Owan Enoh last weekend.
Enoh who is believed to be eyeing the Central Senatorial District seat in 2015 was allegedly blocked from entering Akparabong community, Ndoma-Egba's homestead in Ikom Local Government Area of Cross River State, where he had gone to celebrate the community's new yam festival.
It was alleged that youths from Akparabong had barricaded the road leading to the community when they heard that Enoh, who represents Obubra/Etung was on his way to the event.
Though Ndoma-Egba was not in the state when the alleged incident happened, his perceived opponents have blamed his supporters for barricading the road.
But Ndoma-Egba has exonerated his supporters of blame over the incident.
Speaking with journalists in his residence in Ikom on Friday night, Ndoma-Egba said:"Let me start by expressing my regrets about the incident. I don't have the details. As a lawyer, I know that certain freedoms are guaranteed by our constitution. You have the freedom of movement, you have the freedom of association, you have the freedom of speech, and for me those freedoms are almost like scripture because they are fundamental rights and under no circumstances should those rights be circumscribed by anybody.
I was not around because I was still on my way back from out of the country so, I couldn't make it to the new yam festival. But, what I find sickening is the strenuous attempt to link it to my loyalists. Do they have a registry of my loyalists? "

Original_Search_Title: Race to Succeed Jang Hots Up
Headline: Nigeria: Race to Succeed Jang Hots Up
Body_Text:
Jos — There appears to be a general view that Plateau 2015 governorship elections will be very interesting to watch. As a matter of fact, the governorship aspirants have started coming out with their posters adorning available spaces, places of worship and recreation centres to sell themselves to their party leaders and faithful in the state.
While these declarations have become a daily affair now in the state, it is still at the level of pasting posters and continuous consultations taking place in local government areas of the state waiting the total lifting of ban on public campaigns by the electoral umpire, Independent national electoral Commission (INEC).
At the moment, there are only two functional political parties in Plateau state such as the ruling party, The Peoples Democratic Party and All Progressives Congress (APC) while parties like Labour Party and Democratic Peoples Party (DPP) have been abandoned by those who used their platforms to contest elections in the state in 2007 and 2011 elections and are now seeking refuge in the ruling Peoples Democratic party in the state.
Going by the outcome of the three past governorship elections in the state in 1999, 2003, 2007 and 2011, Plateau state remains the home base of the Peoples Democratic Party due to the influence of the premier national chairman of the Party, late Chief Solomon Daushep Lar, and Governor Jonah David Jang, who played active roles in the formation of the PDP prior to the 1999 general elections that ushered in the nascent democratic era in the country.
While critical stakeholders and political watchers on the Plateau are getting worried over the slow pace of political campaigns in the state by governorship aspirants in less than two months before the party primaries billed for the month of October or November, the last quarter of this year, the truth of the matter remains that the drum beat of this game on the plateau has changed and the dancers to the tune of the political music on the plateau have become very careful so that they will not be swept off their feet before the main game begins during the party primaries.
Aside this fact , Governor Jang, the Chairman of Governors' Forum of Nigeria, has stamped his feet on the ground in the last 8 years on the plateau and have succeeded in running out of the town, his perceived political opponents in the ruling peoples Democratic party in the state to seek refuge in opposition parties in the state that have become uncomfortable to personalities like Senator Joshua Dariye, the immediate past Governor of the state, 3rd republic Governor of the state, Amb. Fidelis Tapgun, Former Deputy Governor of the state, Dame Pauline Tallen, and several others and have indicated their interest to return to their original party, the PDP, as it was gathered that some of them have shown interest in contesting 2015 elections under the platform of PDP on the Plateau.
Governor Jang today on the Plateau has become "a man with the knife and the roasted yam who dictates what quantity he gives out to whoever is interested in eating the yam" as he is fully in charge of political affairs, giving no room for Abuja -based politicians from the state. Jang's elected chairmen are in control of all the 17 Local government councils in the state, empowered to determine who becomes delegates from the councils at the party primaries in October for all elective positions of the ruling party .
Plateau governorship aspirants on the plateau are fully aware of this fact and as a result each of them Daily Independent gathered have employed the services of traditional rulers and elders in their communities believed to be close pals of Governor Jang to make case for them before Jang, seeking his support to succeed him in 2015.
At a recent function at Government lodge and office in jos, Governor Jang repeatedly told Christian leaders of Pentecostal Fellowship extraction that visited him that he was waiting on God to anoint his successor since his coming to power had the anointing of God.
According to Jang, he needs a bold and courageous person to take over the mantle of leadership from him in 2015 to be able to stand his ground in the face of daunting challenges that confront the state, especially security-wise. He called on PFN Christian leaders to pray for a good successor who will continue from where his government will stop in 2015 on the Plateau.
Jang used the occasion to direct all his commissioners to properly prepare their handing over notes so as to give his successor complete and comprehensive information upon which to continue with the work they have started.
The Acting Chairman of PFN, Plateau state chapter, Pastor David Adegboye, who led the delegation said, their visit is to appreciate the governor for the good work he is doing on the Plateau and to pray for him.
As events continue to unfold in the state, governorship aspirants under the platform of the ruling party in the is month of September, are still operating under ground as their campaign offices are being operated without party flags and campaign bill boards as most of them contacted told Daily independent that they were still waiting to get green signals from their party and the leader of the party in the state Governor Jang.
Similarly, the main opposition party in the state All Progressives Congress (APC) is not faring better as the party is enmeshed in intra-party crisis over the position of the party chairman in the state.
Chairman of the party, Letep Dabang's leadership has been challenged by Senator John Shagaya who recently stormed the Kalwa House party secretariat with Amos Gombil, a former lawmaker in the state to pronounce him the chairman of the party in the state in place of the duly elected party chairman, Dabang. Gombil came second during the party state congresses that was held in Jos that produced Dabang.
Senator Shagaya is claiming to have acted on the premise of a letter he received from the national secretariat of the party following a petition written by Gombil against Letep Dabang to the effect that Letep was a staff of the National Assembly and personal assistant to Senator Dariye.
At the moment, Plateau APC has two state secretariats in Jos with no hope of resolving their differences within the shortest possible time that could have given them the advantage of standing as a viable alternative to the ruling party in the 2015 Governorship elections in the state.At the last count, not less than 15 personalities, cutting across the three senatorial districts of the state, have indicated interest to run for the governorship race on the platform of the Peoples Democratic Party. Also, some political gladiators have shown interest to contest Plateau 2015 governorship seat in the state under the umbrella of All Progressive party(APC) in the state with APC zoning its governorship seat to the southern senatorial district of the state while the Governorship ticket of the ruling party remains open to all interested parties within Peoples Democratic party in the state.
Governor Jang, Daily Independent gathered, is still keeping to himself the criteria to be used in picking his successor, but told newsmen at a media chat at Government lodge and office in Jos that going by the achievements of his administration all sectors of the economy in the last seven years on the Plateau, his party is better positioned to produce the next Governor that will succeed him in 2015.
Going by the political calculation that is going on within the ruling party in the state, Daily Independent reliably gathered that the elders of the party and Governor Jang might have zeroed down their mind on having one of the commissioners that have scored above average on Jang Redemption mentorship as the party's candidate in the forth coming party primaries in the state as both Jang and party elders may not be ready to give power to those who do not have knowledge of how the state was governed in the last seven and half years to take over from Jang in 2015.

Original_Search_Title: Onitsha South LG Boss Aids Widows
Headline: Nigeria: Onitsha South LG Boss Aids Widows
Body_Text:
Onitsha — It was a unique event Sunday, September 7 when the chairman of Onitsha South Local Government Area, Anambra State, Lady Ann Chukwuneke, disbursed money and yam tubers to about 800 widows as she celebrated new yam festival for them.
The event, held at her All Progressive Grand Alliance, (APGA), Campaign office Fegge, Onitsha, attracted people apart from the over 800 widows and her deputy, Egbuna A. Achebe, party chieftains that included the APGA chairman in the area, Sylvester Ezeokeonwa, and the secretary of the council, Silvanus Mgbechukwu.
The woman chairman first provided the widows with food and drinks and provided other materials as take-home.
At the event, Chukwuneke revealed that it was an annual event aimed at touching the hearts of widows. According to her, "I am also a widow so I know what their problems are. Today they have finished all the money I had in my bag. I know that some of them don't have money to feed their children today. You have seen me perform this new yam celebration to them today, nobody has done it before.

Original_Search_Title: Waiting for the Jazz Stars
Headline: Zimbabwe: Waiting for the Jazz Stars
Body_Text:
The Harare Jazz Festival which was scheduled for this Saturday at Belgravia Sports Club has been postponed indefinitely.
In an interview from South Africa where he is currently on tour, Sam Mataure the organiser of the festival and also Oliver Mtukudzi's manager yesterday confirmed the gig had been moved to a later date.
"The festival will take place at a later date but everything else remains the same in terms of the artiste line-up and venue. Really there isn't anything major but what happened is that we have been pressed with time here in South Africa where we have shows lined up and Mudhara (Tuku) is involved in a number of projects," Mataure said.
He, however, apologised to jazz lovers for the inconvenience caused saying the show is worth the wait.
"We are sorry for the delay but we would want fans to know that the show will go on and definitely worth the wait," he said.
Mataure, who is also the drummer for Mtukudzi's group - The Black Spirits - said they would return to Harare at the weekend.
This year's edition of the Harare Jazz Festival is expected to feature the likes of Hugh Masekela, Auntie Dorothy Masuku, Beritha Khumalo, Tanga WekwaSando, Prudence Katomeni-Mbofana and the Cool Crooners.
Nigerian Kunle Ayo makes a welcome return to Harare for yet another dance with his fans. Richie Lopes, soulful Kudzai Sevenzo, Jazz Invitation, Maylene Chenjerai, Bulawayo Kwela and Mokoomba are also billed to perform.
Other names on the bill are Matthew Ngorima, Shingi Mangoma, Norman Masamba, Patience Musa, Frank Mavhimira, Kudakwashe Mucheche, Macy and the Red, Silence Nqonqo Nhendere and Mbare Trio.
The presence of Bra Hugh at the festival will add spark to what may already have been a thrilling line-up of performers.
Arguably a crowd puller, Bra Hugh was introduced to Zimbabwean audience by popular music promoter the late Job Kadengu. Together with Kadengu, Bra Hugh performed at a number of shows throughout the country.
He is known for his classic hits among them the sweet song of "Tomorrow", "Bring it Back Home" and "Chileshe" and it would be entertainment galore at the festival when Bra Hugh takes to the stage with his wind instrument to serenade music lovers.
Also expected to add glitter to the festival is Masuku, popularly known as Aunty Dot.
She enjoys performing in the country and always emphasises the beauty of homecoming.
Aunty Dot was born in 1936 in Zimbabwe and hit the charts in the 50s when she popularised "Pata Pata" and she later on released such hits as "Imali Yam Iphel", "Eshabhini" and "Hamba Nontsokolo". She has performed at Harare Jazz Festival before and thrilled audiences. She is likely to do the same this year.
At the festival, fans have been given a chance to attend the show either in groups of 10 or as individuals with tickets going for $30 per head.

Original_Search_Title: EGBA Denies Enoh Access to New Yam Festival
Headline: Nigeria: EGBA Denies Enoh Access to New Yam Festival
Body_Text:
The House of Representatives Committee Chairman on Appropriations, John Owan Enoh and his entourage were denied access to Akparabong Local Government Area in Cross River State by Senator Ndoma Egba supporters, who barricaded the road leading to the venue of the New Yam festival the former was duly invited to.
According to eye-witness, Enoh's convoy of about 100 cars were forced to detour after running into a blockade allegedly initiated by Egba. The development is not unconnected to the forthcoming senatorial election in 2015, which Egba and Enoh are vying to represent Central Senatorial district in the fifth Senate.
Enoh sources said possess a threat to Egba's fifth Senate ambition in the Central Senatorial district in Cross Rivers State. The Central Senatorial District comprises Ikom, Oki, Etun, Obubra, Abii and Yakaau.
Eye-witness said tension mounted immediately Enoh's convey ran into a blockade and was subsequently denied access by Senator Egba's security aids to Akparaobong and Ikom where the new yam festival was being celebrated.
"It was just a matter of second before both security aids of Egba and Enoh to engage in a fisticuffs" however, he said the intervention of re-enforcement unit saved the day.
On a telephone chat, Enoh said the development potents a grave danger for democracy and called on politicians to be guided by the law but not desperation.
"I was invited by friends to attend new yam festival in Akparaobong and Ikom not on political campaign but I was denied access to the place by those loyal to Senator Ndoma Egba. My friends and I were denied entry into the town but the intervention of security men eventually saved the day as I was the only one allowed driving to the town."
According to him, the action of those behind this shameful act is unnecessary because it is not yet time for electioneering campaign.
"The same man who stops me from entering his local government was voted for by my local government in the last election and he still has the right to come to Etun to campaign when the time for that is right."
Enoh said he was encouraged by the way the aged and women trooped out to welcome him.
His words: "We should not be desperate when we have the opportunity to serve people; we should do it well."
He called on politicians to be tolerant and discourage the use of tugs to grab power by all means. However, efforts to reach Senator Egba proved abortive as he was not available for comment as at press time.

Original_Search_Title: Grassroots Will Decide Next Governor of Lagos - Leke Pitan
Headline: Nigeria: Grassroots Will Decide Next Governor of Lagos - Leke Pitan
Body_Text:
After serving as Commissioner for Health and Education over a period of eight years during the Bola Ahmed Tinubu administration in Lagos State, Dr Leke Pitan, thinks it is not out of place to take a shot at the number one seat of power in Alausa, Ikeja. Although so many conjectures have been postulated about who would replace incumbent Governor Babatunde Fashola, beginning from who emerges as choice candidate from the All Progressive Congress (APC) guber primary, Pitan, who is current Special Adviser for Africa on the board of the Geneva based International Hospital Federation (IHF), tells News Editor, ONCHE ODEH, that he has little to worry about, having been tested over time in the state. He also spoke about some of his plans for Lagos, which he says would lead to the review of some current policies for the wellbeing of Lagosians, in actualising what he terms APC's social welfare manifesto. He also spoke on the Ebola outbreak in Nigeria. Excerpts.
At what point did you start nursing the ambition to govern Lagos State?
After the metamorphosis of the party, we all thought it was right to recollect our history. So after the merger, and because of the different compositions of the legacy party, certain leaders and stalwarts of the party met and came out with the decision that there is a need for the party to have an ideology. So we created a social welfare manifesto, which included free education to a point, free health and other social welfare policies for the masses and others.
Having gotten to that stage, some of the stalwarts felt that there was the need for someone with the right credential to run for the governorship in Lagos. This was after the launch of the manifesto in Abuja. Then I was approached. Prior to that, some people had been muting the idea. As far back as last year, people had indicated interest. I was encouraged by those stalwarts to step forward, given my pedigree and my past achievements. Don't also forget that I have supervised and superintended the health and education sectors in Lagos, which are the key social welfare sectors, in the past. Part of the reasons given by the leaders for urging me to come forth also was to engender confidence building process between the government and the populace in Lagos. So they thought it was important to bring out somebody that the populace would easily relate with in this regards.
Like someone rightly suggested, if you say people are in rags and you want to cloth them you will naturally send your best tailors. Especially, a tailor they are already used to, who has good records of dress making before them and not a carpenter that will make coffin for them. So it is not a case of a party, a leader or a godfather imposing me, just a school of thoughts of stalwarts that think that the party needs somebody to lead their social welfare manifesto. They thought it was pertinent to put forward someone the public can easily believe in, more so that we are now going into an era when it is no longer what logo of party you brandish, but more of the personality and product of the candidate. Not that one can be thrown away for the other. But it is now more about the personality of the candidates and not just the party. They realised way back then that as we are getting closer to 2015, the level of sophistication of our people, regardless of the fact that they are grassroots, has gone beyond the party label. They would consider the personality. And therefore it is incumbent on any party that wants to win to put forward their best candidates.
Prior to your stepping out, so many names have been peddled in the media, it is said that some candidates have been anointed ahead of others. Are your in any way intimidated by this development?
There is no way I could ever feel intimidated by that. I don't want to say I have been here longer than most of my colleagues. But I have been in this game for sometimes. That much nobody can take away from me. To that extent, I have seen a lot and I know what the usual sequence of events is, and know **when it is appropriate for the issue of the media and that grassroots acceptance is the first and foundation and not about the media hype.** I am also aware that a man cannot give what he does not possess. If you are already are a grassroots person and politician and well known, the issues of media hype is not necessary. It is a matter of reconnecting with the people to let them know that you are available to be used by the party. Because you already have the pedigree, you can easily be picked. This is readily made off the shelf. You don't buy it. You may, to a certain extent use the media to make a façade. But within a short time the mask would be removed. Under such circumstances, I cannot be intimidated. Now the situation is even different, in terms of public awareness.
How different?
Those of us who were not really in the media glare before are now the toast, not only of the media, but of the relevant portion of the polity, the grassroots. Any party worth its salt must not throw out a candidate that the grassroots are comfortable with, that has no blemish... that is credible and already well known. Not somebody you offer to the public and they begin to ask about his pedigree and records. Any candidate whose past records would be easily blown off with the wave of the hands would not help anybody. Even if you organise a situation internally and make it appear like an apparent democratic setting to make him emerge, you would only create a one-day joy for him, the party and the leadership that made him, which is the day of the primaries. After that day, the rest of the party members would then have the headache of trying to sell the candidate. Imagine a situation where you knock at a door and a person opens the door and you say you want to talk about your party candidates and you say his name, and they ask you: 'who is that?' You have lost already. In that household they don't know somebody who could say I know about him before, not just from the media, but for good. To add an icing, you meet someone who says, yes I know him. He has assisted me or someone that I know at the hour of needs. You have won already. It sounds pedestrian and almost mundane. But it is reality.
The current government in Lagos has been severally accused of running an elite regime. Does it look like something you might want to fix when elected?
Naturally as true democratic politicians, our whole group which started the party together, including the governor, Babatunde Raji Fashola, Wale Edun, Dele Alake, Ogbeni Rauf Aregbesola, Yemi Cardoso... name them, knew that at the end of the day, pleasing the people by serving them is the ultimate goal. If in the process of trying to do so we appear to be having some policies that people feel is pinching them, it is incumbent on us to review and redraw. Saying that it is something we would be going into is simply saying the obvious. Let me also tell you that the present administration is already reviewing some of those policies, based on the fact that the party runs a government that is responsive to the people and have listening ears. They are already doing that and not necessarily waiting till we come. And I can tell you that we would deepen it and relax it more for the people to be happy. The details I would not want to bore you with. But the direction is clear which is to carry our people along, especially at the grassroots and to have it at the back of their mind the people's limited purchasing ability.
Thirdly, the fact that we appreciate that they live day to day, and therefore we would restrategise in such a way that we would not uproot or disrupt people without having alternatives for them, because we know that such a person has a chain of dependents. Even if that person is the woman selling roasted yam by the road side, not fried yam which is higher scale, there is a chain of dependents behind the woman. We always have that at the back of our mind.
Only recently you embarked on free medical mission across Lagos. Was that intended to launch you into the minds of the people?
Coming about it is only natural because the fundamental frame of mind of passionate commitment to selfless service to humanity has always been there. Also, coming to terms with the fact that during the doctors' strike it is only the public hospitals which the poor have access, that were shut. If the place is not running, the poor has nowhere to run. So we decided to provide something for them. We also appreciate the fact that their purchasing power is limited. So it is to help them by appreciating their situation, the environment, and their critical situation and help them get access to health service. You need to feel the relief of someone who is virtually blind that is helped to see again. That person is handed a life back, as he or she is able to see and do economic activities. He is able to feel that he is being granted a level playing ground to participate in the economic activities in Lagos, which is one of the promises that Lagos held for him or her in coming. That is why our sobriquet is: "Delivering Lagos to the People (DLP)". It is going to be the foundation of my government in Lagos.
To be honest, I don't know any other definition of governance than what I have described. This is not like playing to gallery or trying to use it as campaign gimmick. I am telling you sincerely before God and man, that is all I believe, and I am holding on to that. Cast your minds back. The eight years I spent in government were dedicated to that. You want to think about the year we started jigi Bola (free eye glasses distributed free to the aged by the Tinubu administration), it was dedicated to that. When I started the Lagos State Ambulance Services (LASAMBUS) programme, it was to give hope to the helpless, snatching people from the jaws of death, free open heart corrective surgery, free limb correction for the poor cripple crawling on the ground, which is intended to help them stand and do some economic activities that could be of help to even others.
Even as commissioner of education, I started the free school milk programme. Knowing that in the homes these children come from, milk is a luxury, giving them some in school could help make them more brilliant. I even went ahead to create 29 inclusive schools and special education centres for handicapped students in Epe, Badagry, Ibeju Lekki, where in the past the handicapped had no hope. In such places in the past, if a, mother had a child who is blind, or cripple, that would have been the end of that child. The only schools available then were in Surulere- the Wesley School for deaf and the Pacelli School for the blind. There was nothing in Epe, nothing in Ibeju Lekki, nothing in Badagry. It is this same tendencies that would form the bedrock of the next government that is coming to Lagos.
What do you make of the current efforts by the Federal Government to contain the Ebola virus outbreak?
One must commend the FG for what has been done so far, but more importantly, commend the Lagos state government- the governor and the health team... I am proud of them, because they were part of the legacy I left behind. It made all of us of that era feel that our efforts were not in vain. They were tested and now trusted. That we have an Infectious Disease Hospital (IDH) in Yaba, which is now an isolation centre, gives credence to the fact that emergency response was part of our flagship. At that time, if there was any emergency, it is Lagos State they would call upon. Ambulances on the bridges... just name them. So much so, that even the FAAN would call Lagos anytime there were problems. It is all these, coupled with the Health sector Reforms that transformed to Health Facility Monitoring and Accreditation Agency (HEFAMA) that resulted in the high quality services at Lagos private hospitals. It is such level of intervention that made First Consultant to be what they are and for them to respond as fast as they did to pick the first Ebola case in Nigeria and alert the authority, which managed the situation so professionally that even the international bodies have commended. Otherwise the story would have been different. We only need to imagine if this would have been in the hands of quacks who would have tried all manners of injections and drips on a patient who contracted it, how many people they would have infected and how many that would have died. Imagine how the people would have embraced the dead. All those primary contacts would contact so many others, maybe we are lucky one of the tertiary contacts would have been discovered. Do you know what would have happened? My God! We probably don't know what has passed over us. Nigeria should thank God it came through Lagos. If it were some other places, only God knows what would have happened.
The security situation in Nigeria has become a major source of concern. Where did we get it wrong and how can we make amends?
Part of where we got it wrong is corruption. Because of corruption we are unable to provide the needed safety net for the people in case they fall. At that point it becomes a free-for-all situation, and everyone begins to arrange for his own security. This leaves so many poor to just exist without any meaningful thing to hold on to. The way to get it right is to reverse all that. Be sure there is a special safety net for everyone to fall into. Every opportunity must be exploited to create employments. Let me give you an example. If you say you want to feed all school children, especially primary school children, say with just one egg per child per day, do you know what that would do to the poultry industry? From those who would rare the chickens, to those would transport the egg, produce the feeds, the cooks, etc. talk about the school meal. Talk about those would supply the pepper, grand it, do the cooking, that is just one item. Talk about large mass housing. I am not talking about 1000 to 2000 housing units and rolling out the drums. I am talking about building 100, 000 units. Do you know the number of people that would be employed? Talking about the architect, the builders, and even that person who would supply rice and beans to the workers... or that person who would sell pure water? Nelson Mandela when he was coming out said he would do only one term.
Do you know what he promised his people apart from unity and peace? He promised them one million houses. He didn't say 10,000 or 1000. You know the population of South Africa, probably one quarter of Nigeria's. When he was going he apologized to the people. He said he could only build 700,000 units. Do you know what else he did? He gave those housing units free to the people. The money used in building it is your money. He put in it solar heater and pipe borne water, and electricity which is programmed to supply certain measure free, beyond which the occupants pay. That is social safety net. We must move from thinking such things are not sustainable, as if we are doing the people a favour. We must move away from seeing government as business. Government is not a commercial business. Even giving Nigeria's experience, is it by giving a certain Commissioner of finance N200,000 to go to Abuja for FAAC meeting, sleep in NICON Hotel, and paying air ticket to and fro, and coming back with a cheque of N4, N6 billion... in some states N18 billion? What kind of business is that? It is just that you are a messenger going to collect money for the people. Use that money to give them good health. Start tackling their health needs and then start tackling infrastructure as long term projects, so that we stop mixing apples and oranges. We match long term projects with long term funds. If we take away social sector like health first, whatever is left can be put into concrete infrastructure that can last long, like road and schools that can last for 20 to 30 years. We don't need to take all of the money to start big projects, leaving very little for the people.
They need to be alive and well protected so that they can continue to be citizens to benefit from the long term infrastructure. The primary school I intended over 50 years ago is still there. These cannot be done with one-year budget. If I need one thousand schools, it will not make sense to begin to use one year budget for it. There would be nothing left for the people. We are calling for a paradigm shift where the people's immediate socio economic needs are enhanced first. The next is the economic sector, which include the roads, where we can take funds and pay over time. If you have N100 billion, service the people as much as possible... You can also borrow another N200 to N300 billion, from which you can use N60 to N70 billion to service the people. You can use their other part to do the long term projects, so that the people can leave for another 30 years that the projects would last.

Original_Search_Title: Honour for RMD, Ngozi Ezeonu and Ogene Ndigbo in Asaba
Headline: Nigeria: Honour for RMD, Ngozi Ezeonu and Ogene Ndigbo in Asaba
Body_Text:
SOME prominent members of the Nollywood fraternity were recently honoured during the opening ceremony of the 4th Nollywood Igbo Film Festival and Award (NIFA 2014), which held in Asaba, Delta State. The acclaimed promoter of Igbo culture and cause Eze (Dr.) Harris Chuma (a.k.a Ogene Igbo-Ezemuo) was one of those honoured at the event. The multiple award-winning culture advocate was officially conferred and installed as the 'Eze Omenani Igbo Nollywood', which, if translated in Igbo means King of Igbo Culture.
The coronation was presided over by Eze Onyeche Promise Obinna. Eze Obinna is the Ikemba 1 of Ulakwo-Umuselem of Etche Local Government Area of Rivers State. He is also the President of Transparent Leadership Initiative International (TLI).
Eze (Dr.) Harris Chuma who was nominated for the honour by the group, Ohanaeze Ndigbo Nollywood was honoured in recognition of his contributions and involvement in the promotion of Igbo culture and values. It would be recalled that in December 2010, Eze Chuma led the Igbo Community in Nollywood to establish the Igbo Film Forum (IFF), a professional body for filmmakers of Igbo extraction for the actualization of Igbo Film Industry and true rebirth of Igbo Language Films. Subsequently, in December 2011 at Awka, Anambra State, he founded and directed the first ever Nollywood Igbo Film Festival and Awards (NIFA) with a mission to mobilize Ndigbo in Nollywood in creative and cultural discovery through the moving image.
Also in September 2013, in Lagos, he initiated and hosted the first ever Ndigbo in Nollywood New Yam Festival, otherwise known Iri Ji, a full-blown cultural event. Indeed, Eze Chuma has been proactive in the promotion of Igbo culture and language both in Nollywood his primary constituency and beyond. He has been playing a leading role in the campaign to restore Igbo language films.
Consequent and upon his installation, Eze Chuma visited the paramount king of Anioma land, Asagba of Asaba, Obi Chike Edozien, who congratulated him and members of Ohanaeze Ndigbo Nollywood and poured his royal blessings on him while urging the group not to relent in its effort to promote Igbo culture and unity.
Also, Obi Agbogidi E. O. Isichei, who was a special guest of honour at the festival, played host to the newly crowned Eze Chuma at his palace in Ubulu-Okiti, Delta State. He blessed the Eze Omenani and his entourage, and encouraged them not to allow Igbo culture and language to disappear in Nollywood movies.
Similarly, the duo of Ngozi Ezeonu and Emeka Ani were among Nollywood stars, who got shieftaincy titles from the highly revered monarch, Eze Onyeche Obinna. While Ngozi Ezeonu bagged the title of Ezenwanyi Ndigbo Nollywood (Queen Mother) and that of Ugo Ikemba 1, notable actor Emeka Ani was honored with the title of Anya Fulu Ugo 1 (Eyes that saw the eagle).
In the same vein, Eze Omenani Igbo conferred the prestigious shieftaincy title of Dike Oha (Great Man of the People) on foremost actor and Delta State Commissioner for Arts and Culture Richard Mofe-Damijo (RMD). The commissioner thanked Eze Omenani for the honour and promised to continue to work for the development of the arts and film industry. Damijo later hosted Ohanaeze Ndigbo Nollywood at his official residence.

Original_Search_Title: Tension in Enugu Over Murder of Magistrate
Headline: Nigeria: Tension in Enugu Over Murder of Magistrate
Body_Text:
Tension has mounted in Enugu city, Enugu State, following the murder on Tuesday, of a serving Chief Magistrate, O J Nwani.
It was gathered that the jurist was killed by unknown suspected assassins.
It was further learnt in Enugu that the Magistrate, who served in Enugu South Area of the state, Magisterial division until his death, was reportedly invited by his assailants and immediately after coming out, was shot severally at head and on chest.
He was however, rushed to an undisclosed hospital, where he was confirmed dead.
The Enugu State Police Command has confirmed the unfortunate incident, saying it has commenced full investigations on it.
The State Police Public Relations Officer, (PPRO), Ebere Amaraizu said the Command had not made any arrest yet, but efforts were on to track down the killers.
"Our operatives are on the trail of the murderers", Amaraizu said.
In a related development, the Transition Committee Chairman of Isiala Ngwa North Council Area of Abia State, Chief Ginger Onwusibe has denied media reports that he and his aides recently attacked the Senator representing Abia Central Senatorial District, Nkechi Nwaogu.
Onwusibe was accused by the Federal lawmaker, of using hoodlums to hurt her convoy at Uratta junction along Enugu-Port Harcourt express way last week.
Reacting to the allegation, the Council Chief also indicted Nwaogu of being economical with the truth over the incident.
He alleged that the policemen in the Senator's convoy fired shot at his entourage which included Hon. Martin Azubuike, representing Isiala Ngwa North in the state House of Assembly and other chieftains of the People's Democratic Party (PDP) in the council, who were returning from a tour of wards in the area, adding that attempt was made to abduct him.
The Chairman in a statement said: "Senator Nkechi Nwaogu is not telling the truth about the incident which she ordered policemen in her convoy to fire at us.
"If there was an assassination attempt, let her show you people where she was shot or the bullet holes in her vehicle, we only escaped their bullets by the grace of God.
"The truth is that for a month now, we have been on a tour of the 10 wards in the LGA. After the tour, which was the eighth one, on our way to the residence of a party official, who invited us to a reception, we joined the Expressway from the lane of motorists coming from the Umuahia end to get to the other lane.
"As soon as the more than 30 cars in the convoy joined the road median to get to the other lane, we saw a long convoy with a siren coming at a top speed.
"I saw Senator Nkechi Nwaogu giving orders to her security people, 'That is the stupid Ginger boy, shoot him, shoot him.' Policemen and the SSS people in her convoy started shooting.
"Two policemen came down and started beating three of my supporters who were on motorcycles. When they saw my official car, two policemen started dragging the door to my car, where I sat with my wife but I resisted them.
"All through the incident, Senator Nkechi Nwaogu was still shouting to the policemen, 'deal with, deal with him'. Even when the member representing Isiala Ngwa North in the House of Assembly, Hon. Martin Azubuike identified himself as a member of the House, they refused to listen and even threatened to shoot him", narrated Onwusibe.
Onwusibe further alleged that the Senator Nwaogu had continued to attack him and others who have declared support for the decision of the party to zone the 2015 governorship seat to Abia South zone, which clearly excludes her as she hails from Osisioma Ngwa council in Abia Central zone.
"Since the party and the Governor made that statement on zoning, Senator Nkechi Nwaogu has been bitter with anyone who supports the decision. She has also attacked the Governor, but would deny it when confronted.
"We have been very bold supporting the decision of the party. This is why she has continued to attack me and ordered her convoy to shoot at us", he alleged.
Meanwhile, Senator Nwaogu, who addressed newsmen in Aba, affirmed alleged attempt on her life by Onwusibe and hoodlums in his convoy.
She alleged his convoy blocked her entourage as she was returning to Aba after she paid homage to Eze Bernard Enweremadu in Isiala Ngwa North local government Area, whose community held their annual new yam festival.
"We were coming back from an event along the Enugu/Port Harcourt Expressway and on getting to Uratta/Amaekpu junction in Isiala Ngwa North Local Government; a car blocked us right at the middle of the Expressway.
"We went to Umuahia to attend a ceremony and on our way coming back, we went to His Majesty (Eze) Bernard Enweremmadu's palace for his new yam festival.
"After the event, we were heading back to Aba; at the Expressway, we saw a car blocking the expressway and this was at about 6.45 pm. It was not the first time this boy (Mr. Onwusibe) has tried to embarrass me; it was an attempted assassination".
"If not for the policemen that I was given, it was a clear attempt to assassinate me. I thank God that nothing happened to me and to any of the people who were with me".
The federal lawmaker, who accused the council boss of employing every means to stop her from contesting for governorship seat, vowed that nothing would make her drop her ambition.
"I have sent a text message to Governor Theodore Orji and he promised to look into the incident. I have also reported the matter to the Director, SSS of this country and also to the police but I have not been able to reach the State Commissioner of Police because at the time of the incident and till now, his line has not been available" said Nwaogu.
Asked if she had any previous quarrel with the council chief, Senator Nwaogu said "My only offence was that this boy asked for money and I refused.
"Sometime ago, he asked that I should give him N20 million that he would use it to create a good impression before the governor of the state on my behalf, which I refused to".

Original_Search_Title: Vote Out Bad Politicians, Fashola, Others Tell Nigerians
Headline: Nigeria: Vote Out Bad Politicians, Fashola, Others Tell Nigerians
Body_Text:
AHEAD of the 2015 general elections, eminent Nigerians including Governor Babatunde Fashola of Lagos, former Nigerian ambassador to the United States, Ambassador George Obiozor and renowned Professor of Law, Akin Oyebode, yesterday, in Lagos, called on Nigerians not to take their voting power for granted. They were also urged to vote out non-performing office holders or inept party in the forthcoming general elections.
Presiding Bishop of The Redeemed Evangelical Mission, TREM, Dr. Mike Okonkwo, among others, spoke in the same vein, insisting that Nigerians must avail themselves of the opportunities provided by the general elections to make positive change towards the development of democracy in the country.
They spoke at the 15th Mike Okonkwo Annual Lecture, with the theme, 'The power of your vote: A catalyst for a stable and united Nigeria.'
Opportunity
Speaking, Governor Fashola warned that unless the country's value system changes, things might continue the way they were currently, saying, "when you look at the result of our elections, you will struggle to see that (less than) 35 per cent of registered voters come out to vote.
"Another opportunity now beckons, it is a one in four years opportunity. Whether you like it or not, election has started already because permanent voters' cards are being issued. Are you busy at work, stay at work. Those who wish things to happen stay away from them, those who want things to happen, get involved with them. Someone will probably collect your card or return it if it was not used.
"What is happening in February 2015 is balloting out of the whole process of election. After balloting, disputes are resolved and when all that is finished, it is to return the valid candidates and remove the unsuccessful candidates.
"Election is a process and it has begun. When you pick a coach and he does not give us result, what you do is sack the coach. When I employ a driver and he drives me badly, what I need to do is sack him. So, we are your servants, you should not be struggling the job with us. If you think we have not done the job well, sack us.
"In all the places where democracy is practised, it is all about ideas. The idea of a united Nigeria, can we take that away as what we want. In the few times we have had constitutional conferences and we have focused more on our laws and our constitution rather than ourselves.
What are the values and ideals with which we want our nation to run? What is the Nigerian idea for the Nigeria ideal? Good law will do nothing if bad people operate them. Also, with the most badly drafted constitution in the hands of men and women of goodwill, a lot of good can be achieved.
"We should ask ourselves what kind of Nigeria we want. Our value system really is the problem. What is our sense of right and wrong? Is it right only because he is your kinsman or is it right all the time."
Oyebode blasts political class
Oyebode, in his paper, charged Nigerians to vote out the ruling parties both at the federal and state levels if they were not performing, saying, "we just cannot continue to have more of the same and expect to make progress."
He lambasted the political class for failing to deepen the ideals of democracy, saying, "politics in Nigeria is like obscenity. While the false democrats are singing the tune of one-man, one-vote, they are also busy violating the electoral rules while the Independent National Electoral Commission, INEC, is helpless."
He condemned myriads of faceless media campaigns and solidarity rallies currently running in the media, contrary to the date allowed by the Electoral Act, and INEC's arbitrary increase of polling units and failure to prosecute electoral offenders to restore public confidence.
Ethos
The professor of law, while arguing that the nation's founding fathers could be considered as having imbibed democratic ethos, "today's practitioners of the art possibly continue to advertise their discomfort with the tenets of ballot box democracy and more often than not, evince characteristics of desperados -- intolerance of dissent, blackmail and abuse of opponents, naked and crude diktat, a winner-takes-all mentality, obdurate lust for power, jumping the gun, faceless media campaigns, among others. So much so that our political lexicon has since encompassed concepts like 'do or die,' 'no vacancy' and 'carry go."
Oyebode said the people had lost confidence in the political system, necessitating their demand for "stomach infrastructure, because they know once you win election, they will not see you again until four years.
"Nigerians do not care about ideas any more. They would say 'na idea we go chop.' They are hungry. That is why in my own State, Ekiti, the people said 'governor, you have done well with infrastructure, but it does not leave food on my table.' That is why, unbelievably, a highly cerebral and intelligent man lost to a populist pretender.
"When I ran into him at an "amala" (yam flour food restaurant) joint in Abuja, I said, Mr. Governor-elect, how did you win that election? He said it was the hand of God. That is the situation we have with our democracy today."
Chairman of the occasion, who is also the former Director-General of the Nigerian Institute of International Affairs, NIIA Ambassador George Obiozor, in his opening remarks, said "Clearly the organisers of the lecture series understand the precarious nature of Nigeria as a nation and they obviously also recognize the tragedy of nations where ideas, skills, justice and professional competencies are routinely disregarded or relegated to the background in the ordering of national priorities," he said, adding "we continue like that surely, the nation cannot be expected to develop and grow. And if we think that a nation cannot remain undeveloped for 200 years or more, then we need to think again and remember countries like the Republic of Haiti."
Drawing inspiration from Blair Harden's Dispatches from a fragile continent, Prof Obiozor said: "We all know that the very idea of Nigerian unity is still an aspiration and not yet an achievement. Nigeria indeed is still a work-in-progress. The Nigerian Project continues with the recommendations of the just concluded 2014 National Conference. Yet, for Nigeria there are reasons for optimism."
Franchise
Bishop Okonkwo, who is also a former Vice President of the Christian Association of Nigeria, CAN, urged Nigerians to register en masse to vote during elections, saying that his church had begun a process of mobilizing Christians to exercise their franchise in the coming elections.
According to the bishop who will turn 69 on Saturday, all the socio-political cum economic challenges currently plaguing the nation could only be eradicated "if Nigerians deliberately see the Nigerian Project as a collective challenge and actively participate in all electoral processes", stressing that he would mandate

Original_Search_Title: Impersonation - Fake Kai Officer Nabbed
Headline: Nigeria: Impersonation - Fake Kai Officer Nabbed
Body_Text:
A 43-year-old man identified as Shola Onabanjo has been arrested in Ojota area of Lagos State for impersonation and extortion with fake KAI identity card.
Our correspondent learnt that the Kick Against Indiscipline (KAI Brigade), a law enforcement unit of the Lagos State Ministry of Environment, got information about Onabanjo's activities and mobilised its officers to arrest him.
The suspect who was not in KAI uniform at the time of his arrest allegedly demanded N1,000 from a yam seller named Amodu Sulaimon, who was pushing a wheel barrow with some tubers of yam in the area.
Recounting his ordeal, Sulaimon told newsmen that Onabanjo had been extorting money from him and other yam sellers for a long time at Ojota, and assuring them that they would not be arrested by any other KAI official in the area if each of them paid him N1,000 daily.
The bubble burst on him when the KAI patrol vehicle noted that a trader selling tubers of yam at Ojota refused to move away from the spot after being ordered to vacate the place. This brazen act of insubordination led to the arrest of the yam seller who later pointed at Onabanjo, who had given him the mandate to remain at his selling spot.

Original_Search_Title: Because of 1914 Hits Muson Centre September 13
Headline: Nigeria: Because of 1914 Hits Muson Centre September 13
Body_Text:
ODIA Ofeimun's latest critically acclaimed dance drama Because of 1914 will hit the sage at MUSON Centre come September 13 before showing to Abuja theatre-goers on September 30. Ofeimun first had a preview of the dance drama in July before celebrating his mother, Onomonresoa Ofeimun's passing with it at Edo State University auditorium, Ekpoma, Edo State. After that successful outing in his home state, Ofeimun is ready to expose his latest showpiece to a wider, critical Lagos audience. Already, rehearsal is at advance stage, with director, Felix Okolo working hard to put up a fine show.
Tomorrow Died Yesterday is PWBC Book-of-the-Month
AS part of the UNESCO Port Harcourt World Book Capital (PWBC) initiative, 12 books have been meticulously selected as Book-of-the-Month (BOTM) for the 12 months Port Harcourt will be holding the prestigious title. Members of the public are encouraged to read and then meet on the last Sunday of the month to discuss the issues raised in the book, sometimes, with the author present. August BOTM is Chimeka Garriks's Tomorrow Died Yesterday; it's scheduled to hold on Sunday, August 31, at Royal Banquet Hall, Hotel Presidential, Port Harcourt at 3pm. It chronicles the life of a generation caught between the after-effects of government and individual greediness, parents' expectations and how the characters in the novel navigate through their issues in diverse ways. It's a well-researched, humorous, moving tale with a tinge of romance. Tomorrow Died Yesterday makes for an interesting reading.
Igbo Assembly holds New Year festival in Sweden
Igbo General Assembly will organize its new yam festival and Igbo convention on October 18th in Gothenburg, Sweden. The event, which is a yearly cultural event of the Ndigbo community in the Diaspora, will witness one of the largest gathering of Igbo men and women in Europe. According to the President, Mr. Joseph Obinna, the Iri-ji (new yam) is one of the biggest festivals celebrated by the Igbos., "Individual Igbo communities have their days of celebrating the new yam festival. This day symbolizes the conclusion of a work cycle and the beginning of another". The liaison officer of the assembly, Arinze Uzor noted that the event would bring Igbos from Nigeria and all over Europe to discuss developmental issues. There will be award presentations to Igbo sons and daughters who have distinguished themselves in various fields in the promotion of the progress and development in the Igbo community.
Guest expected at the event include the Ambassador of Nigeria to the Nordic region, Benedict Amobi, President of Ohanaeze Ndigbo, Enwo Igariwey, his vice Onyema Agapitus and Obu Ofor Nri of Igboland, Chukwudinigbo Agwuna.
