LABAF 2014 beckons
LAGOS Book and Art Festival (LABAF) 2014, the biggest culture picnic on the continent, will begin on November 14-16 at Freedom Park, Hospital Road, Lagos. Nigeria's Nobel laureate and Africa's illustrious literary icon, Prof. Wole Soyinka will be special guest of honour.

Original_Search_Title: Group Cautions Okorocha, Ihedioha Over Campaign Strategy
Headline: Nigeria: Group Cautions Okorocha, Ihedioha Over Campaign Strategy
Body_Text:
Owerri — A foremost socio-political group known as South East Progressives Assembly (SEPA) has raised the alarm over what it described as 'careless and unguarded utterances' by Governor Rochas Okorocha, who appears to be seeking re- election and the Deputy Speaker, Federal House of Representatives and one of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) frontline governorship aspirants, Emeka Ihedioha, who also seeks to succeed the governor in 2015.
SEPA's position is coming after Okorocha and his PDP rival, Ihedioha, clashed at the venue of the 2014 Mbaise New Yam festival following unprovoked verbal attacks by the two leaders and their supporters.
The group, however, wondered why the two respected and illustrious sons of Imo State against known principles of decency and maturity which many expected them to exhibit resorted to throwing caution to the wind in an apparent show and desperation for power.
The group's position which is canvassed in a statement signed and made available to newsmen by Ebere Uzoukwa and Munonye Maximus, President and Secretary of SEPA in Owerri, the capital city of Imo state, reads: "The attention of SEPA has been drawn to the reported and indeed an avoidable clash between Governor Rochas Okorocha and Deputy Speaker, Federal House of Representatives, Hon. Emeka Ihedioha, and their followers at the venue of the 2014 Mbaise New Yam Festival following attacks and counter attacks by the two leaders that are eyeing the 2015 Imo governorship seat. Our fears and concern about the hostility and seeming desperation for power are not unconnected with the fact that both leaders are well respected and widely perceived by many as role models and men of proven integrity having reaped from the abundant grace and benevolence of God in their political career.

Original_Search_Title: Lawmaker Cautions Ndokwa Youths Against Violence
Headline: Nigeria: Lawmaker Cautions Ndokwa Youths Against Violence
Body_Text:
Asaba — THE Deputy Chief Whip of Delta State House of Assembly, Friday Osanebi, has advised youths of Ndokwa nation to continue to maintain peace and shun violence that could portray the area in bad light.
He assured that he would continue to empower his constituents, calling on the youths of Ndokwa to put the collective interest of the area above their personal and selfish gains to enhance the unity and development of the people.
Osanebi gave the assurance while presenting 10 motorcycles, four Mitsubishi L 300 buses and a Toyota Camry as well as an Infinity Jeep to some youth leaders drawn from Ndokwa West, Ndokwa East and Ukwuani local government areas at Usshie.
Osanebi, member representing Ndokwa East constituency, said the unity of Ndokwa nation should be paramount in their hearts in the interest of all, noting that Ndokwa youths are peace-loving, stressing that no meaningful development can be achieved in an atmosphere of violence.
Osanebi commended the youths for their support to him and the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), adding that they should not mortgage their conscience and the collective interest of Ndokwa nation.
He solicited the support of the Anioma people for Ndokwa nation to produce the next governor of the state and urged the youth to reelect President Goodluck Jonathan come 2015 general elections.
For his part, a top PDP stalwart in the state, Chief Godswill Obielum, commended Osanebi for the empowerment programme, saying he was an icon of youth on Ndokwa nation and urged the youths not to derail in their collective aspiration.
Also, some youth leaders drawn from Ndokwa nation pledged their support to the lawmaker, saying that his empowerment programmes were not restricted to only Ndokwa East but the entire Ndokwa nation.
They called on Osanebi to seek higher political office as he had always carried the youths along in the scheme of things, stressing that they were also solidly behind him in his political aspiration.
The National President, Ndokwa Youth, Ndokwa Youth Council, Benjamin Onwubolu, was presented the Infinity Jeep, while the Toyota Cmary was given to a youth leader by Osanibi.
Meanwhile, Osanebi also visited the Ndokwa Orphanage at Umuseti where he presented bags of rice, tubers of yam, toiletries, beverages and clothes among others. Receiving the items, Mrs. Grace Achi thanked the lawmaker and prayed God to bless him.

Original_Search_Title: Dangote, Honeywell Join MTN in Leboku Festival
Headline: Nigeria: Dangote, Honeywell Join MTN in Leboku Festival
Body_Text:
The Cross River Government says that this year's Yakurr International Leboku New Yam Festival will witness more private sector participation.
MTN Nigeria has been a regular sponsor of the festival in years past by this year;s event bill for next week August 26, will also have the involvement of other major private concerns like Dangote and Honeywell companies.
The Special Assistant to the Governor on Events Management, Mr Totsman Etoty disclosed this on Wednesday in Ugep, Yakurr Local Government Area during the pre-event press briefing.
Etoty, who was represented by Mr Ekpenyong Ojoi also said that, the international festival was now on facebook and U-tube courtesy of the official sponsors MTN Nig.
"As I speak with you now, Leboku International festival is on facebook and you can also browse it on facebook. Again this year's festival will witness a lot of corporate participants including, Dangote group, Honeywell among others.
"All these are part of innovations that the State Government is putting in place to ensure the continuous preservation of the festival, " he said.
He solicited the continued cooperating and support of the people of Ugep and Yakurr in the preservation and celebration of the annual festival.
According to him, the festival has brought development closer to the people through increase in the volume of investment in Ugep and participating communities.
Etoty further said that the government has introduced an independent assessment method in the selection of winners of the new yam festival to ensure government's neutrality.

Original_Search_Title: NFF's Revolving Door of Treacherous Back-Biting
Headline: Nigeria: NFF's Revolving Door of Treacherous Back-Biting
Body_Text:
There are several aspects of the melodramatic impeachment movie scripted, produced and directed by a caucus of government officials and mutinous members of the executive committee of the Nigeria Football Federation (NFF) that left a very sour taste in the mouth. Firstly, the majority members who voted to oust NFF President Aminu Maigari, after reviewing grave allegations of financial impropriety, high-handedness and misgovernance leveled against him, wanted to be clever by half by failing to forward the resolution to the General Congress for ratification, as required by extant statutes concerning such matters.
It doesn't require rocket science to understand why they failed to do so. Truth's that Maigari outsmarted them by quickly drumming up solid support among the chairmen of state football associations, who constitute a clear majority of members of the general congress, culminating in what Afrobeat founder and exponent Fela Anikulapo-Kuti described as 'stalemate'! Lawyers say that he who comes to equity must do so with clean hands or as Ndigbo would say, one can never mold clean, white pounded yam with soiled palms.
Secondly, it was quite bemusing to hear veteran sports journalists, seasoned sports administrators and government officials, who ought to know better, mouth the inanity that our laws are superior to FIFA constitution. Let me even agree, without conceding, that their claim is correct. Shouldn't those mealy-mouthing such a gross hypothesis realize by now that in the real-politik of an increasingly unipolar world, it is only a nation like the United States of America, that unquestionably wags the global state of affairs with its tail, that can muster the muscle required to make such a claim stick? If they still have any doubts, they should simply consider the ceding of Bakassi peninsula to Cameroon in line with the ruling of the International Court of Justice and what became of the noise generated within our shores for the Green Tree Treaty to be first domiciled in consonance with the nation's constitution before the ruling can be effected.
Thirdly, membership of any association - be it domestic or international - is by choice. Joining an international organization such as FIFA is not by force. But once any nation freely opts to become a member, it has, to all intents and purposes, accepted to subordinate specific portions of its laws that clash with constitutional provisions of such a multinational organization to those of the latter - except where exemptions have been clearly codified in a document as part of pre-membership negotiations.
Fourthly, the dubious and destabilizing roles successive governments have played in the administration of football must continue to be roundly condemned. Isn't it shameful that it is largely the same government agencies that instigated the ouster of Sani Lulu and ushered in a then executive committee member in the person of Maigari as his successor that have now remote-controlled the latter's colleagues to equally impeach him?
That's exactly the way the cookie crumbles. Government would handpick an individual whose major qualification for the job is displaying a disproportionately high coefficient of docility and malleability, only to start the process of shoving him aside, as soon as he begins to show signs of rudimentary independence, and manipulating yet another stooge as successor. This has been, and will continue to represent, the story of the NFF's revolving door of treachery, intrigues and subterfuge as written and promoted by government officials.
There's no doubt that the situation in Nigeria is not different from any other African country where the government exercises monopolistic control over national resources. Given such a scenario, it is understandable for government to be the primary financier of sporting activities, especially football. And since it is a trite saying that he who pays the piper dictates the tune, it is equally understandable that government would seek to have more than a passing interest in the individuals who serve as administrators of the various sporting associations. What I, however, completely disagree with is the attitude of government that seems to say that government patronage and meritocracy are antagonistic and mutually exclusive.
Why do successive governments act as if it is a feat to find well-qualified and experienced individuals within their circle of influence, out of an estimated population of 170million Nigerians, who can properly run a handful of sporting associations? The truth is that they have never been truly interested in moving Nigerian sports forward, despite mouthing popular clichés and sound bites from time to time. The number of times this nation has been privileged to have round administrative pegs manning round sporting holes can be counted on the fingers of one hand! This is hardly surprising considering that the sporting sector is only a microcosm of a national polity ravaged by primordial sentiments and the enthronement of mediocrity and impunity.
If you ask me, I'd readily volunteer that we owe international organizations such as FIFA a lot. If successive governments were strictly left alone, this nation would have long transformed into a Hobbesian state where life is very short and brutish. The reign of executive rascality and impunity has become so rampant and pervasive that a good majority of Nigerians have come to timidly accept that government is always right - even when it is clearly obvious that the emperor trotting around the market-place in what he claims to be specially-designed clothes is totally naked - and that it is futile to 'fight' government. But what Nigerians in a supposedly democratic environment couldn't get their government to deliver on, international organizations like FIFA are enforcing. Shame on our leaders and shame on us all!
All said and done, Maigari should be magnanimous enough to implement a give-and-take policy so that the ongoing laudable programmes he has midwife won't be jeopardized by in-house blood-letting and/or sanctions imposed by FIFA. His traducers would do well to sheathe their swords and bid their own time sine there can't be two captains in a boat at the same time.

Original_Search_Title: Okorocha Attends New Yam Festival in Mbaise
Headline: Nigeria: Okorocha Attends New Yam Festival in Mbaise
Body_Text:
Owerri — The governor at the event itemized all the projects and other gestures his administration has extended to the three local governments that make up the Mbaise ethnic nationality.
The people of the area mostly youths had expressed excitement on the arrival of the at the Chioma Ajunwa Stadium in Ahiazu Mbaise Local Government Area where the Iriji Mbaise festival for 2014 edition took place, with politicians across the political parties in attendance at the function. The Chairman of the Mbaise Council of Traditional Rulers, Eze Chidume Okoro, at the function, praised Governor Okorocha for starting work at the site of Mbaise/Ngor Okpala Campus of Imo State University and for the speedy transformation of the state with massive infrastructural development, road network, city transformation, free education and so on.
Though the state is yet to rehabilitate the major road in Mbaise that links Aba Branch in Mbano through Mbaise to Okpala, the monarch added that the Mbaise nation would remain appreciative of the achievements of the governor. He pledged the loyalty of Mbaise people to the governor. Governor Okorocha who was invited to address the people of Mbaise gave graphic account of all that his Rescue Mission administration has done for Mbaise people, adding that starting work at the campus of Imo State University in Mbaise/Ngor Okpala axis of the state was to demonstrate his love for the people of Mbaise.
He said he loves Mbaise people more than some of their sons in power because he has a lot of Mbaise sons and daughters who have benefitted from his personal free education project through Rochas Foundation with most of them having graduated from the university.
"Mbaise people are the greatest beneficiaries of the free education policy of my administration because of their population.

Original_Search_Title: Okorocha, Ihedioha's Supporters Clash At Mbaise New Yam Festival
Headline: Nigeria: Okorocha, Ihedioha's Supporters Clash At Mbaise New Yam Festival
Body_Text:
This year's new yam festival organised by the people of Mbaise in Imo State, which took place at the weekend in Ahiazu Mbaise was literally turned into a battle field following a violent clash between supporters of the state governor, Rochas Anayo Okorocha, and that of the deputy speaker, House of Representatives. Hon. Emeka Ihedioha.
It was not clear what caused the fight, nevertheless not a few attributed it to the contest for power between the two politicians ahead of the 2015 general elections.
The minister of state for foreign affairs, Prof. Viola Onwuliri, who attended the event, blamed Governor Okorocha for not controlling his security aides, who according to her displayed raw power during the clash that nearly, disrupted the event.
Governor Okorocha allegedly stormed out of the venue of the event immediately after the fight abated, an action described by Prof. Onwuliri as disrespectful to the Mbaise ethnic group.
She said, "From the conduct of the governor, it was obvious that he was not interested in the festival, but just came there to create confusion and derail the event.
"Since Okorocha assumed office in 2011, he has never attended the Iri ji Mbaise (new yam festival), until this one that precedes the 2015 general elections. This is not the ground for venting personal animosity.
"He (governor) even left before the actual ceremony began. He just brought trouble to the event. and this is an embarrassment to the state and its people. I am positive that by 2015, we will all have a chance to redress this anomaly".

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan - Okorocha Is an Embarrassment to Democracy
Headline: Nigeria: Jonathan - Okorocha Is an Embarrassment to Democracy
Body_Text:
President Goodluck Jonathan has described Governor Rochas Okorocha of Imo State as an embarrassment to democracy and a disaster waiting to happen.
Jonathan, who was represented by the Foreign Affairs Minister, 1, Professor Viola Onwuliri, at the Iriji Mbaise New Yam Festival, spoke to journalists after the event saying from the conduct of the governor, it was obvious that he was not interested in the festival but just came to create confusion and derail the event.
She said: "Since Okorocha assumed office in 2011, he has never attended the Iriji Mbaise until this one that precedes the election year. This is not the ground for venting personal animosities. He even left before the actual ceremony began. He just brought trouble to the event and this is an embarrassment to the state and its people. I am positive that by 2015, we will all have a chance to redress this anomaly."
The 2014 Yam Festival, at some point, turned into a political rally of sorts with Okorocha and the Deputy Speaker of the House of Representatives, Chief Emeka Ihedioha, relentlessly fought for supremacy.
It was apparent however that the Mbaise nation was not too receptive to the fact that the governor tried to engage their son in their own soil. Okorocha's microphone was intermitently switched off for as long as five minutes, as the people jeered the governor.
Receiving the governor to Mbaise, Ihedioha pointedly told Okorocha that he (Okorocha) would as a matter of necessity hand over to him in 2015, noting that the governor's exit was not negotiable.
Ihedioha also held the governor to task on what he described as "lack of state government presence in Mbaise." According to the deputy speaker, "The major projects in Mbaise are those executed by the federal government. In the few areas credited to the state government, we only see projects that are very poorly executed.
"It is evident that the governor has run out of ideas, so as a matter of expediency, it is expected that he would hand over the reins in 2015 so we can help out."
Responding, an angry Okorocha said: "The green cap (referring to Ihedioha's trademark cap) will not make anybody governor of Imo State and as a matter of fact, there is nobody in the Imo State Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) who is his match in an electoral contest. My three years in office is more productive than the 12 years spent in the state by the PDP. Imo people have rejected PDP because PDP is worse than the Ebola virus."
Okorocha's microphone was switched off again when he said he had laid the foundation for a campus of the Imo State University to be sited in Aboh Mbaise/Ngor Okpala federal constituency.
The crowd booed and what they described as a Greek gift that was intended for political convenience.
The confusion degenerated into a free for all between Okorocha's supporters and those of Ihedioha, but the security personnel on ground contained it in good time before whisking the governor away to safety.
While many believed Okorocha fled the event long before the breaking of kola nuts and the address of the president, his supporters said he left for another assignment.
Meanwhile, Ihedioha has asked Okorocha to apologise to the Mbaise people for defiling their custom and tradition.
The deputy speaker said this in reaction to a statement by the state government which asked Ihedioha to apologise to the governor for the embarrassment and disgrace the latter he at the event.
"The deputy speaker and other Mbaise leaders believed that it was the governor who actually owes the Mbaise people an apology for disrupting the event after coming late in his characteristic manner and defiling the treasured cultural values and tradition of Iri Iji Mbaise," a statement by Chibuike Onyeukwu issued on behalf of the deputy speaker said.
Ihedioha said it was a blatant lie the claim by the state government that he did not welcome the governor to the event, noting that even though Okorocha had an ulterior motive for attending the festival only after spurning invitations in the past three years, he still gave him the courtesy of welcoming him as chief guest of honour.
The statement clarified: "It is on record (and for the avoidance of doubt, the tape of the event can be cross checked by members of the public) that the deputy speaker did not only welcome Okorocha to the event but also enjoined him to accept the kolanut offered to him as chief guest of honour.
"This was in spite of suspicion of an ulterior motive by the Mbaise people on the part of the governor who had found it convenient to ignore several invitations to attend 'Iri Iji' Mbaise in the past three years suddenly decided to attend this year's edition for obviously sinister political reasons. The Mbaise people and friends of the nation present at the event easily saw through this facade and pretence."
Ihedioha said the governor's recourse to attacking him simply confirms the fact that "the governor holds him (Ihedioha) in trepidation and is at a loss as to how to address his growing popularity and acceptance among the Imo people vis-a-vis his (Okorocha's) fast declining rating among the people."

Original_Search_Title: Mayhem Averted As Okorocha's Supporters, Mbaise Youths Clash
Headline: Nigeria: Mayhem Averted As Okorocha's Supporters, Mbaise Youths Clash
Body_Text:
Owerri — Scores of security personnel present at the 2014 New Yam Festival of Mbaise people had a hectic time at the weekend, in stopping what could have degenerated to a full scale war between the supporters of Governor Rochas Okorocha and Mbaise youths.
Earlier in his welcome address, the Deputy Speaker, Chief Emeka Ihedioha, who chaired the event, reminded the Governor of the palpable lack of state government presence in Mbaise.
He pointed out that "the major projects in Mbaise are those executed by the Federal Government", adding that "as a matter of expediency, it is expected that you (Okorocha) will hand over the reins of state administration in 2015, so we can help out."
Responding, an obviously angry Governor said: "There is nobody in Imo PDP that is my match in an electoral contest. My three years in office is more productive than the 12 years spent in Imo by the PDP. Imo people have rejected PDP because PDP is worse than the Ebola virus".
The booing multiplied when Okorocha said he had laid the foundation for a campus of the Imo State University to be sited in Aboh Mbaise/ Ngor Okpala federal constituency.
The crowd became uncontrollable when the confusion quickly degenerated into a free for all between Okorocha's supporters and Mbaise youths, while the Governor was whisked away into safety before the unveiling of the yam barn and cutting of the new yam.
Speaking to newsmen at the scene of the near ugly incident, the Foreign Affairs Minister 1, Professor Viola Onwuliri, who represented President Goodluck Jonathan during the ceremony, lamented that "from what transpired here, it is evident that the Governor was not interested in the festival but came to create confusion and derail the event"
It was the considered opinion of the Minister that "since the Governor assumed office in 2011, he has never attended the Iri ji Mbaise until this one that precedes the election year".
Continuing, Professor Onwuliri said: "This is not the ground for venting personal animosities. He even left before the actual ceremony began. He just brought trouble to the event and this is an embarrassment to the state and its people. I am positive that by 2015, we will all have a chance to redress this anomaly".

Original_Search_Title: I'll Adopt Fashola's Style for Abia - Abaribe
Headline: Nigeria: I'll Adopt Fashola's Style for Abia - Abaribe
Body_Text:
Chairman, Senate Committee on Media, Information and Public Affairs, Enyinnaya Abaribe, represents Abia South Senatorial District in the Senate. In this interview with journalists in Abuja, Abaribe said the incumbent governor of Abia State, Theodore Orji, will never be an impediment to his aspiration to be the next governor of the state. He also spoke on other sundry issues. Our correspondents IGNATIUS OKOROCHA and CHIBUZOR EMEJOR were there. Excerpts:
What is the motive behind your governorship ambition?
I am in the race because I am the most qualified of the 12 aspirants contesting the seat and I must tell you that every other person in the race is a pretender. My reason for contesting the office of the governor is: First, as a person from Abia South Senatorial Zone where it is generally believed the position had been zoned to. Secondly, it does not really matter which part of Abia State the governor or candidate in the 2015 elections comes from - Abia North, Abia Central or Abia South. I believe that I would floor any other candidate at the elections because from political experience to academic experience, and even popularity, I will score the highest votes in Abia State.
They said it is the turn of Abia South Senatorial zone; but, it doesn't matter where you come from to be governor. Among all who want to be governor now, if you place me side by side with any of them by whatever criteria, there is no way I will be found wanting. My coming from Abia South senatorial zone, where it is now zoned to, is just an icing to the cake; otherwise, I am eminently qualified to be the governor. The icing is just an added advantage.
It is purely the act of God that I am from Abia South, otherwise, in 2003 I ran for the office, which means I have been qualified before now. Now that the thing has been zoned to Abia South, in Ngwaland, I can't tell you my joy. The Pentecostal flock in Christendom would say, 'I claim it in Jesus name, Amen.'
Why do you think you are better qualified than the others?
My governorship qualities are such that others don't have. I believe that anybody who desires to be governor of Abia State must understand the state and must have been part of the process as a deputy governor. Having directly experienced and witnessed every process of governance at the level of governor of the state. I have a fairly good idea of what the thing is all about. Usually, people who sit in Abia and operate from there, having no other external input, have a narrow perspective of it compared to somebody who operates from outside, say, Abuja. Like you journalists in Abuja, your perspective about Abia State now is wider compared to the journalist in Abia State.
You could make comparison, but the one in Abia, all he does is to collect bulletin from Government House, and if he has any scoop he will be intimidated into not publishing it. It is not the same with the journalists in Abuja. Here, when you get a scoop, what you do first is to file it and start working on it because the scoop can enhance your job.
So for me, having been in Abuja as a Senator for eight years, my perspective is wider and my understanding is better and different because I understand what Abia State is to the nation. I understand too that anybody who will be the governor of that state must be a player at the national level, so he can leverage on it. We cannot afford a local player who does not understand the way. Otherwise, when he comes to Abuja, he will be looking for who will be in the liaison office here and who will in turn serve as the directional agent to him here, who will probably take advantage of him and reap Abians off.
In 2001, we had pipes imported by Chief Samuel Mbakwe's regime to do pipe borne water all over Abia State. They came in within the military era and were stacked in Umuahia in multiples of thousands. Governor Uzo Kalu sold all of them to oil firms and rigging companies during the time of the investment.
My point is that they left there to come here in other to acquire what they need. They come to Aba for whatever fittings band repairs they need except for the ones they do abroad. We provide skilled man power, and that has always been there, but importantly, we have not been able to factor into this gigantic economic advantage, rather more emphasis is laid on the feeding bottle system were everybody goes to Abuja every month to collect subvention. When there is a delay, nothing happens in the states and local governments.
There is parlance in our local government system, if you complain to the chairman of not being paid, he will tell you 'It has not dropped,' meaning the allocation from Abuja has not dropped. All these have a negative impact because oil theft will continue, breakages, pipes will be aging and other things and of course the budget is predicated on 2.3mb per day, 450,000 for local consumption and others go for export.
After all the calculations and deductions by NNPC and their joint venture partners, Abia State gets about N4 to N5 billion depending on those variables. The overhead wages in Abia is about 50 per cent of that and we have about N2 billion for capital project and others. If you have a capital budget of about N1billion a month that is just 10 km standard road at N100 million per km. it is a difficult situation.
But, you have to devise a means of getting out of such quagmire, and the only way out is that internal production must increase and taxes better collected and ultimately apply prudence in the way you manage the resources available to you and I make boast to say none is in a better position to do it better than me who is a trained economist, who knows the nitty-gritty of all, these things. That is why I am taking my time to go through all these with you.
The local government gets about the same amount. The internal generated revenue is about N600 million, which is very poor that is why we need prudence and good management in other to have good value for money and that is what I am offering Abians.
Why do some people think that you are a difficult person?
People think I am hard, but it is not true. I like to start with what is doable and then to what is possible and from there we'll find out that what we think is impossible is actually very possible. What I'm bringing is practical governance. A friend of mine (classmate) who is the traditional ruler of his village sometime ago could not pass to his village because of pool of water on the road. When he called me, we devised a solution. I bought a pumping machine, we drained the water, poured chippings and now the road is motorable instead of waiting for Federal Government and complaining.
Today my assignment for you is very simple, go and conduct a search on me. You have the information of all the places I have worked before, go and find out who knows me and then draw your conclusion. Made your judgement on who Enyinnaya Abaribe is.
What about the factor of the state governor?
Let me help you, one of the things you will hear in the course of this assignment is that I am in enmity with His Excellency T. A. Orji, the incumbent governor of Abia State. And as a result of the enmity, he won't let me to be governor. All these are demonic concoction from the pit of hell. You know he was the Chief of Staff to the Government House when I was the Deputy Governor. At that time, he called an answered me 'Sir'. Today, I call and answer him 'Sir'. The only thing then was that I refused to work with him as long as he was with Uzor Kalu, the governor then, and that anytime he is free from his shackles, we would make up.
When he got his liberty and joined PDP, all of us welcomed him and has been working together, And so, I don't know where all these stories are coming from. So, I promise you, based on the assurance he gave to me, he will never be an impediment to my aspiration to Government House in Umuahia and that all he assures me was that it is the turn of Abia South senatorial zone. Anybody that wins the primary will enjoy his support. I will go by his word. I'm also doing all my consultations since the level now is primary and it is an internal thing.
Some local governments belonging to Ngwa people are found in Abia Central Zone, while others are in Abia South Zone. There is also this rivalry between the Ukwa Ngwa people and the rest of the Ngwa in the zone. There are speculations that disenfranchised Ngwa people in Abia Central and the Ukwa Ngwa are hoping to play spoilers to the zoning of the governorship to the Abia South. How do you see it?
Yes, Ukwa Ngwa spanned from Bende in Ikwuano (old administrative head) to Oyigbo in present River State. Justice Mamman Nasir, in trying to do boundary adjustment, used the Akwete River by simply drawing a line on it, not putting into consideration our Ngwa brethren who live in the other side in Rivers State, called Obigbo (now Oyibo).
The boundary became Imo River via Akwete. Irrespective of all these boundary brouhaha the Constitution recognises Wards, Local Government, Senatorial Zones and States. The boundary problems between Ukwa Ngwa and Bende are enormous. For the records, Bende includes Ebonyi State and part of Imo State. But for this purpose, what the Constitution recognises is Senatorial District.
There is another group, which says that Ngwa defiled zoning formula when they contested during the time the Abia State governorship seat was zoned to other senatorial zones. Does this explain why politicians from other senatorial zones are jumping into the ring?
Let me make it very clear that Ngwa is one; whether in the Central or Southern senatorial zones, Ngwa is one. That was why I told you when I started that I am not coming as a candidate from Abia South senatorial, but as an Abian. The senatorial segment is only an icing on the cake. I am well aware under Section 42(d) of the Nigerian Constitution that you cannot discriminate against anybody based on gender, sex or tribe.
Once you are qualified, come and buy form to contest. But, when we get to the field, Abians who are delegates, will have the final say. That was what we did for President Ebele Jonathan when we all agreed that he would run. Some said no, but at last, what we agreed prevailed. These things are like that. When I contested for Senate in 2003, during my campaign I only noticed Nyerere Ayim who was campaigning on the platform of APGA, but when INEC released result I saw 12 candidates on the contest list.
So I asked where were others during the campaign period, including a particular candidate from Anambra state who contested from one funny party. All these people bought forms and went for INEC screening but obviously some of them were pretenders. Then (when the Ngwas contested for governorship) parties did not zone the office, but now it has been zoned. Anybody who thinks he can, let him buy form and come and contest.
While Ngwa people are saying it's their turn to produce governor of Abia State, there is a strong rumour that Governor Theodore Orji has an anointed candidate for the job - one man involved in sanitation business in Aba. What is your take on this?
On the rumour of the anointed candidate, the governor have gone on air to disabuse the mind of Abians that he has not anointed any candidate for the office, and one thing he has made abundantly clear is that he can never do like his predecessor, Uzor Kalu, who says one thing in the morning and claims another in the evening, and I hold Chief T.A. Orji, the Executive Governor of Abia State in high esteem for that.
If you become Abia State governor, what will you do about the oil wealth and revenue accruing to it in the hands of River State? Will you allow the local governments to function well by releasing their funds to them because they don't get their fund?
I have answered the first question before, but by way of redressing it, I told you that I have been in Abuja working at the highest level of governance for eight years. Like I said, anybody who will be the governor of Abia State must not be a novice, but understands the machineries and workings of government. He must possess some level of competence to relate with the Boundary Commission, which makes boundary demarcation and the Revenue Mobilisation and Fiscal Commission, which shares revenue to states. If you know the process like me, I promise you we will get our dues. On the local government, I must tell you they get their money and I will tell you how. My local government used to have 1,520 workers on record, but anytime you go there you can never see up to 100 people. So, where are the rest of the workers? They just fill up the role of the local government with fictitious names. They only show up when there is verification exercise or collection of salaries for their pay masters. These things have to do with the amount of supervision and managerial skill you are going to exercise as the governor.
What I expected you to ask was if I will conduct council election and stop using Transitional chairmen, who has no stake like elected officials, who can tell the people that he will do one-two-three and the people will hold him accountable. But, the problem is this feeding bottle system, where everybody is only interested in sucking out of it, I told you my approach is simple. What is achievable? What is possible? By the time we are through with the doable and possible, you will find out that we have done everything we need to do.
You see, we have an abnormal situation where jobs are contracted out; workers are left redundant because no more job to do. This are verified with one of the Transitional Chairman, who told me the reason for this is that when you give job to employed workers, they divert fund, print their own receipts for revenue collection. So, it's better for them to award it to contractors. The only way to solve this fundamental problem is to go to the root of it I know very well, for a fundamental solution.
I will start with the picture in my hand which I took of my primary school that I left in 1980. My question in this respect is: What is the justification for PDP to ask to be voted again when we still have these kinds of structures starring us in the face? The second question: As a Senator of Federal Republic of Nigeria, why did you support what was due to others to be given to another..?
(Cuts in) What is it?
The Presidency was zoned to the North by your party, but due to circumstances which were no fault of theirs, you joined those who manipulated it in other to give it to Jonathan. Again, in 2003, you contested an election with the slogan "Otu onu" (one opportunity) and now you want to contest again, you have changed to "Maka Odi Nma Abia" (for the good of the Abians). Why?
'Otu Onu' wasn't a slogan. Our slogan then was 'Maka Odi Nma Abia'. But what happened then was that the government of Uzor Kalu stopped us from doing any media thing on radio. It's on record that I reported to Nigeria Broadcasting Corporation (NBC) and we needed a way to reach the masses of Abia. In any case, if you don't have power radio or TV advert is useless. If you use print media, how many people read newspaper? But, we needed a way to reach Abians and the only way was to go to them through music and that song came from one of our members during our campaign tour. We adopted and fine-tuned it then, printed 28,000 copies of it, gave them to buses, Okada riders and to the people for free. It became a household music for Abians. The symbol became popularised and through it, we built a mass movement of people. But it was still Odi Nma Abia, of course we called it 'Maka Odima Abia Organisation'.
What gives you confidence that you will win the governorship elections in Abia State in 2015?
In 2007, the person who ran against me spent N350 million as against my N28 million. He lost and wept in the open field. This thing is not in spending money, buying Jeeps for National Working Committee members of party. These people will not vote. The delegates that will vote in that primary are all these ordinary; people I'm always with, eating roasted yam and ground nut with. I know them and they know me. We will be in a familiar terrain that day in the field.
What is your agenda for education in Abia State?
Primary education is under Federal Government, through SUPEB to local governments. So, we will take this question to them. They have Education Secretary and Inspector at the local government headquarters. You know me; if I become governor and you bring this to me, some heads will roll.
There are 826,000 students in primary schools in Abia State, but that thing is distorted because it is supposed to be a pyramid. When you go into secondary school from primary, but in Abia, the reverse is the case. You have about 300,000 plus in primary schools, but in secondary school, it's 500,000 plus, and you ask why?
From my investigations, the problem is that our people have abandoned public schools for mushroom private schools located everywhere in the state, including one very close to my house here. You now ask: What is the Education Secretary or Inspector doing? It is the responsibility of the Educational Inspectorate to approve or disapprove these schools. But, anybody who becomes Chief Inspector turns a millionaire overnight; you know how and why. But, one thing is sure, if I become the governor and start my work, I will be unpopular. But, within three years, people will be congratulating me. When Governor Raji Fashola of Lagos State started work on the notorious Oshodi in Lagos, he was unpopular. But today, everybody is enjoying it. That reminds me, my wristwatch and my wife's necklace were taken from us on that road years back. But today, it's no longer the same.

Original_Search_Title: Imo High Court Warns Against Exploiting Powers of Judiciary
Headline: Nigeria: Imo High Court Warns Against Exploiting Powers of Judiciary
Body_Text:
Owerri — An Imo State High Court presided over by Justice K. A. Orjiako, has warned people who want to exploit the powers of the court to heat up the polity to desist from such act.
Justice Orjiako made his feelings known while reacting to the ex-parte motion, HOR/88/2014, brought before the court, by two agents of Imo State Government, Eze Samuel Ohiri and Edinburgh Olumba, seeking the leave of court to stop Eze Cletus Ilomuanya from celebrating the New Yam Festival of Obinugwu autonomous community.
When the matter first came up in the court, Orjiako said: "I have read through the motion ex-parte and I am of the humble view that the application is not one to be heard ex-parte".
He, therefore, ordered that "the respondent should appropriately be put on notice to enable him react accordingly", and subsequently adjourned the matter to August 14, 2014, "on agreement of both parties".
At the resumed hearing on Thursday, counsel to the claimants/applicants, Mike N. Okafor, told the court that he was not in court with the case file and could not confirm if the respondent had been properly served or not.
Counsel to the embattled royal father, Ogbonna C. Ajuzie, described the submission as "a grand design by the plaintiffs to miscarry justice and asked for N50, 000 cost.

Original_Search_Title: Enugu Centenary Celebrated in 2009 Was a Blunder, Says Historian
Headline: Nigeria: Enugu Centenary Celebrated in 2009 Was a Blunder, Says Historian
Body_Text:
Enugu — 'The Enugu centenary celebrated in 2009 by the Governor Sullivan Chime-led administration was a huge historical blunder in the sense that Enugu was founded on January 11, 1915, while coal was discovered in 1909.
It is important to put the records correct before people will be misinformed to believe that Enugu was founded in 1909.' This is the 'verdict' of a lawyer, researcher and historian, Mr. Anayo Enechukwu, who said he is quite knowledgeable about the history of Enugu State. He is the Chairman of International Trusts Organisation, a non-governmental organization said to have branches all over Africa. And his remark is coming about five years after the centenary celebration organised by the Enugu state government.
"Many people were in a hurry to celebrate the centenary thinking that the governor would not be there in 2015 based on his health challenges. So, they hurriedly arranged the centenary to be part of history without knowing that they were committing a historical blunder. It was bad advisers that misled the government.
"The centenary celebration was in error. The people that held the ceremony were not well informed. Enugu was founded on January 11, 1915. What they celebrated was the discovery of coal," Enechukwu in an an interview said.
The historian also recalled that way back in 1909, one Alfred E. Kingston, a British geologist and the principal of mineral survey in southern Nigeria, accompanied by E.O. Tiele, was looking for minerals in south-eastern Nigeria and coincidentally, they discovered coal not even in Enugu, but in Ofam
Ozalla. They later traced it to Enugu and gathered some of the materials. After testing it they discovered that it had rich quality.
"If you look at the scenario, you will find out that what the government celebrated was the discovery of coal."
As an historian, Enechukwu said he must have to correct the wrong impression, if for nothing else, at least for the young ones who might not bother to find out the truth.
He said: "I have to point out here that without the discovery of coal, there would not have been anything like Enugu. But before the discovery of coal, it is on record that coal was imported from Britain. There is an English proverb that 'you cannot take coal to Newcastle.' There was an agreement entered by sir Walter Edgerton, a commander and also governor of the colony of southern Nigeria to import coal from Britain. The agreement was entered on December 31, 1909 and it was to be done annually. That was the trend before the discovery of coal here."
According to him, coal was also discovered in Benin, Agbor, Ukpanam, Ogwashi-uku, Ukpor and Ichi, stressing that the one in Enugu was of better quality.
He said after the discovery coal, the white men left and nobody thought about doing any other thing. But one engineer G.A. Brown, the district engineer of public works department, PWD at Onitsha, was assigned with further testing of the coal.
"It was given to one Professor Gulsdan for analysis, which was discovered to be rich in quality and quantity. It was at this stage that the First World War started in 1914," he recalled.
According to the historian, the British were afraid that Adolf Hitler might attack them and possibly block their coal miners and thereby subject them to cold winter. "That was how they agreed to develop and use the Enugu coal as an alternative in case of any eventuality. But there was no way they could have transported coal from Enugu without first constructing a railway, so they did a research from River Niger and the Atlantic Ocean and found out that it was better to build the rail from the heart of Port Harcourt. That was in 1913, and it was Chief Onyeama and Chief Chukwuani Nwangwu, the two warrant chiefs installed by the British in 1908/1909 that were instrumental to the construction of the railway."
He recalled that the two chiefs used the local people to do the job, but the rail did not come to Enugu until 1915. The two chiefs supervised the first coal transported from Enugu, he said, pointing out that the rail did not reach Maiduguri until 1928.
"From this analysis, you will see that railway came to Enugu in 1915, but there was what was known as Udi revolt in 1914.
Ordinarily, it would have been called Awkunanaw or Nkanu revolt, but all these places were under Udi division established in 1909."
Explaining the cause of the revolt, he said it was carried out by the natives of Awkunanaw due to "their misconception of the white man being a spirit or a ghost." He recalled that the locals had wanted to chase the white people away, wondering how they could be living with "spirits who ate only eggs and yams, spoke through their noses, and exhibit strange characters."
However, the British subdued the rebels within seven days, he said. "I am saying all these things so that people will be better informed. When they wanted to establish a colliery in Enugu, the Obi of Onitsha gave them one Alfred Innoma and 19 other miners who later became the first inhabitants of Enugu, especially the area known as Ugwu Alfred. Researchers will agree with me that there has been a lot of distortion in the history of Enugu by uninformed persons," Enechukwu said point-blank.
Enechukwu said: "According to engineer William John Lake, the first European that arrived in Enugu also the first colliery manager between 1915 and 1942, Enugu was officially established on January 11, 1915. I stand to be contradicted. This engineer arrived Onitsha from Lagos and proceeded to Udi on January 7, 1915 with 350 human carriers and arrived on January 9. He came to Enugu on January 11 with military escort for fear of another revolt. This is why I still insist that Enugu was founded on January 15, 1915.
"There, they built their quarters and the African market. The market was for miners and other people around, but because of the noise the white men relocated the market to the present Ogbete main market, previously owned by the Catholic Church."
According to him, the first and second maps of the town were drawn in 1913 and 1917 to erect some structures.

Original_Search_Title: Agulu Bends Age-Long Tradition for Akunyili's Burial
Headline: Nigeria: Agulu Bends Age-Long Tradition for Akunyili's Burial
Body_Text:
Awka — Kinsmen of late former Minister of Information, Professor Dora Akunyili, in Agulu, Anaocha local government area of Anambra State, have set aside the customs guiding their age-long traditional New Yam Festival to ensure that she was given a befitting burial on August 28.
According to the custom of Agulu people, burial ceremonies were not allowed to hold during the period of New Yam festival, which usually kicks off after the second week of August and lasts for two weeks.
Last week, the chief mourner and late Professor Akunyili's husband, Dr Chike Akunyili, announced the programme for her burial, which holds on August 28 at their country home in Agulu town.
Although the date fell within the period of the New Yam Festival, Sunday Independent reliably gathered that the leadership of Agulu Town Union, under the President General, Sir Paulinus Anagbaso, set aside the constitution of the town to accommodate the burial within the period of the festival.
According to our source in the town, normally only things of joy like traditional marriages, title taking, and parties among others, were allowed to hold during the period of the festival, while burial ceremonies were forbidden.
"But, because we were told that the President chose August 28 as the most convenient date for him to attend the burial, an emergency meeting of the town union was summoned and the meeting agreed to set aside the constitution of our town to accommodate the burial", our source stated.
Sunday Independent also gathered that the elders of the town and the traditional ruler also gave their consent to the decision of the town union.
When our correspondent contacted the Agulu Town Union President General, Anagboso, popularly known as Isimmili Agulu, he confirmed that the constitution of the town was set aside to bury Akunyili because of her status as an international figure, who served the country very well in her lifetime.
"We allowed it (burial) to hold to honour her. She is somebody celebrated all over the world.
"We called meeting of the town union and set aside our constitution to honour her", Anagbaoso stated.
He however urged the Federal Government, Anambra State and Anaocha Local Government to immortalise the former minister, adding that her legacies should not die with her.
Anagboso said the town union would do everything on its part to ensure that the burial would be hitch-free.

Original_Search_Title: 10 Things Men Hate When Out On a Date
Headline: Nigeria: 10 Things Men Hate When Out On a Date
Body_Text:
Recently, I wrote on the ten things ladies hate when out on a date to give the men a little expo on what not to do. This week, I have given the men a chance to express themselves by talking to different men on what they dislike when they take a lady out on a date. I got really interesting feedbacks I must say. Let's begin!
1. BRINGING YOUR FRIEND ALONG: This particular point was always at the tip of men's lips as soon as I asked the question on what they dislike on dates. Most girls are guilty of this. A guy asks you out on a movie date, dinner date or whatever the case may be, and some ladies deem it fit to bring their friends along. Well ladies, the guys hate this; so, don't do it again if you are guilty.
2. LOOKING TACKY OR UNKEMPT: We know we have bad hair days sometimes as ladies. Before you go on a date, make sure you look nice. Straighten those edges, keep your nails well-manicured or painted and wow your date. Guys love their dates being admired.
3. TALKING TOO MUCH: Some ladies don't know when to stop talking. When you are out on a date with someone you just met, you don't want to give him your whole life history. Leave him wondering what is more to you that makes you awesome. I'm not saying keep mute or don't engage in conversations, no one likes that either. Just try to be comfortable.
4. PRETENDERS: This was another re-occurring issue for the guys. Nigerian guys call it "forming". You go out on a date with this cute guy and because you want to look like a "big girl," you say things like "I don't eat swallow." Meanwhile, you had pounded yam and egusi soup for lunch. I watched a skit on YouTube where a girl was talking to her friends and said "I prefer apple to Mac." If you know you are guilty of this, all I can say to you is "There is God o!"
5. INCONSIDERATE BABES: Ladies, please, when out on a date with a guy, order moderately. It's not fair when you go out without ordering according to the man's pocket. Do unto others what you want them to do unto you.
6. BAD EATING HABITS: These drives most men crazy; eating with your mouth open, making sounds while chewing, talking while eating and so on. Do not put your date in an uncomfortable position .Try to always chew with your mouth closed and if you really need to talk, wait till you swallow the food in your mouth first. If you don't know how to chew food, practice at home in front of the mirror or better still, ask a good friend who will tell you the truth. This should help.
7. TALKING ABOUT THE EX: This is not a good sign for both men and women. Talking about the ex on a date is a no go area! There is a reason why they are called "ex," so move on.
8. CHATTING ON YOUR PHONE OR LENGTHY PHONE CALLS: This is not a good look when out on a date. This could be interpreted to mean you are not interested in your date. Keep your phones away and have a healthy conversation.
9. BUYING TAKE-AWAY FOR FRIENDS: Your date is over and you think it's time to order for your friends at home or in the hostel. Put one thing in mind, he is taking "YOU" out on the date and not your friends. If your friends are hungry, let them get their own date or buy "Mama Put" if they are hungry. They would not lose hair on their bodies if you don't buy for them.
10. SAY THANK YOU: This is really important ladies. After a nice day or evening out, a simple thank you won't hurt.

Original_Search_Title: How Western Culture Erodes Values Among Nigerian Youths
Headline: Nigeria: How Western Culture Erodes Values Among Nigerian Youths
Body_Text:
Cultural imperialism is a term used to describe the imposition of western culture on the culture of people in developing countries. Cultural imperialism often refers to America's cultural influence on other countries, for better or worse.
A man's right lies in his confidence and personality which is greatly nurtured by his culture. Man is essentially the product of his culture. It beats and moulds a person into a particular shape. Culture makes one think, speak, dance and behave in a particular manner. It's culture that makes one Kanuri, Jukun, Hausa, Igbo, Yoruba, Alago, Tiv, etc.
Africa as a continent and Nigeria as a country have their given cultures which are unique in their own respect. This culture may not be absolutely the same among all countries and even within a country, a lot of cultures exist but there are features that differentiate Africans from other people of the world. The issue here is that any person with passion for the African culture today will believe that, the very material, spiritual and aesthetic fabric of African people had been destroyed by western civilization.
Nigeria which is made up of rich and diverse cultural values is beginning to lose most its cultural ideals especially through the adoption of foreign culture showcased through the mass media. Nowadays, it is observed that our young men and women have abandoned our local attires such as the 'Adire' and 'Ankara' for alien attires. It is common to see young people walking across the streets half nude putting on micro-mini skirts, handless top, trousers and other outfits that are not part of our cultural identity.
Africans and indeed Nigerians have their traditional attires, but how many people proudly wear such traditional attires today? A visit to our banks today will give you a perfect example. In fact, it is mandatory in the banks that you wear a suit except on Fridays that you are allowed to wear native attire. The question we must answer is whether wearing of suit part is of African culture or western culture? Must we copy everything western? Can't we promote our rich cultural and traditional outfit so our local industries can boom?
The dress code aspect is of importance in our present situation especially now that there is a global economic meltdown coupled with the need to be inward looking in exploring and exploiting the potential inherent in our local fabrics particularly the "Ankara". A paradigm shift from nude dress code which characterizes the dress code today will not only boost the country's economy but will go a long way in generating employment opportunities for the teaming unemployed youths in our society today.
It is disheartening that most Nigerians no longer patronize shoes, clothes etc made in Nigeria. They often tagged them as "Made in Aba". This will not help our country's economic growth because, when we do not patronize our locally manufactured product, our economic fortunes would continue to dwindle. We must take pride in our homemade product rather than rushing for foreign materials.
Another negative effect of cultural imperialism is the rising cases of immoral dressing and nudity especially amongst the youths. These have also occasioned rapid rate of sexual harassments among students and lecturers in the tertiary institutions as well as bosses and their employees in the offices. Raping of girls has been on the increase in our society today due to recklessnesses in dressing amongst our ladies all in the name of copying western fashions.
Apart from losing our cultural and traditional attires and dressing code to western imperialism, we have also lost our local food delicacies as well as our traditional worship to the alien culture. The kind of food we eat and even the mode of worship in our churches today have been altered. For example, the Igbo's are known for "Akpu", the Yoruba are known for "Amala" and pounded yam, the Igarras are known for "Uka" and the likes. We have our traditional food, but we discover today that most Nigeria people are no longer interested in their local food as they now prefer the western food.
More so, our local dialects are gradually going into extinction due to preference for foreign language. The younger generations can hardly communicate in their local dialects. White men colonized Africa; those colonial masters arrived Africa with their own culture and languages, which Africans consciously and unconsciously imbibed. In the process of accepting the new culture and language, they forgot to sift carefully and select the important and discard the less important ones. They forget the existence of their true (culture and language). The colonial masters provided Africans with their own language and painted array of benefits that would accrue him (the African) once he mastered the white man's language and culture.
Today, many people are even ashamed to speak their local dialect in the public. They prefer to speak English because they believe that is the best language to speak. We often see couples who hail from the same village in Nigeria whose children cannot speak their native language instead, they speak English and even in most cases, they do not speak simple and correct English but pidgin. What an aberration!
Our cultural heritages have been watered down by western civilization. Gone were days where life was treasured and values were welcome and held with high level of respect. As of then, parents hide their faces in shame when their daughters are found out to be non-virgin by their husbands during wedlock. The notions of chastity and virginity have been rendered absurd. The moral excellence of Africa/Nigeria society has overnight transformed into moral decay. Elders are no longer respected; our rich cultural festivals and ceremonies are now seen as "old school".
The mass media are the core influence of cultural imperialism on Nigeria society today. Nigerians , especially the youth now imitate values like gansterism, bribery, drug addiction, rape, semi-nudity, etc. These values have dangerous effects on the youths, who ignorantly mimic them as a way of life.
The mass media in this case is seen as media imperialism which is described as the over-concentration of the mass media from larger nations as a significant variable in negatively affecting smaller nations, in which the national identity of smaller nations is lessened or lost due to media homogeneity in mass media from the larger countries.
One of the negative roles the mass media plays in our today's society is the dominance and propagation of violence particularly on television and video film industry. A lot of the foreign film content on television is mainly scenes of violence. The actors who engage in murder and other violent actors are portrayed as heroes. Home video, which is very popular now with Nigerian films, also carries the same theme. Research has shown that children who watch violence on screen are most prone to violent behavior in adulthood.
The proliferation of pornographic films in our society is also another negative issue of this cultural imperialism. Imported 'blue' movies as they are known are as popular as other movies circulating among both adults and youths. These movies corrupt and defile the minds of people-patronizing them is a practice alien to our society. Public discussion of sex for us is regarded as a taboo, which should not be practiced.
What is the way out? Since Africans/Nigerians have inherited enough from her foreign colonizers, it is time for us to start appreciating these things and blending them into traditional Nigeria society in order to bear a trade mark of our own. Gone were the days where local made product are cherished. Now we tend to enjoy using foreign product to the detriment of our own local made products.
We should be on the revival of traditional morality which our country Nigeria was known for. Parents here should play the major role of ensuring moral behaviour of their children, in paying attention to the kind of films they watch, the kind of clothes they buy for them or they are allowed to wear. This in the long run will surely help-eradicate moral vices and crimes in our society. It is high time we said no to cultural imperialism and uphold our rich cultural values in high esteem
Deborah Yakubu Dangana is a Mass Communication student at the Taraba State University, Jalingo.

Original_Search_Title: Analysts Forecast Lower Inflation for July
Headline: Nigeria: Analysts Forecast Lower Inflation for July
Body_Text:
Analysts in FSDH Research have forecasted a lower inflation rate of 8.1 per cent for July, ahead of the expected release of the figure for the month by the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS). The current inflation rate stands at 8.2 per cent.
The researchers hinged their forecast on the analysis of the average prices of a basket of consumer goods selected from across the country monitored by the FSDH Research in July 2014.
The report showed that there was a general decrease in the prices of essential food items during the period due mainly to suppressed demand during the Ramadan Season.
According to the analysts, there were decreases of 20.32%, 16.67%, 10.48%, 9.52%, 8.33% and 4.76% in the average prices of Irish potato, sweet potato, garri, vegetable oil, beans and yam respectively
However, there were increases of 14.68%, 12.27% and 7.14% in the prices of fish, tomato and onions respectively; while price of rice closed unchanged.
"The movement in the prices of food items in July 2014 resulted in 0.45% increase in our Food and Non-Alcoholic Index to 162.23 points," the report said.
The report further said it noticed an increase in the prices of clothing and footwear, recreation and culture, restaurants/hotels and others between July 2014 and June 2014, on account of the celebration of the end of the Ramadan.
"Our model indicates that the price movements in the consumer goods in July 2014 would increase the Consumer Price Index (CPI) to 159.41 points, representing a month-on-month increase of 0.49%. We estimate that the increase in the CPI in July will produce an inflation rate (year-on-year) of 8.1%, lower than the June 2014 figure," it added.

Original_Search_Title: Ondo Monarch Warns Against Violence in 2015 Polls
Headline: Nigeria: Ondo Monarch Warns Against Violence in 2015 Polls
Body_Text:
The Olubaka of Oka Kingdom in Ondo State, Oba Yusuf Adebori Adeleye has called on youth of the community to avoid being used as instruments of destruction as the 2015 elections approach.
He made this call on Saturday at the annual Oka Day and New Yam Festival which took place on the palace grounds.
Oba Adeleye said: "Now that elections are around the corner and parties are preparing for their primaries, our youth should not make themselves willing tools in the hands of desperate politicians."
He said: "As the era of politicking draws closer, let us shun violence and be more calculating in our approach and decisions."
At the ceremony, Nigeria's Ambassador to the Central Africa Republic, Col. Roland Omowa (Rtd) was honoured with the Okarufe Golden Merit Award, the highest in the land.
Oba Adeleye called on the youth to imbibe positive values of hard work, honesty, transparency and generosity, which he described as the core values of their ancestors which succeeding generations have laboured hard to sustain.

Original_Search_Title: Hard Nuts to Crack
Headline: Nigeria: Hard Nuts to Crack
Body_Text:
Trading by the roadside exposes those engaged in the practice to danger and several attempts by the Lagos State Government to stop such practice have been rebuffed. Chiemelie Ezeobi writes that roadside markets continue to thrive in different parts of Lagos
A trip through Oshodi/Apapa Expressway will leave you confounded. Brisk transactions are everywhere but they are all done in dangerous places. Charity, Iyana Isolo, Ilasa, Cele, Ijesha and Mile 2 among others have become places where roadside markets thrive daily.
Often seen in clusters on the roadside, vendors have taken up the sidewalks on this axis leaving no place for the pedestrians. They have simply turned the roadside markets into a phenomenon.
From food items to clothings, household items, jewelleries, shoes, hats, bag, cosmetics, plastics, books and films amongst others, the market caters for everyone regardless of class.
In fact, one would be pardoned if the markets are referred to as a one-stop shopping centre as they basically cater for the immediate and basic needs of most Lagosians.
In the past, the Lagos State Government through its task force on environment had attempted stopping such practice but vendors always resume sales the next day or even hours after raids are carried out.
Perhaps the traders best understand the concept of 'space is money' as they buy up every available space every day, especially in the evenings for specified amount of money, depending on the area and the viability of the site. One of such markets can be found at Cele Bus-stop, along the ever busy Apapa-Oshodi Expressway. A visit by THISDAY to observe and absorb the bustle and hustle of the market revealed how chaotic the environment had become.
Asides the messy sight, the noise from the traders who try to outdone each other in marketing their wares was depressing. At this market it is not out of place to see a customer wailing after he/she might have been robbed blind by the pick pockets around.
On a certain Tuesday, when THISDAY visited the market, a young lady had fallen prey in the hands of unsavoury characters, who took advantage of the surging crowd to forcibly take the money she had on her.
She only realised her money was gone when she tried to pay for the second- hand clothes (popularly known as ok) she bought from one of the vendors popularly known on as 'Select and pay'. Such sights, the trader said have become a daily ritual for the first time visitors who are not very careful.
All across Lagos, space is money and so any available place, including walkways, street junctions, roads and footbridges are being converted to market space. THISDAY investigations revealed that for seven days in a week, the traders pay different fees to be allowed to sell in peace.
It is not uncommon to see some boys who are the foot soldiers of the Iyaloja's and councilmen go round. especially at Cele bus-stop collecting the mandatory N350.00 for Mondays to Thursdays, N550.00 for Fridays and 250.00 for Saturdays and Sundays However, many argued that although such markets have always been in existence, it exploded in and around Lagos State during the Babatunde Fashola's administration even though the major markets itself have witnessed lots of structural changes.
In other to get the traders to conform to the idea of shops, the government had imposed a ban on street selling and to some it was a welcome development while to others, it was purely vendetta as this was their means of livelihood especially when the government alternative is out of their reach financially.
The government had however considered such petty traders who cannot afford to pay for shops and allowed the "open ground market system" where people that can't afford shops can still sell in the market but on the grounds that they restrict themselves and activities within that marked area, but that grace has been abused repeatedly as traders often encroach the roads.
In order to stop the practice, the government had in times past built more markets and modern lock-up shops like the Tejuosho Market in Yaba, but for the traders, the exorbitant prices at which the government sell the shops are a turn off. Some of these shops go for as much as N2million yearly and some N6million.
The Effects The system whereby markets expand beyond their boundaries, spilling into streets and roads has its implications; traffic is obstructed, precious time is wasted and all suffer the inconvenience of moving at a snail speed.
At Apple Junction in Amuwo Odofin Local Government, Okomaiko, Ijesha, Mile 2 and a host of other places, the walkway meant for pedestrians have been taken over by traders who display all manner of goods, ranging from meat, pepper, fruits and recharge cards. In some cases, pedestrians are left with no other choice but to struggle with motorists and other road users.
But apart from obstructing traffic, lives are often at risk in these places. On the dangers associated with street trading, a trader said that there is no high and fast rule to danger as it can happen to anybody anywhere and anytime.
According to a trader at the Trade Fair Complex at Balogun, the efforts of the government to rid the streets off roadside traders is commendable. She was not saying this based on humanitarian grounds rather the fact that the roadside traders prevent their customers from entering the main market.
She said: "We are suffering in the hands of these roadside traders. They come to us, buy goods and then go by the roadside of the Trade Fair and sell to our potential customers. So, customers that should ordinarily patronise us, patronise them. "So the government's ban on street trading has increased customer influx for us as compared to before when roadside traders will not allow customers to get inside the market."
There are also many others who state that one of the side effects of such market are the sales of counterfeit products. It was alleged that some of these traders often take advantage of the informality of the market to offer either pirated, counterfeit, or bad products. A victim of such sales who for the cheap price of eggs sought to buy them there only to find out they were spoilt. She said, "Usually, an egg goes for N30 but they sell it for N25 sometimes N20 at the foot of Cele Bridge. To my shock, most of the eggs were already spoilt. Truly, all that glitters is not gold.
"That was not the first time I fell into the hands of such unscrupulous persons. The other time, it was smoked fish that I bought and I was so happy I got it for a cheaper rate not knowing that the inside of the fish was rotten." For the traders, another danger associated with such trading that often spills into the night is that they are often the victims of hoodlums who waylay them after sales and make-away with their hard earned money.
Lamentation of Traders Most of the traders lamented that the ban on street trading in Lagos was the reason they changed their business hours from morning to evening to avoid the long arm of the law while others blamed it on the high cost of renting a shop. While the traders respect the ban during the day, the night time tells another story as those who can afford a shop troop out with their wares alongside those who even have shops in the markets.
"I cannot afford to pay for a shop, because it is expensive for the kind of profit I make. That is why I come out in the evening, pay the required fee and sell my yams. I only part with a small fraction of what I make to pay for the space allocated to me." Known as Chukwuma Nwaaba, another trader who sells locally-made shoes at the Cele foot bridge said, "I stay at Barlett in the afternoon but come down to Cele in the night to sell because there are multitude of people going home in the night and we make better sales.
"Even though I have a shop, the proximity and reach to my target customers is one of the reason why I leave the confines of the market to the road to sell in the night.
"But the only problem we have is the Kick Against Indiscipline (KAI). They arrest us all the time. Even though we pay the Iyaloja, yet they don't protect us from arrest. On one of such raids, I had to part with N10, 000 to secure my release when I was arrested.
"And I paid that fee because my brother, a lance corporal came to my aid. So we don't usually come out in the morning or afternoon when they will be around but in the evening, we all troop out because by then they would have closed from work."
Another trader, Nwanyi Akpa, popular for selling bags at Cele said; "Here I am staying, I pay as much as N550 everyday before I can display my wares. The Iyaloja comes everyday to task us for our hard earned money. I don't have an option but to pay up if I want to make my daily bread."
A recharge card seller also at Cele said, "Despite the fees we pay daily, KAI men still harass us and just last week, I was arrested by KAI. They picked me up and bundled me into their car. I had to part with some money after having to spend a night in their office before they released me.
"As you can see, I don't use a table anymore to sell, rather, I walk up and down to sell my cards. I resorted to this method to be able to run away on time. The last time I was trying to dismantle my table when they caught up with me and I swore that next time they won't succeed so easily."
A corn seller at Cele, said she used to sell in the afternoon but since the ban on street trading, she has chosen to trade in the night. While agreeing with the wisdom behind the ban, she however appealed to the governor to let them trade in the evening as against the afternoon.
However, they were others who commended the government's effort at organising the markets. One of such persons said the gain is in the long run as the government policy is meant for good.
He said, "Although the directive is biting on some, they are getting used to it. Look at the people selling on the railway at the Yaba Market; are they not risking their lives? Immediately a train comes by, you see people scrambling to get their wares off the track and some sustain injuries in the process.
"So government's initiative to do away with street trading is partially commendable but they have to put considerate measures on ground to accommodate the petty traders."
At Oshodi, the traders beside the park said they do not sell in peace as they keep running to safety due to the constant raids. Corroborating, one of the traders who was simply identified as Iya Ibeji said despite her old age, she usually runs when such raids begin.
She said, "I do not sell in peace. Imagine an old woman like me running like a thief whenever I hear the sound of a siren or I see anybody in uniform. I cannot afford to pay for a shop that is why I am managing this small space. It's not as if it's for free, I am paying them for it yet nobody defends us when KAI people come."
THISDAY also interfaced with some customers who said the roadside markets are in their favour. According to them, with the proximity of such markets, it was much easier for them to go shopping as against going to the main market to trade.
Funmi Ogums is a believer in this school of thought. She said, "The positive side cannot be neglected. It's not everybody that might have the time to go the market and so these people have brought the market to us instead of us going to the market. They should be encouraged and not crucified."
A banker, Gloria Ezirim said, "I actually shop at the Cele roadside market because its on my way home from work. It is convenient for me. As a banker, I do not have the time of the day to shop so I simply breeze into such markets and but the needful."
Lagos Reinforces Ban Just recently, the State Task Force on Environmental and Special Offences in Ikeja, again warned traders displaying their wares on roadsides to desist from the act or face severe consequences.
This warning, one of the several issued in the past, was again repeated by the Chairman of the task force, Mr. Bayo Suleiman, who said trading by the roadside does not only constitute danger to the lives of the traders, but also impedes free flow of vehicular and pedestrian traffic.
According to him, the reason for the ban was because the roads were not designed for trading, adding that the state government would again begin clamp down on erring traders.
